translation: [All] praise is [due] to Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and made the darkness and the light. Then those who disbelieve equate [others] with their Lord
commentary:
ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ وَٱلنُّورَ ۖ ثُمَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ

Surah Al-An'am is one of the few surahs in the Quran that begins with الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ (Alhamdulillah). Remarkably, expressions of hamd in the Quran are evenly distributed throughout its structure. The pattern begins in the first surah with الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ in Al-Fatihah, then appears again approximately a quarter of the way through the Quran here in Al-An'am. Moving forward about seven sections further, we encounter it again in Surah Al-Kahf: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَنزَلَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ الْكِتَابَ. Then in Surah Saba: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ. Eventually, we find two surahs placed together, the 23rd and 24th, both beginning with this praise. Later still, around seven or eight sections later, we see two consecutive surahs again: both Surah Fatir and Surah Saba opening with الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ. This deliberate spacing shows how praise of Allah ﷻ is evenly spread throughout the Quran's architecture.

The ayah begins: "All praise and gratitude belongs to Allah, who created the skies and the earth." Notice the precise choice of verbs here. When Allah ﷻ mentions the creation of the heavens and earth, He uses خَلَقَ. But when He speaks of darkness and light, He uses جَعَلَ – "He installed, He placed, He made the shades of darkness and light."

Why this distinction? The verb خَلَقَ addresses tangible, physical creation. The heavens and earth are concrete, material realities. But جَعَلَ deals with the intangible, the figurative. Light and darkness are not physical objects you can hold – they are states, conditions, phenomena. You cannot touch darkness or grasp light in your hand. Therefore, Allah ﷻ uses ja'ala for their installation in creation.

Then comes a striking transition: ثُمَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ – "Then those who disbelieved in their Master, they equate." The word يَعْدِلُونَ comes from the root ع-د-ل, meaning to equate or make equals. This is another expression for committing shirk. The concept of عَدْل has a fascinating linguistic characteristic – it belongs to a category in Arabic called أضداد, words of opposites, which can carry contradictory meanings depending on context. When you equate things correctly and fairly, placing them on the same platform when they truly deserve to be compared, this is 'adl in its positive sense. But when you unfairly equate things that should never be placed on the same level, this too is called 'adl – in its negative manifestation.

The scholars have offered two interpretations here. Some say it means they equate their false partners with Allah ﷻ in worship, making them rivals to Him despite His absolute uniqueness. Others offer a different reading: Allah ﷻ made darkness and light distinct and separate, yet those who disbelieve look at them and say, "What's the difference? It's all the same." This represents a dangerous relativism – the attitude that proclaims all paths lead to the same destination, all religions teach the same thing, there's no fundamental difference between truth and falsehood. This dismissive equating of what Allah ﷻ has made distinct is precisely what the ayah condemns: الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ.

translation: It is He who created you from clay and then decreed a term and a specified time [known] to Him; then [still] you are in dispute
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن طِينٍ ثُمَّ قَضَىٰٓ أَجَلًا ۖ وَأَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى عِندَهُۥ ۖ ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تَمْتَرُونَ

"He is the One who created you from طِين" – wet soil, dirt that has moisture in it. The term طِين specifically refers to soil that has been exposed to water, and interestingly, even if that moisture eventually dries up, it continues to be called teen in Arabic. This connects back to the very origin of human creation, reminding us of our humble material beginning.

ثُمَّ قَضَىٰٓ أَجَلًا – "Then He declared a deadline." Here we encounter one of the Quran's many precise words for time. Arabic possesses an incredibly rich vocabulary for temporal concepts: وَقْت, حِين , أَجَل, زَمَن , دَهْر, عَصْر , أَمَد. Each carries distinct shades of meaning.

The word أَجَل specifically refers to the time between the present moment and an appointed deadline. If you schedule an appointment three days from now, those three days constitute the ajal – the interval stretching from now until that fixed point arrives. This term also appears in marriage terminology: when the mahr (dowry) will be given after a specified period, that waiting period is the ajal. In the nikah ceremony, you might hear the terms "ajalan" (deferred mahr) or "mu'ajjal" (immediate mahr, without ajal). So ajal captures this sense of a countdown, a clock ticking toward an inevitable terminus.

Allah ﷻ says He created us from soil, then declared an ajal for us. Scholars explain that this first deadline refers to our death – the span of time we walk upon this earth in these bodies of clay before we return to being clay again, before this soil that was temporarily separated from the earth is reunited with it.

Then comes a crucial addition: وَأَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى عِندَهُۥ – "And another deadline that is very well named, in His possession." We are told of two deadlines. The second one is described as مُّسَمًّى – repeatedly named, famous by name, carrying many titles. This characteristic points strongly toward the Day of Judgment, which indeed has numerous names throughout the Quran: يَوْمُ الْقِيَامَةِ (the Day of Rising), يَوْمُ الدِّينِ (the Day of Recompense), يَوْمُ الْحِسَابِ (the Day of Reckoning), الْقَارِعَةُ (the Striking Calamity), الْحَاقَّةُ (the Inevitable Reality), and many more.

Supporting this interpretation, we find that elsewhere in the Quran, when people ask "When is the Hour?", Allah ﷻ responds: إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّي – "Its knowledge is only with my Master" (7:187). The parallel is unmistakable: here too, Allah ﷻ says this second, well-known deadline is عِندَهُ (with Him) – in His exclusive possession. Its name is known, but its timing remains His secret.

ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تَمْتَرُونَ – "Then you fall into doubt." The Quran employs several words for doubt: مِرْيَة, رَيْب, شَكّ, and here, مَرْيَة from the verb تَمْتَرُونَ. This particular word carries a specific connotation. It comes from the root م-ر-ي, and you find related forms like يُمَارِي meaning to dispute or to be doubtful, and the command لَا تُمَارِ – "do not create doubt."

The distinct characteristic of مِرْيَة is that it refers to doubt about something that is actually undisputed fact. It's morning outside, but someone closes the curtains and argues that it's still nighttime, deliberately creating confusion about what is plain reality. This is إمتراء – manufacturing doubt about matters that are fundamentally known, arguing about established facts purely to sow confusion in people's minds.

Allah ﷻ applies this to the Day of Judgment. The Day of Judgment is something known, something certain. القيامة is established truth. It has been given many names – it is مُّسَمًّى. Later in the Quran, particularly in the final sections, we find that human nature itself testifies to the reality of accountability. Your own soul contains the evidence of the Day of Judgment. When someone asks for proof of Judgment Day, one of the Quranic responses points inward: the answer lies within your own being, in your innate sense of justice, your conscience, your awareness of right and wrong. This will be emphasized later in the surah and throughout the Quran's final passages.

translation: And He is Allah, [the only deity] in the heavens and the earth. He knows your secret and what you make public, and He knows that which you earn
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يَعْلَمُ سِرَّكُمْ وَجَهْرَكُمْ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْسِبُونَ

"And He, in fact, is Allah in the skies and in the earth." This declaration addresses a common distortion in people's conception of God. Many who maintain a loose belief in the Divine imagine a God concerned only with the grand machinery of the universe – spinning galaxies, cosmic phenomena, the macro-level operations of existence. In this flawed view, God is too busy with the "big things" to be bothered with individual human affairs, with personal problems, with the minute details of daily life.

Allah ﷻ corrects this misconception decisively: He is Allah in the heavens and in the earth. His authority, His knowledge, His concern encompass both the cosmic and the intimate.

يَعْلَمُ سِرَّكُمْ وَجَهْرَكُمْ – "He knows your secrets and what you make public." If you thought He was too occupied with managing the universe to notice you, understand this: He knows what you conceal in the depths of your heart and what you openly express. Nothing is hidden from Him, nothing is too small for His awareness.

وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْسِبُونَ – "And He knows what you earn." The verb كَسَبَ refers to what you earn through your own efforts, your actions, your choices. He is fully aware of everything you do, every consequence of your deeds, everything you accumulate through your behavior. The God who created the heavens is the same God intimately aware of every thought, every intention, every action in your life.

translation: And no sign comes to them from the signs of their Lord except that they turn away therefrom
commentary:
وَمَا تَأْتِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ

"And there does not come to them a single miraculous sign from among the signs of their Master except that they have been deliberately turning away from it."

The structure here is emphatic: وَمَا تَأْتِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ – "Not a single sign comes to them..." The repetition of مِّنْ creates emphasis in Arabic grammar, stressing the totality. No matter which sign, no matter how clear, no matter how powerful – the response is always the same.

إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ – "except that they turn away from it." The word مُعْرِضِينَ comes from إِعْرَاض, which means to turn away, but with a deeper implication. Consider the verb عَرَضَ, which means to present something, to display it, like when Allah ﷻ presented the names before the angels: عَرَضَهُمْ عَلَى الْمَلَائِكَةِ (2:31).

Now إِعْرَاض flips this concept. It's as if someone is about to present something to you, but you deliberately turn your face away, hiding from what should be shown. You have access to the truth, you know it's there, but you purposefully avert your gaze from it. This is more than passive ignorance – it's active, willful ignoring. They're not simply failing to see the signs; they're choosing not to look at them, deliberately suppressing what they know in their hearts should be acknowledged.

This is why the translation "they have been bent upon ignoring it" captures something of the intentionality. They've made a decision, taken a stance of rejection, and they maintain it consistently regardless of what clear evidence is presented to them.

translation: For they had denied the truth when it came to them, but there is going to reach them the news of what they used to ridicule
commentary:
فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِيهِمْ أَنۢبَـٰٓؤُا۟ مَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

"They have already lied against the truth when it came to them." The verdict is pronounced. The word فَقَدْ indicates something completed, established, done. The judgment has been passed. Their pattern of denial is so consistent, their rejection so entrenched, that their status is now determined.

This is one of the indications, along with various narrations, that led scholars to identify this as a late Makkan surah. Despite appearing as the sixth surah in the Quran's arrangement, its revelation came toward the latter part of the Makkan period, when the positions had hardened, when the lines had been clearly drawn between those who would accept and those who would persist in rejection.

فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِيهِمْ أَنۢبَـٰٓؤُا۟ مَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ – "Then soon the news will come to them – the fulfillment of what they have been mocking."

The word أَنۢبَـٰٓؤُا۟ is particularly powerful. While often translated as "news," anba' carries the sense of consequential information, significant reports, weighty announcements. And يَأْتِيهِمْ doesn't just mean "will come to them" – the verb أَتَىٰ can also mean to attack, to come upon someone forcefully. So the meaning intensifies: "Soon the very things they mock will attack them, will come upon them with force."

What makes this ayah remarkable is the context in which it was revealed. Consider the situation: the Muslims have no standing army, no political power, no material strength. All they have is a small band of companions enduring relentless persecution – beatings, torture, abuse, day after day. You might expect that in such circumstances, the rhetoric would soften, the tone would become more conciliatory, perhaps more cautious.

Instead, the Quran becomes even more direct, even more uncompromising. The threats intensify: "You're mocking? Soon you'll find out. The very thing you ridicule will overwhelm you."

This is profound. Allah ﷻ speaks with absolute authority – He is the Lord of the heavens and earth, He can say whatever He wills. But the disbelievers don't see Allah ﷻ speaking. They don't hear a voice from the sky. Who do they see? They see the Prophet ﷺ, who in their eyes is powerless. They see his companions (RA), whom they beat and persecute daily. Yet these apparently powerless people speak with the authority of the Lord of the worlds.

This infuriates them further. How dare you talk like this? We're the ones with power, with wealth, with armies. We'll increase your suffering! Yet the Quranic message doesn't waver, doesn't compromise, doesn't adjust its tone based on the political climate or the apparent weakness of the Muslims' position.

This teaches us something essential about iman: we don't modify what we believe based on who's listening or what the circumstances are. Truth remains truth regardless of the political weather, regardless of whether we're in a position of strength or weakness. Allah ﷻ issues His warnings and promises as He wills because He is the ultimate Authority. He doesn't need to calculate the consequences or worry about the backlash. The Quran speaks from a position of absolute truth, and that never changes.

translation: Have they not seen how many generations We destroyed before them which We had established upon the earth as We have not established you? And We sent [rain from] the sky upon them in showers and made rivers flow beneath them; then We destroyed them for their sins and brought forth after them a generation of others
commentary:
أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ مَّكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَا لَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّكُمْ وَأَرْسَلْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْهِم مِّدْرَارًا وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمْ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَأَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا ءَاخَرِينَ

"Haven't they seen?" Pay attention to how frequently this question appears throughout this surah and the Quran generally: أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ – "Don't they see?" أَلَمْ تَرَ – "Haven't you seen?" This repeated appeal to observation, to looking at the evidence, to examining what's plainly visible before you. Mark these occurrences as you read.

كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ – "How many generations before them We destroyed." But Allah ﷻ doesn't just mention their destruction. He first describes what they possessed, what advantages they enjoyed:

مَّكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَا لَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّكُمْ – "We had established them in the land with power and resources that We have not given to you." This is directed at the Quraysh, and it's a subtle but stinging comparison. You think you're powerful? You think your position in Makkah, your control of the trade routes, your wealth makes you invincible? Previous nations had far more than you ever will.

وَأَرْسَلْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْهِم مِّدْرَارًا – "And We sent the sky upon them, continuously pouring." The word مِدْرَار is beautiful and highly evocative. It comes from the root د-ر-ر, and in Arabic, a she-camel that produces milk abundantly and continuously, never seeming to run dry, is described as دَرُور. Actually, one of the words for milk itself in Arabic is دِرَّة, from this same root. When someone's eyes have a medical condition causing continuous tearing, unable to stop, Arabs say أَدَرَّتْ عَيْنُهُ – "his eye is pouring."

So when Allah ﷻ says He sent the sky upon them midraran, He means the rain came and kept coming, an unceasing downpour of blessing. But understand, this isn't describing a destructive flood – this is actually describing favor, continuous blessing. The sky here represents all the benefits heaven provides: rain for crops, water for drinking, moisture for the land.

Think about what continuous, reliable rainfall means: flourishing agriculture, abundant harvests, economic prosperity. The land is always fertile, the lakes and wells always full. Trade flourishes because there's surplus to export. Other cities want to establish commercial relationships because of the abundance. Water itself becomes a valuable commodity. Cities positioned near water sources – whether rivers, lakes or oceans – hold strategic economic value. Port cities control trade routes. This is the reality Allah ﷻ is describing: these previous nations had natural resources that guaranteed their prosperity.

And there's an implicit jab here at the Quraysh: "You don't get much rain in Makkah, do you?"

وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمْ – "And We made rivers flow beneath them." Not just rain from above, but rivers flowing below – constant, reliable water sources. Some scholars explain this as rivers literally flowing beneath their dwellings and gardens, while others point to rivers visible from elevated positions, flowing through the valleys below their cities. Either way, they had abundant, permanent water supplies. Imagine the agricultural wealth, the economic stability this provided.

The Quraysh, by contrast, lived in a desert valley with minimal water sources. "What do you have?" Allah ﷻ seems to ask them. The comparison is devastating.

These previous nations had every material advantage: political power, economic resources, agricultural abundance, strategic positioning, continuous rainfall, flowing rivers. They had security and prosperity that the Quraysh could only dream of. They were far better equipped to withstand any calamity, far more capable of defending themselves, far more established in every measurable way.

فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ – "Then We destroyed them because of their sins."

Despite all their power, all their resources, all their advantages – when the decree came, none of it helped them. Not their wealth, not their armies, not their strategic positions, not their economic might. Their sins brought them down, and all their worldly strength couldn't save them.

وَأَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا ءَاخَرِينَ – "And We raised after them another generation."

The verb أَنشَأَ is significant. It means to raise something up and allow it to grow, like caring for a weak plant – watering it, ensuring it gets proper sunlight, nurturing it until it becomes strong. Allah ﷻ says that after those mighty nations fell, He took other nations that were initially weak and raised them to power, allowed them to grow into strength.

This happened with the nations of Prophets like Nuh, Salih, Shu'ayb (peace be upon them all). Who could have imagined that their small groups of believers would eventually become established? And think about the early Muslims hearing this. The Quraysh looked at 20, 30, perhaps 50 companions and thought they'd crush them within days. Could anyone have imagined that within 40 years, these persecuted few would control vast portions of the earth? That continents would be under the authority of this faith?

It was beyond comprehension at the time. Yet this is precisely what the ayah promises: We raise the weak, We elevate those who seem to have no chance, We give power to those who started with nothing but truth. The same pattern that destroyed the arrogant and elevated the humble in previous generations would repeat itself.

This is the historical lesson, the warning and the promise, all contained in one powerful ayah.

translation: And even if We had sent down to you, [O Muhammad], a written scripture on a page and they touched it with their hands, the disbelievers would say, "This is not but obvious magic
commentary:
وَلَوْ نَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ كِتَـٰبًا فِى قِرْطَاسٍ فَلَمَسُوهُ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ لَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ

This ayah addresses one of the central themes of Surah Al-An'am. Throughout the late Makkan period, the Quraysh developed what became their signature criticism of the message: "Until we see it, we won't believe." They insisted that if the Prophet ﷺ was truly a messenger, he should show them something physical, not just words. The Quran is just words, they argued. They demanded to see the book physically descending, to witness angels, to experience something tangible. Their stance was clear: once we see proof, we're ready to believe, but until then, don't waste our time with your message.

Allah ﷻ responds to this demand with a powerful hypothetical: "Had We sent down to you a book on قِرْطَاس..." The word qirtas refers to a special type of paper, the kind used in Egyptian hieroglyphics - a thicker, crusty paper that came in scrolls. This wasn't the typical writing material known to the Arabs. It was rare, exotic, something they recognized as coming from elsewhere. By using this word, Allah ﷻ paints a picture of alien, otherworldly paper, emphasizing that this would be clearly supernatural in origin.

The ayah continues: "...so they could touch it with their hands." The phrase فَلَمَسُوهُ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ is particularly significant. The root لمس means to touch with your hands specifically - not just any kind of contact, but the deliberate examination that comes from handling something with your hands. Allah ﷻ emphasizes this by mentioning "hands" twice in the construction. This isn't casual contact; it's thorough investigation, testing it out, fiddling with it, turning it over to examine every aspect.

Even after all this - after receiving a physical book on exotic paper that they could hold, examine and verify with their own hands - Allah ﷻ reveals what would happen: لَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ - "Those who are bent upon disbelief would still say: 'This is nothing but obvious magic.'" They would conclude it's clearly sorcery, that this only proves he's a magician.

This reveals something profound: Allah ﷻ is telling His Messenger ﷺ that no matter what sign is sent, their minds are already made up. He knows what's truly in their hearts. What they're asking for wouldn't help their cause - it would actually reinforce their disbelief. The ayah teaches us that the problem isn't a lack of evidence; it's the condition of their hearts.

This connects to recent revelation in the surah. When was the last time a physical miracle was sent upon someone's request? The people of Salih (AS) and the she-camel. What was the divine warning that accompanied it? Once the miracle comes, if you disbelieve, immediate punishment follows. The disbelievers of Makkah don't even realize what they're asking for. If they received their physical miracle and then rejected it, we already know from earlier passages what would happen. Allah ﷻ is doing them a tremendous favor by not obliging their request.

translation: And they say, "Why was there not sent down to him an angel?" But if We had sent down an angel, the matter would have been decided; then they would not be reprieved
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ ۖ وَلَوْ أَنزَلْنَا مَلَكًا لَّقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ثُمَّ لَا يُنظَرُونَ

The criticism continues: "And they say: 'Why has an angel not been sent down to him?'" This was their next demand - if you're truly a messenger, show us an angel.

Allah ﷻ responds with stark clarity: وَلَوْ أَنزَلْنَا مَلَكًا لَّقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ - "Had We sent down an angel, the matter would have been judged." The word قُضِيَ means the matter would be decided, finished, executed. In other words, if they saw an angel in its true form, their time would be over. This is because when angels are sent in their actual form to disbelieving nations, they come as executioners, not as entertainment. They don't come to impress you or satisfy your curiosity.

The ayah continues: ثُمَّ لَا يُنظَرُونَ - "Then they would not be given respite." Once the angel appears and you've witnessed it, there's no grace period. No "Okay, we've seen the angel. Just give us a couple of minutes to think about it." Time would be up immediately. The mercy they're currently experiencing - the space to reflect, reconsider and potentially accept guidance - would vanish the moment their demand was fulfilled.

translation: And if We had made him an angel, We would have made him [appear as] a man, and We would have covered them with that in which they cover themselves
commentary:
وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ مَلَكًا لَّجَعَلْنَـٰهُ رَجُلًا وَلَلَبَسْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّا يَلْبِسُونَ

This ayah addresses an implied criticism. The disbelievers were questioning: "You're just a man. Are you claiming to be an angel? Where are your wings?" This is a form of لَبْس - creating confusion through baseless criticism. The Prophet ﷺ never claimed to be an angel, yet they mocked him as if he had, leaving him in a position where there's almost no way to respond to such absurd accusations.

Allah ﷻ answers: "Had We made him an angel, We would have made him appear as a man." Even if the messenger had been an angel, he would have needed to take human form to interact with humanity. Why? Because angels in their true form bring death and judgment. For an angelic messenger to deliver a message that people could actually hear and process, he would need to appear human.

The ayah concludes: وَلَلَبَسْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّا يَلْبِسُونَ - "And We would have caused them the very confusion they're currently experiencing." The word لَبَسْنَا comes from the same root as لَبْس, which means to confuse or obscure something. The same confusion they have now - "Is this man truly a messenger?" - would exist even if an angel came in human form. They would say, "That's just a man. How do we know he's really an angel?" The confusion they're using as an excuse would persist regardless.

translation: And already were messengers ridiculed before you, but those who mocked them were enveloped by that which they used to ridicule
commentary:
وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَحَاقَ بِٱلَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

This ayah provides consolation to the Prophet ﷺ while warning his opponents. The word اسْتُهْزِئَ describes a particular type of mockery - not just laughing at something, but ridiculing it because you consider it stupid and beneath you. It's the kind of insulting dismissal that involves a smirk, a scoff: "Yeah, right. Word of God? An angel? Sure." This is particularly painful for someone engaged in calling people to truth, to have their sincere message met with contemptuous laughter and dismissal.

Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ﷺ: "Messengers before you were mocked in exactly this way." Don't be overwhelmed by this treatment. Between the lines, we can understand that the Messenger ﷺ might have genuinely thought that if he could just show them something physical, they would believe. After all, his concern wasn't personal glory but bringing people to iman. He might have sincerely believed that meeting their demands could lead to their guidance.

But Allah ﷻ clarifies: this mockery isn't serious inquiry. It's a game, a deflection. Even previous messengers faced this, and it never ended with belief upon seeing miracles.

The ayah then shifts to warning: فَحَاقَ بِٱلَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ - "The very thing they mocked came to surround those who ridiculed it." The word سَخِرُوا takes the mockery further - not just looking down on something, but actively going out of your way to humiliate it. Like laughing someone off a stage or pushing them out of a gathering while everyone joins in the ridicule.

The word حَاقَ is particularly vivid - it describes something falling from the sky and trapping you like a net, or a boulder that falls and pins you down, leaving you encircled with no escape. What they used to mock - divine punishment - came and trapped them from above. This alludes to angels descending with punishment, surrounding the mockers completely. Then they discovered how funny it really was.

translation: Say, "Travel through the land; then observe how was the end of the deniers
commentary:
قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ

"Tell them: Travel through the land." The word سِيرُوا means to take a stroll, to go on journeys, to explore the earth through road trips and excursions. This isn't a command to aimlessly wander, but to actively investigate.

"Then look" - ثُمَّ ٱنظُرُوا۟ - take a careful, considered look. Don't just pass by; actually observe and reflect. كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ - "What was the outcome of those who denied the truth?" The word مُكَذِّبِينَ refers specifically to those who called the truth a lie, who actively rejected and denied it.

The evidence is all around them. The ruins of previous nations, the stories passed down, the archaeological remains - all of it testifies to the reality of divine punishment. Allah ﷻ is telling them: you want proof? Go look at history. The proof is scattered across the earth in the destroyed civilizations of those who mocked their messengers.

translation: Say, "To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and earth?" Say, "To Allah." He has decreed upon Himself mercy. He will surely assemble you for the Day of Resurrection, about which there is no doubt. Those who will lose themselves [that Day] do not believe
commentary:
قُل لِّمَن مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ قُل لِّلَّهِ ۚ كَتَبَ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةَ ۚ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

"Say: To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?" This question is remarkable when compared to similar questions elsewhere in the Quran. In Surah Luqman, which was revealed around the same late Makkan period, Allah ﷻ asks: "If you were to ask them who created the heavens and earth, they would certainly say 'Allah.'" They had no problem acknowledging Allah ﷻ as Creator.

But here, the question is different: "Who owns everything in the heavens and earth?" Interestingly, Allah ﷻ doesn't say "they will answer 'Allah.'" Instead, He says: قُل لِّلَّهِ - "Say: To Allah."

Why would they refuse to answer this particular question when they readily acknowledged Allah ﷻ as Creator? Because acknowledging Allah ﷻ as Creator doesn't necessarily obligate you to Him. You can accept that a manufacturer created something, but once you purchase it, you owe the manufacturer nothing. The relationship is finished. Similarly, they could acknowledge Allah ﷻ created everything while claiming no ongoing obligation to Him.

But ownership is different. If Allah ﷻ owns everything in the heavens and earth, that means He owns me too. And if He owns me, then I owe Him something. I have obligations. I'm accountable. They couldn't say this because they understood its implications. So Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ: You tell them. You say "To Allah."

The ayah continues: كَتَبَ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةَ - "He has prescribed mercy upon Himself." Allah ﷻ has mandated, made obligatory upon Himself, the attribute of mercy. This is significant because it means the disbelievers are currently experiencing divine mercy - they're not being punished despite deserving it. Every moment they're given is an act of mercy, another opportunity to turn back.

لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - "He will surely gather you until the Day of Resurrection." The oath form (the lam of emphasis with the doubled nun) emphasizes absolute certainty. Notice the unique construction here: إِلَىٰ typically indicates spatial direction (going to a place), while حَتَّىٰ indicates temporal duration (until a time). Here Allah ﷻ uses ila with Day of Judgment, treating time as space, as if the Day of Judgment is a destination toward which you're traveling.

This creates a powerful image: every second that passes is like a step taken on a journey toward the Day of Resurrection. Even after death, even in the grave, that journey continues. You're constantly moving closer and closer to that Day of standing before Allah ﷻ.

لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ - "There is absolutely no doubt about it" - about that Day, about being gathered, about facing judgment.

The ayah concludes with a profound statement: ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ - "Those who have lost themselves - they are the ones who do not believe." The word خَسِرُوا means to bankrupt, to put oneself in total loss. Allah ﷻ gave every human being an incredible treasure - the fitrah, the natural inclination toward recognizing and worshiping the Creator. This comes from the name of Allah, Al-Fatir, which doesn't just mean Creator, but the One who creates, then carves, fine-tunes, perfects, beautifies and produces the finished product. Anyone can create something crude, but fatara means to perfect it.

Every human being was given this perfected, fine-tuned fitrah inside them. Those who disbelieve have bankrupted that treasure. They've squandered the very gift that was meant to lead them to faith. Of course they're not going to believe - they've thrown away the tool that enables belief.

translation: And to Him belongs that which reposes by night and by day, and He is the Hearing, the Knowing
commentary:
۞ وَلَهُۥ مَا سَكَنَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

"And to Him alone belongs whatever is stationed in the night and the day." The word سَكَنَ means to be stationary, to remain still and silent. The ayah says "whatever is stationed in the night" and then says "and the day." The construction is deliberately asymmetrical - it mentions what's stationary in the night but doesn't explicitly mention what's stationary in the day.

This is a profound rhetorical device. The implication is that what moves in the day is also His. By stating one side and leaving the other implied, Allah ﷻ is engaging the human capacity to fill in the blanks. We naturally understand: if He owns what's still in the night, He owns what moves in the day; if He owns what's still in the day, He owns what moves in the night. Everything, in every state, belongs to Him.

This rhetorical technique serves a larger purpose: if you have the intellectual capacity to fill in these obvious blanks, why can't you fill in the most obvious blank of all - that there is a Creator, that there's something more to existence than randomness?

وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ - "And He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing." He hears everything - every prayer, every mockery, every whisper. He knows everything - every intention, every excuse, every secret thought.

translation: Say, "Is it other than Allah I should take as a protector, Creator of the heavens and the earth, while it is He who feeds and is not fed?" Say, [O Muhammad], "Indeed, I have been commanded to be the first [among you] who submit [to Allah] and [was commanded], 'Do not ever be of the polytheists
commentary:
قُلْ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَلَا يُطْعَمُ ۗ قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ ۖ وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to declare: "Should I take anyone other than Allah as a protector and ally?" The word وَلِيّ encompasses multiple meanings - protector, guardian, close friend, ally, the one you turn to in need.

Then comes one of the names of Allah ﷻ that directly connects to the previous discussion: فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ - "The Fatir of the heavens and the earth." As explained earlier, fatir means the One who creates with perfection, who shapes and fine-tunes until the creation is complete and beautiful. This isn't crude creation; this is artistry and perfection.

وَهُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَلَا يُطْعَمُ - "And He provides food, and He is not provided for." This is a fundamental distinction between Creator and creation. Everything in existence requires sustenance from outside itself. We need food, water, air, energy. But Allah ﷻ is completely self-sufficient. He provides for all, needs nothing from anyone. This absolute independence is central to understanding divine nature versus created nature.

قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ - "Say: Indeed I have been commanded to be the first of those who submit." The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to emphasize, in first person, that he has received a direct command. He's using quotation: "I've been told - I should be the first to submit to Allah."

Then comes the warning, also in quotation form: وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ - "And don't you dare be among those who commit shirk." The use of the emphatic form shows this is a serious warning. The Prophet ﷺ himself has been commanded to avoid shirk and warned against it.

Why is this significant? This establishes a crucial principle of prophethood: the messenger himself is a slave, obligated, accountable. He's not above the law or beyond judgment. He's commanded to submit and warned against disobedience. This humility is itself proof of authentic messengership. A false prophet wouldn't present himself as vulnerable to divine displeasure.

translation: Say, "Indeed I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous Day
commentary:
قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّى عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ

This ayah continues the theme of prophetic humility and accountability. The Prophet ﷺ is told to declare: "Say: I myself fear, if I were to disobey my Master, the punishment of a tremendous Day."

The emphasis on "I myself" (إِنِّىٓ) is powerful. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ, the one through whom revelation comes, the one who has seen Jibreel (AS), who has experienced the miracle of revelation - even he declares his fear of divine punishment.

What would disobedience mean for the Prophet ﷺ? In his context, it would mean failing to deliver the message, giving up on calling people to truth, abandoning the mission of dawah despite opposition and mockery. He's saying: I'm afraid of the consequences if I don't continue this work, if I don't keep sharing this message with you, regardless of how you respond.

This is one of the fundamental pillars of understanding Risalah (messengership). First, we learned that miracles are at Allah's discretion - the Quran is sufficient, and the Messenger ﷺ will not produce signs on demand. Second, here we see that the Messenger himself is completely obligated, entirely accountable, fully aware of his servitude to Allah ﷻ.

What makes this even more powerful is that Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ to describe himself in a state of fear and vulnerability. A false prophet would never portray himself this way. The very fact that the Messenger ﷺ is commanded to present himself as afraid, as accountable, as potentially subject to punishment - this itself becomes evidence of the message's authenticity.

translation: He from whom it is averted that Day - [Allah] has granted him mercy. And that is the clear attainment
commentary:
مَّن يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُۥ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

"Whoever is averted from punishment on that Day - He has certainly shown him mercy." The word يُصْرَفْ means to be turned away, diverted from something you were heading toward. Picture someone walking toward destruction, and they're redirected, turned away from that path.

The ayah emphasizes: this turning away is itself mercy. Simply being spared from punishment, being diverted from the torment that was deserved - this is divine mercy in action.

وَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ - "And that is the clear triumph, the manifest success." Not worldly success, not temporary victory, not the accumulation of wealth or status. The ultimate success, the only success that truly matters, is being turned away from divine punishment on the Day of Judgment. Everything else pales in comparison.

translation: And if Allah should touch you with adversity, there is no remover of it except Him. And if He touches you with good - then He is over all things competent
commentary:
وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ ٱللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلَا كَاشِفَ لَهُۥٓ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

This ayah brings the passage to a powerful conclusion by addressing direct, personal experience of divine power. "And if Allah should touch you with harm..." The word يَمْسَسْكَ means to barely touch, the lightest contact. Even if Allah ﷻ just barely touches you with any kind of difficulty, harm or trial...

فَلَا كَاشِفَ لَهُۥٓ إِلَّا هُوَ - "...there is no remover of it except Him." The word كَاشِفَ means one who removes, uncovers, alleviates, cures. No one - absolutely no one - can alleviate that harm, remove that difficulty, or provide relief except Allah ﷻ Himself. No matter how many people you turn to, how much wealth you possess, how much influence you have - if Allah ﷻ has touched you with harm, only He can remove it.

وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ - "And if He should touch you with good, then He has power over all things." Again, the word is "touch" - if He barely touches you with good, with blessing, with fortune. The statement that follows is open-ended: He has complete power over all things. That small good can become immense. That little blessing can transform into something beyond your imagination. That tiny seed of opportunity can grow into enormous benefit - all because He has absolute power over everything.

Some scholars understand this ayah as general guidance for all believers: recognize that harm and good both come from Allah ﷻ alone. Look at yourself, examine your life. When difficulty comes, nobody will remove it except Him. When good comes, recognize His complete power to expand it beyond expectation.

Other scholars see this as specific consolation to the Prophet ﷺ during his time of great difficulty in Makkah. The message would be: If Allah ﷻ places you in some trial - the mockery, the rejection, the opposition you're facing - nobody will relieve you from it except Him. Trust in Him alone. And if Allah ﷻ gives you even a little good, even a small opening or minor success, remember that He has power over all things. That small good can become the foundation of something tremendous, something you cannot currently imagine.

Both understandings carry the same essential truth: absolute reliance on Allah ﷻ, recognition of His exclusive power over benefit and harm, and trust that His wisdom governs everything that touches our lives.

translation: And He is the subjugator over His servants. And He is the Wise, the Acquainted [with all]
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ

"And He is the Subjugator over His servants, and He is the Wise, the Aware."

This ayah highlights Allah's ﷻ absolute dominance over His creation through a grammatically significant construction. In Arabic, when you see a nominal sentence where the predicate begins with الـ (the definite article), it creates an exclusive emphasis. The structure وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ doesn't simply state "He is dominant" - it declares "He is THE dominant one," implying exclusivity. No one else holds this position. He alone is القاهر, the One who cannot be denied, avoided, or escaped.

The word قاهر comes from the root ق-ه-ر, which conveys overwhelming dominance. This is the hyperbolic form, intensifying the meaning: He is the Utterly Dominant, the Irresistibly Overpowering. You might try to avoid the messenger, you might run from the message, but you cannot escape the One who masters over you completely.

فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ - "above His slaves" - emphasizes His absolute authority over those He has created. We are His servants, and He exercises complete sovereignty over us.

The ayah closes with two of Allah's ﷻ beautiful names: الْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ - the All-Wise, the All-Aware. These aren't just decorative additions. When Allah ﷻ describes His dominance, He immediately reminds us that His power is coupled with perfect wisdom and complete knowledge. He doesn't rule arbitrarily or ignorantly. Every command, every decision, every decree flows from infinite wisdom and comprehensive awareness of all things.

These ayahs describing Allah's ﷻ attributes and His relationship with His servants appear frequently throughout the Quran, and they're often overlooked in our study. We rush past them seeking stories or legal rulings, forgetting that understanding who Allah ﷻ is transforms how we live. When you truly grasp that He is القاهر - the Inescapable, the Undeniable - while simultaneously being الْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ - the perfectly Wise and completely Aware - it changes everything about your relationship with Him.

translation: Say, "What thing is greatest in testimony?" Say, "Allah is witness between me and you. And this Qur'an was revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it reaches. Do you [truly] testify that with Allah there are other deities?" Say, "I will not testify [with you]." Say, "Indeed, He is but one God, and indeed, I am free of what you associate [with Him]
commentary:
قُلْ أَىُّ شَىْءٍ أَكْبَرُ شَهَـٰدَةً ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ شَهِيدٌۢ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۚ وَأُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ لِأُنذِرَكُم بِهِۦ وَمَنۢ بَلَغَ ۚ أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً أُخْرَىٰ ۚ قُل لَّآ أَشْهَدُ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ وَإِنَّنِى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ

"Say, 'What thing is greatest in testimony?' Say, 'Allah is witness between me and you. And this Quran was revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it reaches.' Do you truly testify that there are other gods with Allah? Say, 'I will not testify.' Say, 'He is only one God, and I am truly innocent of what you associate.'"

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ to challenge the disbelievers directly: "What testimony could possibly be greater?" The answer comes immediately: "Allah ﷻ Himself is witness between me and you."

When the Quraysh demanded signs and proofs, the Prophet ﷺ was instructed to present the ultimate testimony - Allah's ﷻ own witness to his prophethood. This witness manifests through the revelation itself: وَأُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ - "and this Quran has been revealed to me."

The purpose is clearly defined: لِأُنذِرَكُم بِهِۦ - "so that I may warn you by means of it." Notice the preposition بِهِۦ- "by means of it." This phrase repeats throughout Surah Al-An'am, emphasizing a crucial point: the Quran itself is the warning. The Prophet ﷺ doesn't need lightning from the sky, earthquakes, or angelic visitations. Warning through the Quran is his primary mission.

Then comes the profound phrase: وَمَنۢ بَلَغَ - "and whomever it reaches." These two small words contain an immense reality. The Prophet's ﷺ mission extends beyond his immediate audience. Every person the Quran reaches - across time and geography - falls under the scope of his warning.

This means something remarkable for us today. When we study and teach the Quran, we're continuing the Prophet's ﷺ actual mission. He was sent to warn his people and whoever the message would eventually reach. That "whoever" includes us, over fourteen centuries later. And when we convey this message forward, we're participating in the very task he attributed to himself in this ayah.

The phrase وَمَنۢ بَلَغَ also contains a note of hope from the Prophet ﷺ himself. He doesn't know who it will reach or where it will end up. Allah ﷻ had already told him how insignificant the Quraysh were in the grand scheme - there were nations before them, far greater, with abundant resources and power. The Quraysh had very little. Yet this revelation would reach far beyond them.

And look at how it unfolded. Four-fifths of the Muslim Ummah today isn't even Arab. The message that started with a small population in Makkah spread to the entire world. Those early believers, vastly outnumbered, got the message to millions. Now we're over a billion strong, yet somehow we haven't conveyed it as effectively. The proportion has reversed - they were one to millions and succeeded; we're one to five and struggle. This should humble us.

The ayah then confronts their shirk directly: أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً أُخْرَىٰ - "Do you really testify that alongside Allah there are other gods?" The tone is incredulous - are you actually standing by this claim?

The response is emphatic: قُل لَّآ أَشْهَدُ - "Say, I do not testify to that." The Prophet ﷺ completely dissociates from their testimony.

Then the declaration of pure monotheism: قُلْ إِنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ- "Say, He is only one God, a single deity worthy of worship and obedience."

And finally: وَإِنَّنِى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ - "and I am completely innocent of what you associate with Him." The word بَرِىٓءٌ means absolved, dissociated, having nothing to do with. The Prophet ﷺ declares total separation from their practices of shirk.

This declaration echoes a pattern we'll see later in this very surah. The Quran is full of stories, and except for the story of Yusuf (AS) which appears complete in one place, most prophetic narratives appear in fragments throughout different surahs. They're not arranged chronologically but by relevance to each surah's theme.

When we reach the story of Ibrahim (AS) later in Al-An'am, we'll see a parallel moment where he too declares his innocence from the shirk of his people. That particular episode from Ibrahim's (AS) life is highlighted here because it reinforces the central theme of this surah. The Quran doesn't aim to give us complete historical accounts with dates, locations, and exhaustive details. It extracts from each story precisely the lesson that serves the surah's purpose. Understanding this principle prevents the misguided criticism that "the Quran doesn't tell complete stories." It's not trying to. It's teaching lessons through the most relevant portions of those accounts.

translation: Those to whom We have given the Scripture recognize it as they recognize their [own] sons. Those who will lose themselves [in the Hereafter] do not believe
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَعْرِفُونَهُۥ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَآءَهُمُ ۘ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

"Those to whom We gave the Scripture recognize it as they recognize their own sons. Those who have lost themselves - they do not believe."

The conversation suddenly shifts. Until now, Allah ﷻ was addressing the Quraysh of Makkah. Now He speaks about أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ - the People of the Book, specifically the Jews and Christians.

Why this shift? During the later Makkan period, interaction was already happening between the Quraysh and the Jewish communities, particularly those in Madinah. The disbelievers of Makkah were running out of arguments. Every criticism they launched against the Prophet ﷺ and the Quran had been systematically dismantled and refuted. They needed fresh ammunition.

So they began consulting the Jews, who would feed them new questions and objections: "Why don't you ask him this? Challenge him with that." Allah ﷻ exposes this behind-the-scenes collaboration and addresses the People of the Book directly.

The comparison is striking: يَعْرِفُونَهُۥ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَآءَهُمُ - "they recognize him as they recognize their own sons." The pronoun هُ - "him" - refers to both the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and the Quran itself. They recognize both with absolute certainty.

Think about what this comparison means. How well do you recognize your own child? You'd know them in any crowd, in any circumstance, even if years passed. That's the level of recognition the Jews and Christians had regarding Muhammad ﷺ and his message. Their scriptures contained descriptions of the final prophet. They knew the signs, they knew the prophecies, they knew what to look for. When he appeared, they recognized him with the same certainty a parent has identifying their child.

Yet despite this undeniable recognition, ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ - "those who have caused loss to themselves." The word خَسِرُوا means to lose something of value, to suffer bankruptcy. They didn't just harm themselves - they caused themselves to lose their very souls, to bankrupt their own spiritual reality.

فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ - "so they do not believe." Despite knowing the truth with absolute clarity, recognition alone doesn't guarantee faith. Knowledge without submission is worthless. They saw but refused to believe because belief requires action, requires change, requires abandoning their positions of religious authority and social status.

translation: And who is more unjust than one who invents about Allah a lie or denies His verses? Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed
commentary:
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

"And who is more unjust than one who invents a lie about Allah or denies His signs? Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed."

This ayah follows a rhetorical pattern that appears multiple times in the Quran: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ - "and who is more unjust?" or "who could be a worse wrongdoer?" Whenever Allah ﷻ uses this construction, pay attention. He's highlighting one of the gravest injustices a person can commit.

It's worth collecting these verses throughout the Quran. One of the most severe appears in Surah Al-Kahf: "And who is more unjust than one who is reminded of the signs of his Lord but turns away from them and forgets what his hands have put forth?" (18:57) That's terrifying - to be reminded of Allah's ﷻ ayahs and deliberately turn away, to forget your own deeds that will confront you on the Day of Judgment.

Here, Allah ﷻ identifies two categories of extreme wrongdoing:

First: مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا - "one who invents a lie about Allah." The word ٱفْتَرَىٰ means to fabricate something from nothing, to make up something false without even realizing it. It suggests a lie so baseless it's almost accidental - except it's deliberate. To attribute to Allah ﷻ what He never said, to claim divine authority for your own opinions, to invent religious laws and call them Allah's ﷻ commands - this is spiritual fraud of the highest order.

Second: أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ - "or denies His signs." This person doesn't fabricate lies about Allah ﷻ, but deliberately rejects His clear revelations and miraculous signs. They see the truth and consciously turn away from it.

Both crimes share something fundamental: they're acts of injustice against Allah ﷻ Himself, attributing to Him what isn't true or rejecting what He has made manifest.

The conclusion is absolute: إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ - "Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed." The إِنَّ at the beginning emphasizes certainty. Sometimes إِنَّهُۥ - "indeed it" - serves as a placeholder to introduce emphatic statements. The translation captures it as "the fact of the matter is" or "without doubt." 

There's no success, no prosperity, no ultimate achievement for those who commit these injustices. يُفْلِحُ means to succeed, to prosper, to achieve your goals. The wrongdoers - ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ - will fail. Not might fail. Will fail. It's a certainty.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], the Day We will gather them all together; then We will say to those who associated others with Allah, "Where are your 'partners' that you used to claim [with Him]
commentary:
وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآؤُكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ

"And the Day We will gather them all together - then We will say to those who associated others with Allah, 'Where are your partners whom you used to claim?'"

Allah ﷻ now paints a scene from the Day of Judgment. وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا (wa yawma nahshuruhum jami'an) - "And the Day We will gather them all together."

The word نَحْشُرُ deserves attention. In Arabic, there's a difference between جَمْع (jam') and حَشْر. Both involve gathering, but جَمْع implies people coming together voluntarily, while حَشْر means forced gathering - herding people against their will, like shepherds driving sheep.

On that Day, humanity will experience both. There will be a moment when people rush toward their groups willingly - running to line up behind their false gods, their false prophets, whoever they followed in this world. That's جَمْع. But the initial resurrection, when people are pulled from their graves and driven forward toward the gathering place - that's حَشْر. They're being herded like reluctant animals, moved along whether they want to go or not.

Then comes the devastating question: ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآؤُكُمُ - "Then We will say to those who associated partners with Allah: Where are your partners?"

Notice it's not just "where are the false gods you worshipped?" but specifically شُرَكَآؤُكُمُ - "YOUR partners," the ones you claimed shared in divinity, the ones you elevated to partnership with Allah ﷻ.

ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ - "those you used to claim." The word تَزْعُمُونَ comes from ظَنّ, which means assumption or conjecture. But زَعْم carries a stronger connotation - it's a confident assumption, a claim made with certainty despite lacking knowledge. You were so sure, so confident in your theology. Where are they now?

This question will shatter them. Everything they built their lives around, everything they defended and argued for, all the assumptions they held with absolute confidence - where is any of it now?

translation: Then there will be no [excuse upon] examination except they will say, "By Allah, our Lord, we were not those who associated
commentary:
ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُن فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ وَٱللَّهِ رَبِّنَا مَا كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ

"Then there will be no trial for them except that they will say, 'By Allah, our Lord, we were not those who associated others with Him.'"

After that devastating question, what defense will they offer? ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُن فِتْنَتُهُمْ - "then there will be no trial for them." The word فِتْنَة here means their line of defense, their retaliation, their response. They'll have no argument left, no reasoning to present, no excuse to offer.

إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ - "except that they say" - this is all they can manage: وَٱللَّهِ رَبِّنَا مَا كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ - "By Allah, our Lord, we were not those who associated partners with Him!"

The irony is crushing. They're swearing by Allah ﷻ, calling Him "our Lord," claiming they never committed shirk. Complete denial. "You misunderstood us. You've got it wrong. We didn't worship those idols - we just used them as means to get closer to You."

This reveals something profound about how people rationalize their religious innovations. Even today, people will tell you: "I'm not worshipping the grave, I'm worshipping Allah ﷻ. The grave is just... it's like a faster connection. It's a means. I'm still worshipping Allah ﷻ alone, I'm just going through this channel because it's more effective."

They'll construct elaborate theological explanations to justify their practices. The Hindu will tell you the idol means nothing - you could break it, no problem. It just represents God, reminds me of God, helps me focus on God. It's a tool, not the object of worship.

On the Day of Judgment, these rationalizations will be their last desperate attempt at defense. "We weren't really doing shirk - you just didn't understand our theology correctly. Let us explain..."

translation: See how they will lie about themselves. And lost from them will be what they used to invent
commentary:
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ

"Look how they lied against themselves, and lost from them is what they used to invent."

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ and us: ٱنظُرْ - "Look! Observe!" كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ - "how they lied against themselves."

They're not just lying to Allah ﷻ or the angels or other people. They're lying against their own souls. Their own consciousness knows the truth, but they're contradicting it. Self-deception reaches its peak - denying what their own hearts witnessed throughout their lives.

وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم - "and lost from them" or "wandered away from them." The word ضَلَّ means to be lost, to go astray, to wander off. It can also mean to be forgotten. Everything they fabricated, everything they invented, everything they claimed to believe in - where is it now?

مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ - "what they used to invent." All those lies they fabricated about Allah ﷻ, all those false beliefs they constructed, all those rationalized justifications - completely vanished. Gone. They can't even remember how their theology used to work. The elaborate systems they built in their minds have collapsed into nothing.

translation: And among them are those who listen to you, but We have placed over their hearts coverings, lest they understand it, and in their ears deafness. And if they should see every sign, they will not believe in it. Even when they come to you arguing with you, those who disbelieve say, "This is not but legends of the former peoples
commentary:
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَجَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا ۚ وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كُلَّ ءَايَةٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُوكَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَكَ يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

"And among them are those who listen to you, but We have placed over their hearts coverings, lest they understand it, and in their ears deafness. And if they should see every sign, they will not believe in it. Even when they come to you arguing with you, those who disbelieve say, 'This is not but legends of the former peoples.'"

The surah shifts back to the situation in Makkah. By this point, a decade has passed since the revelation began. Many young people have embraced Islam. Some influential figures have converted. The Quraysh leadership is losing control of the narrative. Every criticism they've launched has backfired.

So they devise a new strategy - one remarkably similar to what the Jews of Madinah would later attempt. The plan: influential elders of the community would come and listen to the Prophet ﷺ, or at least pretend to listen carefully. Then, after hearing him out, they'd use their social credibility to dismiss him: "You young people are impressed by this? We're the elders, we have gray hair, we have life experience. Trust us - this has nothing to offer. Come back to what we taught you. I gave it a fair hearing because I care about you, but this is garbage. It's nothing."

The strategy wasn't to engage with the arguments or provide counterevidence. It was to leverage their social status and credibility to undermine the message itself. "Don't listen to the content - listen to me. I'm telling you it's not worth it."

This pattern persists throughout history. People of influence will use their credibility to discredit Islam without offering proofs or evidence - just their opinion based on who they are. This is why the Prophet ﷺ hoped Umar (RA) would embrace Islam. He was a person of influence. If he joined, others would be encouraged. Islam would be strengthened through his conversion.

Allah ﷻ describes these listeners: وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ - "And among them are those who listen to you."

But then: وَجَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً - "We have placed over their hearts coverings." The word أَكِنَّة, plural of كِنّ, refers to an impenetrable barrier, a protective shield, a covering that nothing can pass through. It's like bulletproof glass or concrete walls designed to stop any projectile.

أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ - "lest they understand it." They're prevented from comprehending what they hear. It's not that the message isn't clear - it's that their hearts have been sealed from understanding it.

وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا - "and in their ears deafness." Not physical deafness, but spiritual deafness. Sound can travel through, but guidance cannot. Their ears are functionally deaf to the truth.

Why would Allah ﷻ do this? Because they earned it. This is a crucial point. Allah ﷻ placed these barriers as a consequence of their own choices. When the heart decides absolutely that it will not accept something, when emotional bias becomes impenetrable, reasoning becomes impossible.

Let me share something from personal experience to illustrate this. I once had an extremely stubborn friend. When he made up his mind about something, that was final. "Let's go to class." "I'm not going." "Come on, you need to go." "You're dumb. I'm not going." No matter what you said, no matter what reasoning you offered, he had one answer until you gave up trying. "Forget it, I'm done talking to you."

His heart was made up, so his mind shut down. He didn't need to think anymore because his decision was final. The heart became impenetrable, and at that point, reasoning was pointless.

Understanding isn't purely a matter of the mind - it's intimately connected to the heart. Once your heart locks onto something - "I'm going to do this no matter what" - you can't reason with that person anymore. They'll say "I understand" but they don't really understand. There's an Arabic saying about love: لَيْسَ فِي الْحُبِّ شُورَىٰ - "There's no consultation in love." When someone is determined to marry someone, you can present every rational argument - "You're too young, you don't have a job, your parents disapprove, she's not compatible with you" - and they'll nod along saying "I understand, I understand," but they don't. Their heart is locked in. You can't counsel them. They'll marry her, divorce two years later, and then say "I should have listened."

This is the state Allah ﷻ describes. When people become obsessed with their position, when their hearts lock onto their worldview, He seals that barrier in place. They earned it through their stubborn refusal to consider the truth, and now they're incapable of understanding.

The consequences: وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كُلَّ ءَايَةٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَا - "And if they should see every sign, they will not believe in it."

This is significant because the Quraysh kept demanding: "Show us a miracle and we'll believe." Allah ﷻ responds: even if they saw every single miracle possible, they still wouldn't believe. Miracles aren't the issue. Their hearts are the issue.

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُوكَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَكَ - "until when they come to you arguing with you." The word يُجَـٰدِلُونَكَ comes from جِدَال, which means to argue with the intent to win, to one-up someone in debate. They're not coming for dialogue or understanding - they're coming to defeat you in argument.

يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ - "Those who disbelieve say, 'This is nothing but legends of the former peoples.'"

The word أَسَاطِير, plural of أُسْطُورَة, comes originally from the Greek word "historia," from which we get the English word "story" or "history." It's fascinating how words travel between languages - Greek to Arabic to English, all connected.

Some people raise an objection: "The Quran claims to be قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا- an Arabic Quran. But it contains words from other languages like Greek and Persian. How can it be purely Arabic?"

This reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of how language works. All languages are interconnected. Just because English uses words from Arabic (admiral, algebra, alcohol) doesn't make English any less English. Just because Arabic borrowed words from Persian or Greek doesn't make it less Arabic. Languages exchange vocabulary naturally through contact and history.

If we trace human linguistic history back, all languages ultimately derive from whatever original language Adam (AS) was taught. As his descendants spread across the earth, they developed different languages, but they all share a common ancestor. Languages are like children of an original parent language, and like genetic inheritance, traces of that original tongue appear in all its descendants.

So finding Greek or Persian loan words in Arabic doesn't contradict the Quran's Arabic nature any more than finding Arabic loan words makes English a foreign language. This is natural linguistic development.

The irony of the disbelievers calling the Quran أَسَاطِير - "stories" or "legends" - is that it proves exactly the opposite of their claim. If this were merely ancient legends, it would be structured like stories: complete narratives with beginnings, middles, and ends, following chronological order with full detail about characters, dates, and locations.

But the Quran doesn't do this. It presents fragments of narratives, extracting only the portions relevant to its theological and moral lessons. It's not trying to tell complete stories - it's teaching principles through strategic portions of historical accounts. The very structure they criticize actually demonstrates that this isn't legendary storytelling at all. It's divine instruction using history as a teaching tool.

Their criticism exposes their own blindness. They can't see what's right in front of them because their hearts are sealed, their ears are deaf, and no amount of evidence will penetrate their self-imposed barriers.

translation: And they prevent [others] from him and are [themselves] remote from him. And they do not destroy except themselves, but they perceive [it] not
commentary:
**وَهُمْ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ وَيَنْـَٔوْنَ عَنْهُ ۖ وَإِن يُهْلِكُونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ**

The ayah describes two distinct behaviors of those who reject the message. First, وَهُمْ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ - they forbid others from it. They actively work to keep people away from the truth through baseless criticism and fabricated stories. Their message is simple: "Don't worry about it. It's nothing." They dismiss the Quran and the message as insignificant, working to prevent anyone from taking it seriously.

The second behavior goes deeper. وَيَنْـَٔوْنَ عَنْهُ - they distance themselves from it in arrogance. The root نأى carries the meaning of becoming distant, going far away while showing arrogance and turning one's face away. It's not just that they reject the message - they can't even stand to be near it. The mere proximity to guidance disturbs them. If they sense they're getting close to hearing something about the truth, they become unsettled: "I don't want to be here. I'm not coming. I have nothing to do with this." They're completely turned off by it.

Then Allah ﷻ reveals the devastating reality: وَإِن يُهْلِكُونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ - they're not destroying anybody except their own selves. The Arabic construction here uses إِن with نَ together, which functions as نفي meaning "they are not destroying anyone." Their efforts to harm the message, to keep people away from it, to mock it and dismiss it - all of this only harms themselves.

And the ayah concludes with a haunting observation: وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ - they don't even realize it. They have no awareness whatsoever that they're destroying themselves. They think they're winning, they think they're smart, they think they're protecting themselves from something foolish. But the reality is the complete opposite, and they're utterly oblivious to it.

translation: If you could but see when they are made to stand before the Fire and will say, "Oh, would that we could be returned [to life on earth] and not deny the signs of our Lord and be among the believers
commentary:
**وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ وُقِفُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ فَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰلَيْتَنَا نُرَدُّ وَلَا نُكَذِّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّنَا وَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ**

Allah ﷻ now takes us to a scene on the Day of Judgment: وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ - if you could only see. This is an invitation to witness something powerful. إِذْ وُقِفُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ - when they are made to stop on the flame, standing at the Fire.

The verb وُقِفُوا is significant. In classical Arabic, this form means "they were made to stop" - someone else stopped them there. They didn't choose to be there. They're being held there, forced to confront what they denied.

Then comes their desperate plea: فَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰلَيْتَنَا نُرَدُّ - they would say, "Oh, if only we could be turned back!" The word يَـٰلَيْتَ expresses a wish that can never be fulfilled - it's used for impossible hopes about the past, like crying over spilled milk. They're wishing for something that will never happen.

Their false promise continues: وَلَا نُكَذِّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّنَا - "and we would not lie against the ayat of our Master." Notice the conditional structure here - "if we would be turned back, in that case we would not lie." It's hypothetical, full of desperation and false hope.

وَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - "and in that case, we will become from those who believe." The progression is striking: first comes hopelessness (يَـٰلَيْتَ), then false hope (maybe we can go back), then empty promises (we'll straighten everything out).

translation: But what they concealed before has [now] appeared to them. And even if they were returned, they would return to that which they were forbidden; and indeed, they are liars
commentary:
**بَلْ بَدَا لَهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُخْفُونَ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلَوْ رُدُّوا۟ لَعَادُوا۟ لِمَا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ**

Allah ﷻ immediately exposes the emptiness of their promises: بَلْ بَدَا لَهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُخْفُونَ مِن قَبْلُ - rather, the fact of the matter is whatever they were hiding all this time has now become exposed to them. Everything they concealed from way back has come out in front of them. There's no hiding anymore, no pretending, no cover-ups.

Then comes an astonishing declaration: وَلَوْ رُدُّوا۟ لَعَادُوا۟ لِمَا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ - and if they were turned back, they would have returned to the very things they were forbidden from. Think about how powerful this statement is. These people are standing at the Fire, experiencing the horror of it, making desperate promises to change - and Allah ﷻ tells us that even if they were sent back after witnessing the Day of Judgment itself, they would return to their old ways.

This connects directly to the theme of the surah. Earlier, the disbelievers demanded miracles, signs they could see with their own eyes. Allah ﷻ is teaching us something profound: they don't need more evidence. They could witness the Day of Judgment itself and still not change. The problem isn't lack of evidence - it's in their hearts.

وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ - and truly, without any doubt, they are nothing but liars.

translation: And they say, "There is none but our worldly life, and we will not be resurrected
commentary:
**وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ**

Now Allah ﷻ reveals what some of the disbelievers really believed deep down: وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا - they just came out and said it: "This is nothing but our worldly life."

Among the mushrikeen of Makkah, there were different groups. Some had vague notions of an afterlife, but others rejected it completely. This group is being identified here - those who believe there's no resurrection, no accountability, nothing beyond this existence.

The word ٱلدُّنْيَا itself is meaningful. The alif at the beginning indicates possession - "our life." But dunya comes from the root meaning "closer" and "lower." It's the life that's right here, immediately accessible, tangible. Their attitude is: "This is my life, and I can do whatever I want with it right now. I don't care what anybody says."

وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ - "and we're not going to be raised again, we're not going to be brought out of the ground, we're not going to be resurrected."

This is the rationalization of many who reject the message. It's not necessarily that they find it impossible to believe - they simply have no sense of urgency about it. Why should they give up the pleasures and freedoms they enjoy right now for a message about some unseen world that nobody has come back to confirm? There's no proof anyone needs to worry about what happens after death, so why not just enjoy this life to the fullest?

This mentality exists today just as it did then. The ultimate goal becomes pleasure, entertainment, comfort. There's no thought given to responsibility, consequences, or what comes after.

translation: If you could but see when they will be made to stand before their Lord. He will say, "Is this not the truth?" They will say, "Yes, by our Lord." He will [then] say, "So taste the punishment because you used to disbelieve
commentary:
**وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ وُقِفُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ قَالَ أَلَيْسَ هَـٰذَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا ۚ قَالَ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ**

Once again: وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ - if you could only see. But notice the difference from ayah 27. In that ayah, they were made to stand عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ (at the Fire). Now: إِذْ وُقِفُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ - when they're made to stand before their Master. This is a different scene - this is standing before Allah ﷻ Himself.

The dialogue is devastating in its simplicity. قَالَ أَلَيْسَ هَـٰذَا بِٱلْحَقِّ - He asks them: "Isn't this the truth? Isn't this real?" 

You used to laugh about it. You said it was impossible. You mocked those who believed in it. Is it real enough for you now?

Their response: قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا - "Of course! Yes! And we swear by our Master!" Now they're swearing by Him. Now they're calling Him Master. Now they acknowledge the reality.

The judgment comes: قَالَ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ - "Then taste the punishment." You wanted to taste the luxury of life, to sample every pleasure, to experience everything this world had to offer? Now taste this. This is what you get بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (because of the continuous disbelief that you engaged in).

translation: Those will have lost who deny the meeting with Allah, until when the Hour [of resurrection] comes upon them unexpectedly, they will say, "Oh, [how great is] our regret over what we neglected concerning it," while they bear their burdens on their backs. Unquestionably, evil is that which they bear
commentary:
**قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً قَالُوا۟ يَـٰحَسْرَتَنَا عَلَىٰ مَا فَرَّطْنَا فِيهَا وَهُمْ يَحْمِلُونَ أَوْزَارَهُمْ عَلَىٰ ظُهُورِهِمْ ۚ أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَزِرُونَ**
