قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱللَّهِ - Those who lied against the meeting with Allah ﷻ have truly bankrupted themselves. The word خَسِرَ means to lose everything, to be in complete loss, to be bankrupt. Everything they thought they were gaining in this life? Total loss.

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً - until the Hour comes to them بَغْتَةً (suddenly, without warning, unexpectedly). 

Before we even discuss the final Day of Judgment, we should remember: whoever dies, their judgment has already come. You don't have to wait for the end of the world to worry about accountability. There are two appointed times - one for each individual, and one for all of humanity together.

But when that final Hour comes, it will be بَغْتَةً - completely sudden. Imagine: it will be just another day. Traffic moving normally. People at work, watching shows, sitting in movie theaters. A mother feeding her baby breakfast. Everything proceeding as usual. She takes the spoon out of the cereal, reaches toward her child's mouth - but before the spoon reaches, the Day of Judgment has begun.

Someone typing an email - before they click the next key, it begins. Someone driving to work - before they reach the next intersection, it begins. Just another ordinary day, and then everything changes. There will be no forecasts, no warnings, no countdown.

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰحَسْرَتَنَا - they will then say: "Oh, our regret! Oh, our devastating regret!" The word حَسْرَة is difficult to capture in translation. In older languages, including classical Arabic, there existed sophisticated words that expressed deep emotions - words that we simply don't have equivalents for in modern speech.

Today, we just say "Oh no!" or "Oh my God!" We've lost the linguistic richness that allowed people to express profound emotions through precise words. This is actually why ancient civilizations, despite having less technology than us, were far more eloquent and therefore far more sophisticated thinkers. When you communicate at a higher level, you think at a deeper level. Allah ﷻ describes the peak of our humanity in the Quran: الرَّحْمَـٰنُ - عَلَّمَ الْقُرْآنَ - خَلَقَ الْإِنسَانَ - عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيَانَ - "He taught eloquence." When we become less eloquent, we become, in a sense, less human. This is reflected in everything - read old poetry in any language and compare it to modern song lyrics. The decline is obvious.

So when these people cry out يَـٰحَسْرَتَنَا, it's not just "oh no" - it's a cry of overwhelming, crushing, devastating regret that penetrates to the core of their being.

عَلَىٰ مَا فَرَّطْنَا فِيهَا - over what we fell short in, regarding this life. The word فَرَّطَmeans to fall short of what you were supposed to do - to do less than your obligations. You were supposed to pray five times; you only prayed three. You were supposed to fast the entire month; you didn't. You were expected to fulfill certain responsibilities, and you fell short.

وَهُمْ يَحْمِلُونَ أَوْزَارَهُمْ عَلَىٰ ظُهُورِهِمْ - and they will be carrying their loads on their backs. The word وِزْر means a heavy burden - it was also used to describe the heavy armor that soldiers wore. 

Here's something profound: the word حِمْل in Arabic is used for both a physical load and for responsibility. In this life, these people refused to carry any responsibility. They rejected accountability. They were too busy enjoying themselves to worry about obligations to their Creator or to others. The equivalent of all that responsibility they threw off and refused to carry? It's now piled on their backs on Judgment Day, a crushing weight they cannot escape.

أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَزِرُونَ - listen up! You had better know - how horrible is that load they're carrying! How terrible is what they bear!

translation: And the worldly life is not but amusement and diversion; but the home of the Hereafter is best for those who fear Allah, so will you not reason
commentary:
**وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ ۖ وَلَلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ**

The surah concludes this passage with a profound statement: وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ - worldly life is nothing but games and entertainment, play and amusement.

But we must understand this correctly. Every single time in the Quran when Allah ﷻ describes worldly life this way, He mentions the Akhirah immediately next to it. He never just says this life is games and entertainment in isolation. Why? Because it's a comparison. Compared to the Akhirah, this is nothing.

The punishments of this world? Games and entertainment compared to the punishments of the next life. The pleasures and joys of this world? Games and entertainment compared to the joys of Paradise. Everything is relative to what comes next.

Think about entertainment for a moment - like a theatrical play. Someone plays the role of a king, someone else plays a servant. They act out their parts for three hours. At the end of the play, is the king really a king? Is the servant really a servant? No - they were just role-playing. In reality, they're both actors, and the servant might actually be wealthier than the "king."

This worldly life is exactly like that theatrical performance. Someone is a president. Someone is a billionaire, wealthy and powerful. Someone else is poor and insignificant - nobody knows their name. But on Judgment Day, the real roles are revealed. Who is truly celebrated with Allah ﷻ? Who is truly honored in His sight?

We may not even know their names in this life. There are people who live quietly, unknown to the world, who are celebrities with Allah ﷻ. One of the great scholars wrote a beautiful story that captures this reality - about a man whose son thinks he's wasting money on gambling. Nobody knows where his money goes. After the father passes away, a stranger comes looking for a notebook. In it are addresses - orphans, widows the father had never met, people he knew about through others. He had been sending money to them anonymously for years. Nobody knew. At the end of the notebook were lists of ahadith he was memorizing, with checkmarks next to each one - hadith memorized, hadith fulfilled through action. Nobody in the world knew who this man really was.

Where are the real celebrities with Allah ﷻ? We don't know. This world is games and entertainment. The next life reveals the truth.

وَلَلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ - and the home of the Akhirah is far better for those who have taqwa, for those who are conscious of Allah ﷻ, who protect themselves from His displeasure.

أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ - will you not then use your intellect? Will you not think? Will you not apply reason?

This is the final challenge - to think deeply about what truly matters, to see beyond the temporary performance of this life, and to prepare for what is real and lasting.

translation: We know that you, [O Muhammad], are saddened by what they say. And indeed, they do not call you untruthful, but it is the verses of Allah that the wrongdoers reject
commentary:
قَدْ نَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيَحْزُنُكَ ٱلَّذِى يَقُولُونَ ۖ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ

Allah ﷻ returns here to the main subject of the Surah, addressing His Messenger ﷺ with profound comfort and wisdom. The ayah opens with قَدْ نَعْلَمُ - "We already know." This is unusual phrasing in the Quran. Normally, we would expect simply نَعْلَمُ - "We know." But the particle قَدْ is added for emphasis, signaling something deeply significant. Allah ﷻ is saying: We certainly know, We absolutely know.

What does Allah ﷻ know? إِنَّهُۥ لَيَحْزُنُكَ ٱلَّذِى يَقُولُونَ - "It grieves you what they say." The word يَحْزُنُكَ carries the meaning of deep sadness, of hurt that weighs heavily on the heart. Allah ﷻ acknowledges that what the disbelievers say causes the Prophet ﷺ real pain. This is the Master of the Universe recognizing the emotional state of His slave and messenger.

Then comes the pivotal comfort: فَإِنَّهُمْ لَا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ - "They do not lie against you." The offense is not against you personally. Your honor is not what's being attacked. Rather, وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ - "However, the wrongdoers argue against and refute the signs of Allah." The word يَجْحَدُونَ means to deny something while knowing it to be true, to argue against it, to attempt to refute it.

Allah ﷻ is telling His Messenger ﷺ something profound: Yes, when they call you a liar, you feel hurt. You feel personally attacked. But you have no need to feel that way. These aren't your words they're rejecting - they're Mine. They're not calling you a liar when they call you a liar - they're calling the Quran a lie. These are My words. I have the right to be offended. You don't need to be offended.

This establishes a critical principle for the messenger's role: remain patient, don't become angry, don't let their words overwhelm you. Just do your work. If anybody has a right to be offended by their words, it's Allah ﷻ Himself. The crime is against Him.

What does this mean for us as carriers of the Messenger's mission today? When we carry the message of Islam, people will say hateful things. Our immediate response nowadays is often to hit back, to defend aggressively. But Allah ﷻ is teaching us something different. The One who has the right to be offended by rejection of His message is Him alone. Our job is to patiently convey the message - to convey it even to people who don't want to hear it, who will be obnoxious and offensive and out of line. And through all of that, we must remain patient. That's when you know you're truly doing the work of Allah's Messenger ﷺ.

translation: And certainly were messengers denied before you, but they were patient over [the effects of] denial, and they were harmed until Our victory came to them. And none can alter the words of Allah. And there has certainly come to you some information about the [previous] messengers
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَصَبَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا كُذِّبُوا۟ وَأُوذُوا۟ حَتَّىٰٓ أَتَىٰهُمْ نَصْرُنَا ۚ وَلَا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكَ مِن نَّبَإِى۟ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

The Prophet ﷺ is being given a powerful reminder: وَلَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ - "Messengers were lied against much before you." You're part of a chain. This isn't new. This has all happened before. 

فَصَبَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا كُذِّبُوا۟ وَأُوذُوا۟ - "They were patient over whatever lies were made against them, and they dealt with harm patiently." This is a pep talk from Allah ﷻ to His Messenger ﷺ. There's a reason I told you all these stories of the prophets. It's not just historical information - it's for you. You have to do this work now, and they put up with this same treatment. You have to as well.

حَتَّىٰٓ أَتَىٰهُمْ نَصْرُنَا - "Until Our aid came to them." What is Allah ﷻ telling His Messenger ﷺ? My aid is coming. Be patient until it arrives.

وَلَا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ - "And there is no one at all in existence that has the power to change or alter the words of Allah." This phrase carries two powerful meanings.

First, it refers to the Quran itself. My words, My ayat, will come and you will recite them to the people. These words will be offensive to some, uncompromising, politically incorrect, unpopular. But you cannot choose when to say what. You don't have the luxury of looking at a situation and deciding, "Maybe I shouldn't talk about this here. This crowd might not take this message well." The Messenger ﷺ doesn't have that choice. Allah ﷻ gives him the words and commands him: Recite to the people right now. These people. Yes, right now. No one can change the words of Allah. Declare them as they are and be patient over what people say.

The second meaning refers to the decrees of Allah ﷻ. When the help of Allah comes, nobody can change it. And until it comes, nobody can bring it earlier. It will arrive precisely when Allah ﷻ has decreed.

وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكَ مِن نَّبَإِى۟ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ - "And already the news of previous messengers has come to you." You already know their track record. You know what they endured and how Allah ﷻ delivered them.

translation: And if their evasion is difficult for you, then if you are able to seek a tunnel into the earth or a stairway into the sky to bring them a sign, [then do so]. But if Allah had willed, He would have united them upon guidance. So never be of the ignorant
commentary:
وَإِن كَانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْرَاضُهُمْ فَإِنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتَ أَن تَبْتَغِىَ نَفَقًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَوْ سُلَّمًا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتَأْتِيَهُم بِـَٔايَةٍ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَمَعَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۚ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ

This is one of the most powerful ayahs addressed to the Messenger ﷺ in the entire Quran. A thought might have crossed his mind: maybe if I showed them a miracle, maybe they would listen. Maybe it would help.

Allah ﷻ addresses this directly: وَإِن كَانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْرَاضُهُمْ - "And if their continual turning away has become hard for you to bear, if that is becoming a big deal for you..." The word كَبُرَ (kabura) means it's been difficult, it's been weighing on you.

Then Allah ﷻ says: فَإِنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتَ أَن تَبْتَغِىَ نَفَقًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَوْ سُلَّمًا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتَأْتِيَهُم بِـَٔايَةٍ - "If you are capable of pursuing a deep trench into the earth or a well-fortified ladder up into the sky, and you can use either the trench or the ladder to bring them a miracle sign - then go ahead."

This is Allah ﷻ speaking with profound authority. You want to perform a miracle? Go dig a trench into the earth. Build a ladder to the sky. Bring them their miracle. The tone is unmistakable: this is not your role, and this is not what will guide them.

وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَمَعَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ - "Had Allah wanted, He would have gathered all of them on the same guidance. He would have unified them on guidance." If Allah ﷻ had willed, He could have made everyone believers instantaneously.

فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ - This phrase is difficult to translate fully. The word الْجَـٰهِلِينَ refers to those who are overwhelmed by emotion, who think with their emotions rather than with wisdom. Don't you dare become of those who get so emotionally wrapped up in wanting to give them guidance that you forget this fundamental lesson.

Even the Messenger of Allah ﷺ is being told this in such direct, authoritative terms. This section of the Quran fortifies the institution of Risalah - of messengership. It clarifies not just who the messenger is to us, but the relationship between Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ. Yes, the Messenger ﷺ holds the highest station among humanity. But at the end of the day, he is still a slave of Allah ﷻ, and Allah ﷻ speaks to His slave the way a Master speaks to a slave. This doesn't decrease the Messenger's honor in our eyes. Rather, it makes us appreciate what it truly means for Allah ﷻ to be the Master of us all, including His most beloved messenger ﷺ. He can speak to His slave with this authority.

translation: Only those who hear will respond. But the dead - Allah will resurrect them; then to Him they will be returned
commentary:
۞ إِنَّمَا يَسْتَجِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ ۘ وَٱلْمَوْتَىٰ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ

إِنَّمَا يَسْتَجِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ - "The only ones who are going to respond are the ones who truly listen." Remember what was mentioned earlier in this Surah? Barriers were placed on their ears - وَقْر was placed in their ears so they cannot hear. Their ears have been sealed.

So the only ones left who will actually respond are the people whose deal hasn't been sealed yet, whose hearts are still open to receive guidance.

وَٱلْمَوْتَىٰ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ - "And the dead, Allah will raise them." This phrase carries a profound double meaning. 

First, it refers to the Day of Judgment - all the dead will be raised. But there's a more immediate meaning within the context: the rest of them, the ones whose hearts are sealed, whose ears are blocked - they're as good as dead right now. This also implies something deep about spiritual death. The ears and the heart are the nourishment of the soul, just as food is the nourishment of the body. When the body stops receiving nourishment, it dies. When the heart and ears stop receiving the nourishment of divine guidance, the soul dies. They are spiritually dead.

Allah ﷻ will raise them on the Day of Judgment. ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ - "Then to Him they will all be returned."

translation: And they say, "Why has a sign not been sent down to him from his Lord?" Say, "Indeed, Allah is Able to send down a sign, but most of them do not know
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَادِرٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُنَزِّلَ ءَايَةً وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

The disbelievers continue their demands: وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ - "And they said, 'How come a miraculous sign hasn't come to him from his Master?'"

Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ: قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَادِرٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُنَزِّلَ ءَايَةً - "Tell them: No doubt about it, Allah is capable, powerful enough to send down a miracle, to send down an ayah."

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - "However, most of them don't know." The question becomes: what don't they know? They don't know what they're actually asking for. They don't understand the implications of their demand. They are the ignorant ones.

translation: And there is no creature on [or within] the earth or bird that flies with its wings except [that they are] communities like you. We have not neglected in the Register a thing. Then unto their Lord they will be gathered
commentary:
وَمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا طَـٰٓئِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَنَاحَيْهِ إِلَّآ أُمَمٌ أَمْثَالُكُم ۚ مَّا فَرَّطْنَا فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ

This ayah opens with a powerful declaration about Allah's dominion over all creation: وَمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا طَـٰٓئِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَنَاحَيْهِ - "There is not a single beast on the earth, nor a bird that flies with both its wings open..." The specificity of بِجَنَاحَيْهِ - "with both its wings" - paints a vivid picture of birds in flight.

إِلَّآ أُمَمٌ أَمْثَالُكُم - "Except that they are species just like you, nations and tribes just like yourselves." Just as you belong to organized communities and nations, so do they. I control you just as I control all of those animals and species. You're asking for signs? Look at the creation around you.

مَّا فَرَّطْنَا فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن شَىْءٍ - "We did not leave out anything, We didn't fall short in mentioning anything in the Book." This raises an obvious question: the Quran doesn't discuss nuclear physics or advanced biology. What does Allah ﷻ mean that He didn't leave anything out?

The answer lies in understanding the Quran's purpose: anything you need to be saved, anything you need for your guidance, everything you need to be convinced that this is the truth - it's all there. Everything necessary for guidance is present in the Book. If you're asking for anything else, you haven't properly appreciated what's already been given to you.

ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ - "Then to their Master alone they will be herded." Even the animals will return to their Lord.

translation: But those who deny Our verses are deaf and dumb within darknesses. Whomever Allah wills - He leaves astray; and whomever He wills - He puts him on a straight path
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا صُمٌّ وَبُكْمٌ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ ۗ مَن يَشَإِ ٱللَّهُ يُضْلِلْهُ وَمَن يَشَأْ يَجْعَلْهُ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا صُمٌّ وَبُكْمٌ - "Those who lied against Our miraculous signs - they are deaf and mute." This is a striking description. Usually, when Allah ﷻ describes the spiritually blind, He mentions three qualities together: deaf, mute and blind (أَعْمَىٰ). What's the third quality that's typically mentioned? عُمْي - blind.

The scholars point out: don't you see how the Quran says "Don't you see?" throughout? If they were completely blind, they couldn't see anything. So Allah ﷻ highlights specifically the other two problems: they can't speak the truth, and they can't hear it. But see? They can very well see - and you'll notice in the next ayahs Allah ﷻ will say "Don't you see yourselves?"

فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ - "In layers of darkness." Even though they can physically see, they are engrossed in shades upon shades of darkness. It's still difficult to perceive truth when you're surrounded by such darkness.

مَن يَشَإِ ٱللَّهُ يُضْلِلْهُ وَمَن يَشَأْ يَجْعَلْهُ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ - "Whoever Allah wills, He misleads, and whoever He wills, He places on a straight path."

These are the ayahs that people become very confused about. Shaytan comes and whispers: "Hey, whoever Allah wants, He can misguide. Maybe Allah wants me to be misguided. It's not my fault."

But we have to understand this ayah in its complete context. This is not a standalone statement. It comes after describing specific people: those who lied against Allah's miraculous signs. That's their stance. That's their choice. Then the consequence upon them is that they're misguided - their ability to benefit from hearing and asking the right questions is taken away. They become mute and deaf, engrossed in layers of darkness. That's what it means when Allah ﷻ wills these people to be misguided - because they didn't appreciate guidance when it was clearly given to them.

It's not a random statement out of nowhere, as if Allah ﷻ arbitrarily decides who to guide and who to misguide. The Quran never says it like that - it's always contextual, always connected to people's choices and responses. But someone who wants to make an excuse for themselves and not take responsibility will always try to use predestination as a shield.

Let me put this as simply as possible. If someone asked me about predestination and tried to use it as an excuse, I would want to respond like this: imagine I slapped him across the face. He would be angry and say, "You slapped me!" I would respond, "God is in control. I'm not in control. How is it my fault?" He would say, "But you hit me!" I would say, "God already knew this was going to happen. He's the one in control. Why are you looking at me with anger? It's your philosophy - you believe God controls everything."

You see the problem? If you're ready to give me personal responsibility for slapping you, but you're not willing to take personal responsibility for your own wrong actions, then your problem isn't philosophical - it's psychological. It's as simple as that. Predestination can be as complex a philosophical problem as you want it to be, and it can be as simple as you want it to be. The choice is yours.

translation: Say, "Have you considered: if there came to you the punishment of Allah or there came to you the Hour - is it other than Allah you would invoke, if you should be truthful
commentary:
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتَىٰكُمْ عَذَابُ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَدْعُونَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

Allah ﷻ instructs His Messenger ﷺ to pose a powerful question to the disbelievers: قُلْ أَرَءَيْتَكُمْ - "Say: Have you ever watched yourselves? Can you imagine looking at yourselves?"

إِنْ أَتَىٰكُمْ عَذَابُ ٱللَّهِ - "If the punishment of Allah came upon you..." أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ - "Or even worse, if the actual Hour came..." When Allah's punishment descends on a nation and destroys it, or when the ultimate punishment begins on the Day of Judgment...

أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَدْعُونَ - "Are you going to be calling anyone other than Allah at that point?" Look inside yourselves and ask yourselves this honest question.

إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ - "If in fact you're truthful." If you're honest with yourselves, you already know the answer.

translation: No, it is Him [alone] you would invoke, and He would remove that for which you invoked Him if He willed, and you would forget what you associate [with Him]
commentary:
بَلْ إِيَّاهُ تَدْعُونَ فَيَكْشِفُ مَا تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِن شَآءَ وَتَنسَوْنَ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ

بَلْ إِيَّاهُ تَدْعُونَ - "No, you're only going to be calling Him alone." When real calamity strikes, all pretenses drop away.

فَيَكْشِفُ مَا تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِن شَآءَ - "And He will alleviate from you whatever you call to Him about, if He wills." Notice that Allah ﷻ adds إِن شَآءَ (in sha'a) - "if He wills." This addition is significant because there's an exception mentioned elsewhere in the Quran: there will be some people who, when raised on the Day of Judgment, will actually go toward their false gods first. Some people will turn to their idols even then. But that will be crushed for them, and then they will forget.

وَتَنسَوْنَ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ - "And you'll forget all the shirk that you've been doing. All of it will disappear from your mind."

translation: And We have already sent [messengers] to nations before you, [O Muhammad]; then We seized them with poverty and hardship that perhaps they might humble themselves [to Us]
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَضَرَّعُونَ

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ - "We had sent to nations much before you..." Notice what's not mentioned explicitly here: what was sent? It's understood - messengers were sent, revelation was sent, the message was sent.

فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ - "And We seized them with difficult times (al-ba'sa) and hardship (ad-darra)." We put them through war, poverty, hunger - extremely difficult circumstances.

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَضَرَّعُونَ - "So that they could become humbled, so that they might humble themselves." The word يَتَضَرَّعُونَ comes from the root that means to become like a branch (الضرع) - specifically, a branch that has no leaves, no good in it, worthless branches covered in thorns that people look down upon. To do tadarra' means to feel so humble that you see no good in yourself, like that useless branch. You come down and put yourself completely down before Allah ﷻ.

This state of tadarra' includes two essential qualities: khushu' (humility in body) and khudu' (humility in heart). Khushu' means being physically overwhelmed, being physically humbled. Khudu' means being emotionally humbled, having no sense of pride when you obey, not being worried about feeling humiliated when you submit to someone. Tadarra' combines both - you are physically and emotionally completely humbled.

Allah ﷻ sent messengers and revelation to nations before, then gave those nations a really hard time, hoping that they would humble themselves in this world before they have to humble themselves in the next life. Because once that time comes in the Hereafter, that humility will be of no benefit. Here and now, humility can still benefit them.

Consider what trials were given to the Quraysh. They faced the Battle of Badr and other confrontations - Allah ﷻ was making them taste difficulty so they might be humbled. What signs were given to the nation of Prophet Musa (AS)? Around nine major signs - the frogs, the blood, the locusts, and more. Each was an opportunity for Pharaoh and his people to get their act together, to humble themselves before worse came.

translation: Then why, when Our punishment came to them, did they not humble themselves? But their hearts became hardened, and Satan made attractive to them that which they were doing
commentary:
فَلَوْلَآ إِذْ جَآءَهُم بَأْسُنَا تَضَرَّعُوا۟ وَلَـٰكِن قَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

فَلَوْلَآ إِذْ جَآءَهُم بَأْسُنَا تَضَرَّعُوا۟ - "Then how come it wasn't the case that when Our punishment came upon them, they became humbled?" When difficulties descended on these nations, instead of them becoming humbled before Allah ﷻ, something else happened. It backfired.

وَلَـٰكِن قَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ - "Rather, their hearts became hard." Instead of softening in humility, their hearts grew harder in defiance. This is the tragedy of wasted opportunities for guidance.

وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - "And Shaytan beautified for them whatever they were doing." Even in the midst of divine warnings and clear signs, Shaytan was able to make their corrupt deeds appear good to them. He made their shirk, their defiance, their rejection of the messengers seem reasonable, even noble. This is the ultimate deception - when punishment comes as a wake-up call, but instead of waking up, the heart hardens further and evil appears beautiful.

This ayah serves as a sobering reminder: trials and difficulties are not always a curse. They can be divine mercy, opportunities to turn back to Allah ﷻ. But if the heart refuses to humble itself, if Shaytan is allowed to beautify our wrong actions, then even the mercy of hardship becomes a missed opportunity. The question for each of us is: when difficulty comes our way, do we humble ourselves before Allah ﷻ, or do we allow our hearts to harden?

translation: So when they forgot that by which they had been reminded, We opened to them the doors of every [good] thing until, when they rejoiced in that which they were given, We seized them suddenly, and they were [then] in despair
commentary:
فَلَمَّا نَسُوا۟ مَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَبْوَٰبَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فَرِحُوا۟ بِمَآ أُوتُوٓا۟ أَخَذْنَـٰهُم بَغْتَةً فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْلِسُونَ

When they completely forgot what they were being reminded of by the messengers, Allah ﷻ opened upon them the doors of everything. This phrasing is crucial—they were constantly being reminded, but they chose to ignore it entirely until they forgot it altogether.

Then something unexpected happened: Allah ﷻ opened the doors of opportunity for them. Their economy flourished, their conquests increased, they experienced unprecedented prosperity. Once they denied the messengers and completely forgot the reminder, they entered a phase of worldly success they had never seen before. Think of it as the calm before the storm—before a violent storm hits, the weather becomes eerily peaceful and still.

The ayah continues: until they were overjoyed with what they had been given. In the middle of their celebration and partying, Allah ﷻ seized them suddenly. The word بَغْتَةً emphasizes the shock—it came without warning, without any chance to prepare.

Then they became مُّبْلِسُونَ —utterly hopeless. This word deserves attention. Yes means sadness or depression, but إبلاس goes much deeper. It means to lose all hope, to have no expectation that things will ever get better again. It's not just being sad—it's complete despair, utter hopelessness. This is why Iblis himself carries this name: he has absolutely no hope in Allah's mercy, no expectation of anything good ever coming to him again.

translation: So the people that committed wrong were eliminated. And praise to Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
فَقُطِعَ دَابِرُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ۚ وَٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Their دَابِر —their root, their foundation, their power—was completely cut off. The word literally means "the last trace" or "the rear." When you cut the dabir of something, you eliminate it entirely, leaving no remnant. The nation that did wrong was made utterly powerless, paralyzed, their influence completely removed.

And then comes a profound statement: وَٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ—all praise and gratitude belongs to Allah ﷻ, the Master of all nations. This teaches us that Allah ﷻ doesn't need any nation to survive for His praise to continue. Even when a powerful nation is destroyed, الحمد remains. Allah's glory doesn't decrease when a civilization falls. He is still the Rabb of all the worlds, worthy of all praise.

translation: Say, "Have you considered: if Allah should take away your hearing and your sight and set a seal upon your hearts, which deity other than Allah could bring them [back] to you?" Look how we diversify the verses; then they [still] turn away
commentary:
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ سَمْعَكُمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرَكُمْ وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِكُم مَّنْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ثُمَّ هُمْ يَصْدِفُونَ

Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ to ask them a penetrating question: "Have you considered—haven't you thought about this—if Allah ﷻ were to take away your hearing and your sight, and seal your hearts?" Note that رَأَيْتُمْ doesn't just mean physical seeing; it means understanding, contemplating, thinking deeply—just as in English when we say "I see" to mean "I understand."

The question continues: What entity worthy of worship and obedience other than Allah ﷻ could bring these faculties back to you? Who else has that power? The answer is obvious: no one.

Then Allah ﷻ tells His Prophet ﷺ: انظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ—observe how We keep presenting the ayat, how We alternate them, how We say the same essential teaching one way, then another way, then another way. This is تصريف, which comes from the same root as the word for wind changing directions. The wind blows this way, then that way, then another direction. Similarly, Allah ﷻ presents the same fundamental truth from multiple angles, using different approaches, making the message accessible through various means.

Yet despite all this effort, ثُمَّ هُمْ يَصْدِفُونَ—then they themselves turn away.

translation: Say, "Have you considered: if the punishment of Allah should come to you unexpectedly or manifestly, will any be destroyed but the wrongdoing people
commentary:
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتَىٰكُمْ عَذَابُ ٱللَّهِ بَغْتَةً أَوْ جَهْرَةً هَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

The Prophet ﷺ is told to ask them: "Have you thought about yourselves? What if Allah's punishment came to you بَغْتَةً—suddenly, without warning—or جَهْرَةً —openly announced, in plain sight?" Whether it comes as a surprise or with advance notice, هَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ—will anyone except the wrongdoing nation be destroyed?

Even if the announcement came that punishment is on its way, and you thought you could take precautions or make your escape, it wouldn't matter. Only the wrongdoers will be destroyed, and no precaution can save them. Allah ﷻ will find them wherever they are, whether the punishment comes with warning or without.

translation: And We send not the messengers except as bringers of good tidings and warners. So whoever believes and reforms - there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
وَمَا نُرْسِلُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ ۖ فَمَنْ ءَامَنَ وَأَصْلَحَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ

We didn't send messengers for any purpose except that they would be مُبَشِّرِينَ —givers of good news—and مُنذِرِينَ —issuers of warning. These are the two fundamental roles of every messenger.

Then notice the beautiful correspondence: فَمَنْ ءَامَنَ وَأَصْلَحَ—whoever believed and fixed their behavior. These two actions perfectly align with the two roles of the messengers. The messenger gives you good news of a healthy life here and a beautiful life in the hereafter, so you come to iman. Then he warns you, and in response to that warning, you correct and improve your behavior (islah).

For those who respond with faith and reform: فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ—there is no fear falling upon them, no danger threatening them, and they will not be among those who grieve.

translation: But those who deny Our verses - the punishment will touch them for their defiant disobedience
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَمَسُّهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ

As for those who denied Our miraculous signs and revelations, يَمَسُّهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ —punishment will touch them. Notice the word مَسّ — a touch, a light contact. This word is used because this refers to worldly punishment. When the final punishment comes in the hereafter, the Quran uses much stronger language. But even when Allah ﷻ destroys nations—which is quite violent—He calls it just "a touch."

This isn't arrogance; it's perspective. Compared to what Allah ﷻ could really do, compared to the punishment of the hereafter, even the destruction of entire civilizations is just a light touch. The real punishment is still to come.

They receive this punishment بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ—on account of their continual corruption, their persistent deviation from the path.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "I do not tell you that I have the depositories [containing the provision] of Allah or that I know the unseen, nor do I tell you that I am an angel. I only follow what is revealed to me." Say, "Is the blind equivalent to the seeing? Then will you not give thought
commentary:
قُل لَّآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِندِى خَزَآئِنُ ٱللَّهِ وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ وَلَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّى مَلَكٌ ۖ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ ۚ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ ۚ أَفَلَا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ

The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to clarify what he is and isn't claiming. Tell them: "I don't say to you that I possess the treasures of Allah ﷻ. I never said to you that I know the unseen. Nor have I said to you that I'm an angel."

This addresses criticisms being made against the Prophet ﷺ that had nothing to do with what he was actually saying. It's as if they weren't listening to his message at all, but instead creating strawman arguments. "You're making all these criticisms against me, but none of them address what I'm actually claiming. I never said any of these things you're attributing to me."

إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ—I am only following precisely whatever has been revealed to me. That's it. That's the extent of my role.

Then comes a powerful rhetorical question: هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ—Could the one who is blind and the one who can see possibly be the same? Are they equal? This connects back to all the previous questions about "Have you seen? Have you observed?" Now finally: Can the blind one and the seeing one be the same?

أَفَلَا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ—Why don't you think about this seriously? Why don't you really reflect on this?

translation: And warn by the Qur'an those who fear that they will be gathered before their Lord - for them besides Him will be no protector and no intercessor - that they might become righteous
commentary:
وَأَنذِرْ بِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ أَن يُحْشَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۙ لَيْسَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ وَلِىٌّ وَلَا شَفِيعٌ لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ gives specific instruction about who to warn: وَأَنذِرْ بِهِ—warn by means of it, by means of the Quran. Warn ٱلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ—those who have any potential for fear, any semblance of awareness that they will be herded towards their Master.

These are the people who recognize that لَيْسَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ وَلِىٌّ وَلَا شَفِيعٌ—they won't have any protective friend besides Him, nor anyone to intercede on their behalf. Focus your warning on those who understand this reality, لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ—so that they may develop consciousness of Allah ﷻ and protect themselves.

translation: And do not send away those who call upon their Lord morning and afternoon, seeking His countenance. Not upon you is anything of their account and not upon them is anything of your account. So were you to send them away, you would [then] be of the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَلَا تَطْرُدِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَدَوٰةِ وَٱلْعَشِىِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُۥ ۖ مَا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ وَمَا مِنْ حِسَابِكَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَتَطْرُدَهُمْ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

This ayah addresses a critical issue of leadership and community building. The command is clear: وَلَا تَطْرُدِ—don't turn away from, don't ignore, don't overlook those who call upon their Master بِٱلْغَدَوٰةِ وَٱلْعَشِىِّ—in the morning and evening, meaning all day long, all night long. يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُۥ—They want nothing but His Face, His pleasure.

The context is crucial. In Makkah, society was stratified by class. There were the financial elite, the political elite, the slave class, foreigners living under tribal protection. Different classes of people occupied different rungs on the social ladder. The first people attracted to Islam were often the weak in society—the oppressed, the slaves, the young, those without influence. This is natural because the message of Islam threatened the power structure, so those at the top of society weren't attracted to it. Accepting Islam would mean taking a loss of status and privilege, which went against their interests.

So now there's a large number of Muslims who aren't socially influential—yet. The Prophet ﷺ, understanding human dynamics and the importance of leadership, made dua for one of two influential men to accept Islam (Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) or Abu Jahl). He knew that even one or two influential people accepting Islam would help tremendously, empowering the movement and strengthening the morale of the weaker members of the community. As part of his legitimate strategy, he made efforts to reach out to the leadership, holding meetings with them to discuss Islam.

While that outreach was important, Allah ﷻ reminds His Prophet ﷺ: Your real assets are those who call on Me day and night, regardless of their social status. Don't ignore them. They're the priority. They're the ones who made the sacrifice. They may not have much value in the eyes of society, but they have immense value with Me.

مَا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ—You're not responsible for their accounting on the Day of Judgment. Whether others listen to you or not, you're not responsible for their audit. وَمَا مِنْ حِسَابِكَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ—And in terms of your accounting, they will have no case against you. They won't be able to say you didn't do your job. You are doing your job, so don't worry about what others think.

But here's the warning: فَتَطْرُدَهُمْ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ—If you were to overlook them, abandon them, not pay attention to them, then you would be from among the wrongdoers. Even the Prophet ﷺ himself is warned about becoming a wrongdoer if he neglects the sincere but socially insignificant believers.

This is profound wisdom about leadership embedded in the Quran. The Prophet ﷺ played many roles—husband, father, friend, caller to Islam, messenger, leader. Every time he's mentioned in the Quran, we must ask: What aspect of his role is being highlighted here? What are we learning? This ayah teaches leadership principles: When a leader is concerned with growing his work and expanding his organization, he'll naturally look to those who can help him most. But Allah ﷻ teaches: Don't overlook the many little volunteers, the workforce, the foot soldiers. Don't just worry about the generals—worry about the infantry. Don't just pursue the elite—take care of your entire congregation. Pay attention to everyone, not just those who can give the most.

Consider this in our modern context. When we think about expanding the masjid, who do we call? All the wealthy people in the community. And who do we almost completely ignore? The average person just trying to make ends meet. We hold fundraising dinners at expensive hotels with $100 entrance fees, and the cab driver who can barely pay his rent can't participate. The working-class family can't be part of that gathering. We can't overlook people like this. Everybody is significant.

The Prophet ﷺ himself is being taught to create a society where we don't evaluate people based on their financial status or social standing before we assign them value. Unfortunately, these lessons have been almost entirely lost on us. Class societies and racism have crept into Muslim communities despite Islam's clear rejection of such hierarchies. In some places, the poor are treated worse than animals—and this happens not just among secularized elite, but even among religious leaders. There's an elitist attitude pervading every sphere of society.

This even makes its way to Muslim communities in the West. In some masajid across the country—though thankfully less so in some places—you find tragic examples of racism and classism. People from certain economic backgrounds, certain villages, certain language groups form their own exclusive cliques, and everyone else can fend for themselves. This is heartbreaking because it's the exact thing the Messenger ﷺ came to warn against.

We're supposed to be calling the world to a just way of life—a way of life free from racism, free from classism, where people aren't judged by the color of their skin, the language they speak, their background or educational credentials. All of us are equal before Allah ﷻ. Only He knows who has more taqwa. He took that right of judgment away from us. Yet we find the worst examples of the very things we came to eradicate within our own communities.

The younger generation, often fed up with what they see in masajid, needs to take a more active role—not to start fights, but to create a new culture in these spaces. When we say "Muslim community," we must remember that in many places, the Muslim community and the masjid are one and the same. The masjid is the community, and the community is the masjid. They're not separate entities.

translation: And thus We have tried some of them through others that the disbelievers might say, "Is it these whom Allah has favored among us?" Is not Allah most knowing of those who are grateful
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ فَتَنَّا بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لِّيَقُولُوٓا۟ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنۢ بَيْنِنَآ ۗ أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ

وَكَذَٰلِكَ فَتَنَّا بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ—And that is how We tested some people by means of others. What a remarkable statement. Some people are so proud of their lineage, their race, their economic status, the neighborhood they live in, the car they drive, that when they see someone else who actually has the gift of guidance, they say: "You want me to follow them? I'm supposed to go to their house? Really, their house? Of all the people in the world, Allah ﷻ chose him? It has to be him?"

That kind of elitism, that arrogance—Allah ﷻ says He tests people through each other. لِّيَقُولُوٓا۟ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنۢ بَيْنِنَآ—So they could say, "These are the people Allah ﷻ has favored over us, from among us?"

Then comes the response: أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ—Doesn't Allah ﷻ know best who is truly grateful to Him? Allah ﷻ chose those who are genuinely grateful to Him. He appreciates those who show authentic gratitude, regardless of their worldly status.

This ayah relates to historical incidents where people came to Prophet Nuh (AS) and said, "We want to listen to you, but the scum of society hangs out with you. We can't be seen with them. Can you get them away from you so we can talk? I don't want to be seen with the homeless guy—it's not becoming of me." They wanted him to separate the believers by creating a class hierarchy among them.

The hypocrites tried similar tactics in Madinah, calling the poor Companions "dogs" and insulting them with statements like, "We have to feed them—they can't even feed themselves." They tried to create classism among the Sahaba (RA).

Even among the Jews who disagreed with the Prophet ﷺ, they claimed he couldn't be a messenger. Why not? Because he wasn't from their race. But Allah ﷻ knows better where to place the station of prophethood. Allah ﷻ knows better who the truly grateful are, who should stand by the side of His messengers.

translation: And when those come to you who believe in Our verses, say, "Peace be upon you. Your Lord has decreed upon Himself mercy: that any of you who does wrong out of ignorance and then repents after that and corrects himself - indeed, He is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَإِذَا جَآءَكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَقُلْ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ كَتَبَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةَ ۖ أَنَّهُۥ مَنْ عَمِلَ مِنكُمْ سُوٓءًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ ثُمَّ تَابَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَصْلَحَ فَأَنَّهُۥ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Now Allah ﷻ teaches the Prophet ﷺ beautiful etiquette. وَإِذَا جَآءَكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا—When those who truly believe in Our miraculous signs and revelations come to you, فَقُلْ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ—then say to them, "Salaam alaikum."

This is a lesson in proper Islamic manners. Normally, the junior should greet the senior first. The student should greet the teacher. The one initiating the salaam is showing respect to the elder, the higher-ranking person. So when someone comes to the Prophet ﷺ, who should say salaam first? They should, out of respect for him. But Allah ﷻ commands His Prophet ﷺ: When these people come to you—these people whom others think nothing of—you say salaam to them first. Subhan Allah.

Then tell them: كَتَبَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةَ—Your Master has written mercy upon Himself. Allah ﷻ has mandated, has made it binding upon Himself, that He will show you people mercy. You deserve my salaam because of this reality.

This mercy manifests in a specific way: أَنَّهُۥ مَنْ عَمِلَ مِنكُمْ سُوٓءًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ—Whoever among you does any evil in a state of overwhelming emotion. The word جَهَـٰلَة here doesn't mean ignorance of the law. It means being overwhelmed by emotion—getting really angry, becoming intensely jealous, being overcome by greed or desire. In that state, you do something wrong.

ثُمَّ تَابَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَصْلَحَ—Then he made tawbah after that and corrected his behavior. Notice: "after that." In another place in the Quran, Allah ﷻ even says مِن بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ—even much after that. Someone might realize months later, "You know, a couple of months ago I did this terrible thing. I need to repent." Allah ﷻ won't say, "Yeah, you should have thought about that two months ago." No. The door remains open.

This is a special mercy for those who stood by the Messenger ﷺ during the toughest times. You have an open-ended opportunity to make tawbah. While the general principle is to repent immediately after committing a sin, Allah ﷻ is teaching that His mercy extends beyond the immediate moment.

فَأَنَّهُۥ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ—Certainly He is extremely forgiving, always merciful. The intensive forms غَفُور and رَّحِيم emphasize the abundance and constancy of Allah's forgiveness and mercy.

translation: And thus do We detail the verses, and [thus] the way of the criminals will become evident
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَلِتَسْتَبِينَ سَبِيلُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ—And that is how We explain the ayat thoroughly, how We detail them, how We open them up. The word فَصَّلَ means to open something that's folded up, to unfold it layer by layer. Imagine a piece of paper folded into a small shape—when you unfold it, it becomes larger and more visible. That's the imagery here.

Allah ﷻ takes something small, mentions it briefly in one place, then opens that subject up somewhere else. He alludes to something here, then makes it a major topic elsewhere. This is how the ayat work throughout the Quran—unfolding meanings, revealing layers, explaining from multiple angles.

وَلِتَسْتَبِينَ سَبِيلُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ—And so that the path of the criminals will become abundantly clear. When you might think of yourself as righteous, when you might not see the criminal behavior in your own actions, over time it will become crystal clear to you. The distinction between the path of truth and the path of crime will become undeniably apparent. You won't be able to claim you didn't know, you couldn't see, you weren't shown. The detailed explanation of the ayat makes the way of the criminals unmistakably clear.

translation: Say, "Indeed, I have been forbidden to worship those you invoke besides Allah." Say, "I will not follow your desires, for I would then have gone astray, and I would not be of the [rightly] guided
commentary:
قُلْ إِنِّى نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُل لَّآ أَتَّبِعُ أَهْوَآءَكُمْ ۙ قَدْ ضَلَلْتُ إِذًا وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to deliver a clear and uncompromising message: "I have been forbidden to worship those you call upon besides Allah ﷻ." This isn't just a personal preference or a negotiating position—it's a divine prohibition. The messenger is then instructed to continue: "I will not follow your empty desires."

This ayah addresses the Quraysh's repeated attempts to find a compromise solution. They wanted the Prophet ﷺ to give a little, take a little—to incorporate some aspects of their religion while maintaining some of his own. For them, religion was fundamentally political. The idols represented the official religion of Mecca, and this religious authority legitimized their entire political structure. When they proposed negotiations, they were thinking politically: "Take a little bit of our religion, keep a little bit of yours. Let's be political about this."

But the Messenger ﷺ is instructed to turn this down completely. There is no compromise in matters of tawheed and the fundamental principles of faith. The ayah continues with the Prophet ﷺ explaining the consequences of such compromise: "If I were to follow that, then I would have been misguided. In that case, I would have slipped off the straight path, and I would not at all be from those who are committed to guidance."

translation: Say, "Indeed, I am on clear evidence from my Lord, and you have denied it. I do not have that for which you are impatient. The decision is only for Allah. He relates the truth, and He is the best of deciders
commentary:
قُلْ إِنِّى عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَكَذَّبْتُم بِهِۦ ۚ مَا عِندِى مَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۖ يَقُصُّ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰصِلِينَ

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to declare his position: "I am committed to a clear proof from my Master." The word بَيِّنَة carries profound meaning. A path that is clear and well-lit is described as bayyinah. Later in the Quran, we learn that bayyinah represents a combination of two essential elements: a clear message and a flawless character. When these two qualities come together—pure revelation and pure conduct—you get bayyinah.

This is beautifully defined later in Surah Al-Bayyinah, where Allah ﷻ describes: "A messenger from Allah ﷻ reciting purified scripture." The character and the message together—that's what makes something a bayyinah. The Prophet ﷺ stands on this clear proof, but the response he receives is rejection: "And you have lied against it."

The "it" here is grammatically masculine, though bayyinah is feminine, because it refers to the Quran—the clear and undeniable proof that has been given to him. They lied against the Quran itself.

The Quraysh would mockingly challenge: "Bring the punishment you're threatening us with!" The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to respond: "I don't have what you're rushing toward. I don't possess it. It's not mine to give you. I have no control over it." Then comes the principle: إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ—"The verdict, the rule, the governance belongs only to Allah ﷻ."

يَقُصُّ ٱلْحَقَّ—"He articulates, narrates, and describes with full detail and texture, the truth." The verb يَقُصُّ implies telling something with complete precision and depth. وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰصِلِينَ—"And He is the best of judges." The word فَاصِل refers to a decision that is final and conclusive. As Allah ﷻ says elsewhere in the Quran: إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ—"It is a final, decisive word." Once Allah ﷻ makes a judgment, it's finished. He is the best of those who make final, conclusive decisions.

translation: Say, "If I had that for which you are impatient, the matter would have been decided between me and you, but Allah is most knowing of the wrongdoers
commentary:
قُل لَّوْ أَنَّ عِندِى مَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِۦ لَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to say something remarkable here. Even though he might not naturally want to express this sentiment, he is told: "Tell them—if I had in my possession what you want to rush toward, the matter would have been settled between me and you."

The implication is powerful: If the decision were in my hands, I would have asked for your destruction. I'm done with you. You should thank Allah ﷻ that He possesses this authority, not me. This is a striking moment of transparency—the Prophet ﷺ is instructed to reveal his human frustration with their persistent rejection and mockery, while simultaneously pointing them back to Allah's ﷻ mercy and wisdom in withholding immediate punishment.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ—"And Allah ﷻ knows best who the wrongdoers are." Allah ﷻ possesses complete knowledge of who deserves what, and when. His timing is perfect, His knowledge complete.

translation: And with Him are the keys of the unseen; none knows them except Him. And He knows what is on the land and in the sea. Not a leaf falls but that He knows it. And no grain is there within the darknesses of the earth and no moist or dry [thing] but that it is [written] in a clear record
commentary:
۞ وَعِندَهُۥ مَفَاتِحُ ٱلْغَيْبِ لَا يَعْلَمُهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ ۚ وَمَا تَسْقُطُ مِن وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُهَا وَلَا حَبَّةٍ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا رَطْبٍ وَلَا يَابِسٍ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ

This ayah shifts to one of the most beautiful descriptions of Allah's ﷻ infinite knowledge. وَعِندَهُۥ مَفَاتِحُ ٱلْغَيْبِ—"And in His possession are the keys to the unseen." The word مَفَاتِح can mean keys, but it also means openings—the places where doors to the unseen open up, the methods by which access to the unseen is granted.

The sky can become a maftah—an opening. The Quran uses this imagery when describing how the sky will open up with doors on the Day of Judgment. Even in this life, there are mafatih: in your grave, one side opens and you see Paradise, or for another person, one side opens and they see the Hellfire. These are the مَفَاتِحُ ٱلْغَيْبِ.

لَا يَعْلَمُهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ—"No one knows them except He."

Then Allah ﷻ describes His knowledge of the visible world: وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ—"He knows what is in the land and what is in the sea."

Then the details become stunning in their specificity: وَمَا تَسْقُطُ مِن وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُهَا—"Not a single leaf falls from any tree, anywhere, that He doesn't know about." He knows how it turns as it falls, where it lands, its trajectory, its final resting place.

وَلَا حَبَّةٍ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْأَرْضِ—"Not a tiny seed hiding inside the darknesses of the earth that He doesn't know about." Notice the plural: ظُلُمَـٰتِ —layers upon layers of darkness beneath the surface of the earth. Even there, not a single seed escapes His knowledge.

وَلَا رَطْبٍ وَلَا يَابِسٍ—"Neither a fresh plant nor something dried up"—those little blades of grass you see on the ground, or the yellow straws scattered about—not one of them exists outside His knowledge.

إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ—"Except that it is completely written on record, captured in a clear book."

When you truly contemplate this ayah, you become overwhelmed by how much Allah ﷻ knows. And here's what's remarkable: that's just the earth. That's just describing His knowledge of the physical world we can see. Then there are the seven heavens. Then there's everything happening in our lives. Then there's the مَفَاتِحُ ٱلْغَيْبِ—the unseen realities. This is just the شَهَادَة (the witnessed realm). There's the entire غَيْب (unseen realm) on top of that. وَهُوَ ٱللَّهُ—and that is Allah ﷻ. Incomprehensible in His knowledge and awareness.

translation: And it is He who takes your souls by night and knows what you have committed by day. Then He revives you therein that a specified term may be fulfilled. Then to Him will be your return; then He will inform you about what you used to do
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَتَوَفَّىٰكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا جَرَحْتُم بِٱلنَّهَارِ ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ لِيُقْضَىٰٓ أَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

This ayah gives us a deeper understanding of the word تَوَفَّىٰ. Fakhr al-Din al-Razi (may Allah ﷻ have mercy on him) provides an excellent explanation by dividing life into three components: the body, life itself, and consciousness.

When we go to sleep, what is taken? The body remains, life remains—what's taken is consciousness. When we die, what is taken? Life and consciousness are taken; the body remains. So there is a type of wafat (taking away) in sleep—something is removed, one of these three elements. In death, two of the three things are taken: consciousness and life.

Notice the eloquence of the Quran here. When Allah ﷻ describes taking someone fully (in death), He uses the ism (noun): إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ—"I am taking you" (to Isa (AS)). The ism is grammatically complete and indicates completeness. But when He describes taking someone partially (in sleep), He uses the verb يَتَوَفَّىٰكُم, which is grammatically incomplete, indicating the incomplete taking that occurs in sleep.

وَيَعْلَمُ مَا جَرَحْتُم بِٱلنَّهَارِ—"And He knows whatever you have been doing during the day." The word جَرَحْتُم literally means "injured" or "wounded," but here it means whatever movements you've been making, whatever damage you've been causing, whatever you've been up to during the day.

ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ—"Then He raises you back in it"—meaning He wakes you up from sleep.

لِيُقْضَىٰٓ أَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى—"So that the named deadline should be executed." He takes you in sleep and raises you again so that your appointed time can finally arrive and be fulfilled.

ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ—"Then to Him is your final return."

ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ—"Then He will thoroughly inform you of all the things you were up to."

translation: And He is the subjugator over His servants, and He sends over you guardian-angels until, when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him, and they do not fail [in their duties]
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنَا وَهُمْ لَا يُفَرِّطُونَ

وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ—"And He is the dominant One above His slaves." This is the second time this exact phrase appears in Surah Al-An'am. The first time Allah ﷻ mentioned it, perhaps we didn't fully grasp its meaning. But in between, He gave us all these detailed reasons demonstrating His complete knowledge and control. Now He repeats it: Do you understand now that I am Al-Qahir above My slaves? This isn't random repetition—it's pedagogical reinforcement after providing the evidence.

وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً—"And He sends upon you guardians." The Christian tradition calls them guardian angels, but this concept exists in Islam as well. Angels are protecting us throughout our lives.

Sometimes you feel their protection tangibly. You're crossing the street, you didn't look the other way, and a car speeds right past where you would have been—something held you back. An accident happens right in front of you the moment after you change lanes. You just had a feeling to turn. Sometimes your car hydroplanes in the rain, starts spinning out of control, you're about to crash, and then suddenly it catches traction again. These protective angels are sent because they must guard you until your appointed time comes.

Your time could come at any moment, so Allah ﷻ sends these protective angels to keep you from trouble, to keep you safe—حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ—"until death comes to one of you." When that moment arrives, it's like a shift change for security guards. The guardian angels' shift is over. They leave, and the Angel of Death arrives for his turn.

تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنَا—"Our messengers take them away." Now, what do they take away? Based on our earlier discussion, they take away consciousness and life.

وَهُمْ لَا يُفَرِّطُونَ—"And they don't fall short." In other words, they don't just knock someone unconscious and leave their life behind. No, they complete their mission fully. They don't miss out, they don't miss their target. These are precise executors of Allah's ﷻ command.

translation: Then they His servants are returned to Allah, their true Lord. Unquestionably, His is the judgement, and He is the swiftest of accountants
commentary:
ثُمَّ رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ أَلَا لَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ وَهُوَ أَسْرَعُ ٱلْحَـٰسِبِينَ

ثُمَّ رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ—"Then they are returned to Allah ﷻ, their true Mawla." The word مَوْلَىٰ means protector and guardian. Allah ﷻ mentions this title here for a profound reason. In the previous ayah, the guardian angels were protecting you throughout life. But now the reality is clarified: Who was guarding those guardians? Who is the real source of all protection? You now come back to Me, your true Protector—مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ—the Real Protector, Allah ﷻ.

أَلَا لَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ—"Listen up! Doesn't He alone own all decision-making?" This is an emphatic rhetorical question demanding acknowledgment of Allah's ﷻ exclusive authority.

وَهُوَ أَسْرَعُ ٱلْحَـٰسِبِينَ—"And He is the quickest in taking account." He is the fastest at performing calculations, at judging and reckoning. There is no delay, no inefficiency in His judgment.

translation: Say, "Who rescues you from the darknesses of the land and sea [when] you call upon Him imploring [aloud] and privately, 'If He should save us from this [crisis], we will surely be among the thankful
commentary:
قُلْ مَن يُنَجِّيكُم مِّن ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ تَدْعُونَهُۥ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً لَّئِنْ أَنجَىٰنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِۦ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ

قُلْ—"Ask them, put this question before them": مَن يُنَجِّيكُم مِّن ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ—"Who rescues you from the multiple shades of darkness on the land and in the sea?"

Picture the scenarios: You're traveling through the woods at night. You're on a highway with no lights, almost out of gas. You're lost in complete darkness. You're out at sea in the pitch black of night. These are horrifying, terrifying situations.

Notice that most horror movies exploit exactly this—darkness. They're never set in a sunny field. It's always dark, always nighttime, always a room without light or with just a hint of illumination where you can barely see something. That's the psychology of fear.

In such moments of terror: تَدْعُونَهُۥ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً—"You call on Him with humility and in secret." تَضَرُّعًا means in desperation: "I know I messed up. I know this morning I woke up late for Fajr. I know I was thinking about my exam during Salah. I'm sorry. Please, just a gas station!" وَخُفْيَةً—"And secretly," calling on Allah ﷻ, whispering to Him, too scared to even raise your voice.

لَّئِنْ أَنجَىٰنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِۦ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ—"If You just rescue us from this, we will be so grateful!"

In these moments of desperation, people who never pray suddenly find themselves making promises to Allah ﷻ. Even those who aren't particularly knowledgeable about religion, even those who openly reject faith—when that moment of terror arrives, they instinctively turn to Him.

translation: Say, "It is Allah who saves you from it and from every distress; then you [still] associate others with Him
commentary:
قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُنَجِّيكُم مِّنْهَا وَمِن كُلِّ كَرْبٍ ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ

قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُنَجِّيكُم مِّنْهَا—"Tell them: Allah ﷻ will rescue all of you from those difficulties."

وَمِن كُلِّ كَرْبٍ—"And from all calamities." كَرْبٍ refers to a really difficult, distressing situation, a serious problem that weighs heavily on the heart.

ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ—"Then you commit shirk." This is a profound play on words. In the previous ayah, people promised لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ—"We will be from those who are grateful" (shakirin). But what do they actually do? تُشْرِكُونَ—they commit shirk. The letters are similar: shukr (gratitude) becomes shirk (polytheism) when rearranged.

Instead of being grateful as they promised, they return to their shirk. This is volume (wrongdoing) at its core—as the Quran defines it: وَضْعُ ٱلشَّىْءِ فِى غَيْرِ مَحَلِّهِۦ—"putting something in other than its rightful place." Instead of gratitude, they offer shirk. Instead of thanking the One who saved them, they turn back to their false gods.

Modern expressions of shirk among Muslims can be subtle but serious. Some hang religious symbols in their cars, treating them as protective charms rather than reminders. If you hang Ayat al-Kursi in your car to recite it, that's beneficial. But if you hang it because you think the physical paper will protect the vehicle like a force field, then you've entered dangerous territory.

Some people visit the Kaaba and secretly cut a piece of the covering to frame in their homes, praying toward it as if it has independent power. This completely misses the lesson of Umar (RA), who kissed the Black Stone while declaring to it: "You're just a rock. You don't benefit, you don't harm. I'm only kissing you because the Prophet ﷺ did." He talked to the rock before kissing it, clarifying his intention, protecting his tawheed.

One teacher made a startling observation: many people commit shirk by worshiping the Kaaba itself rather than Allah ﷻ. We don't pray to the Kaaba—we pray toward it because Allah ﷻ commanded us to. The structure itself has no inherent power; Allah ﷻ blesses it. That clarity must remain in the mind of every Muslim.

translation: Say, "He is the [one] Able to send upon you affliction from above you or from beneath your feet or to confuse you [so you become] sects and make you taste the violence of one another." Look how We diversify the signs that they might understand
commentary:
قُلْ هُوَ ٱلْقَادِرُ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ أَوْ مِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُم بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَفْقَهُونَ

قُلْ هُوَ ٱلْقَادِرُ—"Tell them: He is the One in complete control, the One with complete power."

This ayah describes three categories of punishment, typically progressing from bad to worse to worst—a common pattern in Quranic listings.

عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ—"To send upon you punishment from above you." This could be devastating storms, fire from the sky, or other calamities descending from above.

أَوْ مِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ—"Or from beneath your feet." This could be earthquakes, the earth cracking open, lava emerging, landslides—punishment rising from the ground itself.

أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا—"Or He will break you up with confusion into different factions." This is the third and actually the worst form of punishment.

وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُم بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ—"And make one group among you taste the violence and warfare of another." You will fight each other to death.

Isn't the Ummah facing this third punishment today? How many shootings have occurred inside masajid in the Muslim world? How many groups exist whose sole purpose is to declare other groups disbelievers, insisting that you can only protect your faith by hating those people? This rhetoric of murder and hate—not even directed at outsiders, but at fellow Muslims.

Allah ﷻ explicitly describes this internal division and violence as punishment. When self-proclaimed scholars spend their time making takfir of other Muslims, when the message becomes about who to hate rather than who to love, when violence between Muslim factions becomes normalized—this is the punishment Allah ﷻ warned about.

ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ—"Observe how We keep changing the lessons, how We alternate the ayat."

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَفْقَهُونَ—"So that hopefully they will understand deeply." فِقْه means deep, comprehensive understanding. The repetition, the variation in presentation, the multiple angles—all of this is so people might finally grasp the message with depth.

translation: But your people have denied it while it is the truth. Say, "I am not over you a manager
commentary:
وَكَذَّبَ بِهِۦ قَوْمُكَ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ قُل لَّسْتُ عَلَيْكُم بِوَكِيلٍ

وَكَذَّبَ بِهِۦ قَوْمُكَ—"Your nation has lied against it." What have they lied against? The Quran—this is the third or fourth time in this passage that the rejection of revelation is emphasized.

وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ—"While it is the truth." Despite its clear truthfulness, despite all the evidence, they persist in their denial.

قُل لَّسْتُ عَلَيْكُم بِوَكِيلٍ—"Tell them: I am not in charge of you. I am not the caretaker of your matters, I am not the guardian responsible for your affairs."

The implication between the lines connects to what was already said about punishment: You've offended Allah ﷻ, you've rejected the truth, and I am no longer responsible for what happens next. Whatever comes now is on you. The messenger has delivered the message; he cannot force acceptance.

translation: For every happening is a finality; and you are going to know
commentary:
لِّكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُّسْتَقَرٌّ ۚ وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ

لِّكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُّسْتَقَرٌّ—"For every major event, there is a particular time and place where it will settle and occur." نَبَإٍ refers to significant news or momentous events. مُّسْتَقَرٌّ means a settled station, an appointed time and place.

Every major event—including the punishment that has been discussed—will take place at its designated time. Yes, punishment for these people has been declared, but Allah ﷻ has appointed a specific time for its arrival.

وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ—"And soon you will find out." 

Notice the shift in address. Throughout this passage, the Prophet ﷺ has been instructed to speak to the disbelievers: "Tell them... tell them... tell them..." But here, regarding the final threat of punishment, Allah ﷻ speaks to them directly: "You will find out. All of you—soon you will know exactly when that appointed time arrives."

This direct address carries weight—it's no longer mediated through the Messenger ﷺ. It's Allah ﷻ Himself making the promise to those who persist in rejection: You will certainly discover the reality of what you're denying.

translation: And when you see those who engage in [offensive] discourse concerning Our verses, then turn away from them until they enter into another conversation. And if Satan should cause you to forget, then do not remain after the reminder with the wrongdoing people
commentary:
وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَخُوضُوا۟ فِى حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِۦ ۚ وَإِمَّا يُنسِيَنَّكَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَلَا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Allah ﷻ begins this ayah with a powerful instruction: "And when you see those who يَخُوضُونَ in Our signs..." The word يَخُوضُونَ carries deep meaning beyond simple conversation. It means to talk trash, to run your mouth carelessly. The older Arabic term for this is الخوض - empty, critical talk done purely for entertainment.

This is exactly what the disbelievers of Quraysh used to do. They would mock the religion with questions designed to belittle: "Your religion says angels have how many wings? What do you get in Jannah? Water? Rivers under your feet? Really?" They would frame their questions as though the one answering was foolish, or what they believed was absurd. This is خوض - mockery disguised as inquiry.

We see this same pattern today. Your uncle at the party starts in: "Islam says 2.5% zakat? That doesn't make any sense. Alcohol - what's really wrong with it?" Or the dinner table conversation turns to casual criticism: "The Quran was compiled when exactly? We're not even sure. And hadith - there's so much confusion there." This is party talk, entertainment talk, where religion becomes a game to poke holes in rather than something sacred to understand.

Allah ﷻ even mentioned this behavior earlier in Surah Al-An'am at ayah 68, and in Surah An-Nisa (4:140), where He commanded believers to leave gatherings where people mock His signs until they change the subject. The instruction was clear then, and it's reinforced here: when people engage in this kind of talk, don't sit with them. It will challenge your integrity and your character in Islam.

Now Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ directly: when you see those who talk trash about Our ayat, فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ - ignore them, turn away from them. This is not the time for dawah. When people are asking those kinds of questions at your house gatherings, at parties, at the MSA, that is not the moment to continue the conversation. Why? Because they are digging their hole deeper. They're putting themselves in sin, and you might think you're doing good by responding, but you're not.

When you start debating, they realize they've hooked you - and that's all they wanted. They'll play with you more and more, and you won't even realize it. An hour goes by, two hours go by, and you've exhausted yourself. At the end of it all, they say, "Yeah, see? All your arguments are the same." You've fallen into their trap.

Many of us have fallen into these conversations before. You feel compelled to respond, you get into it, and nothing comes of it. They're just playing you the whole time. You must be smart enough to recognize when someone is doing خوض. And when you recognize it, the command is clear: ignore it. حَتَّىٰ يَخُوضُوا۟ فِى حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِۦ - until they start running their mouth about something else. Until they start criticizing something else. They talk about Islam the way they talk about football or basketball, very casually. So until they change the subject, don't engage.

But then Allah ﷻ acknowledges human weakness: وَإِمَّا يُنسِيَنَّكَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ - and if Shaytan makes you forget. What are we learning here? Shaytan will keep you from remembering this command. You will get into that conversation, and only after significant damage has been done will you realize, "Oh my God, I shouldn't have done that. I shouldn't have even started. I don't know why I got caught up in this."

فَلَا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - then after the reminder has come to you, do not sit with the wrongdoing people. Once you remember, once you realize what's happening, leave immediately. Don't continue sitting with that crowd.

Let me share a personal experience. I was at an Eid party in New York years ago - a traumatic experience I still remember. A mutual friend had invited me, and I didn't know most of the people there. One guy said, "You know, if the Quran was revealed today, it would have been completely different. What am I going to do with the Battle of Badr? It doesn't benefit me at all. The Quran has no relevance to me. It's just talking about these weird old stories that are irrelevant to our time, to our problems, to our context."

He thought he sounded really intelligent. My blood was boiling. But Alhamdulillah, I remembered this ayah. These are exactly the people Allah ﷻ is describing. Later, I told my friend, "Can we have dinner with that guy sometime? Just bring him over to my house." I wanted to engage him on my turf, not his, and not to start an argument but to have a real conversation.
