When we met privately, I asked him, "Do you really think it's a revelation from God or not?" He said, "Yeah, of course I do." I said, "So God doesn't know what's coming in the future? You believe it's the last revelation?" He said, "Yeah." I said, "So God didn't think ahead? Is that what you're saying?" He paused: "I'm not saying that." I said, "But that's what you said. You said 'What am I going to do with Badr?' So what did you mean?" There was silence.

But imagine if I had that conversation at the party. What would have happened? Other people would have chimed in: "No, but you're being too orthodox. We need an enlightened view. We have to look at things more comprehensively." Then someone throws in, "What about Christians going to hell? My boss is really nice and he's Jewish - is he going to hell?" Now you're passing verdicts at a party about who goes to hell. Someone else brings up alcohol, then something else, and you're being bombarded from all directions.

You have to get out of those situations. Don't get tangled in those conversations. This is the wisdom of what Allah ﷻ is teaching us here.

translation: And those who fear Allah are not held accountable for the disbelievers at all, but [only for] a reminder - that perhaps they will fear Him
commentary:
وَمَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ وَلَـٰكِن ذِكْرَىٰ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ

Allah ﷻ now addresses a concern believers might have: what about our responsibility toward these people who mock? He says, وَمَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ - and those who have taqwa, those who are truly conscious of Allah, they have no responsibility in terms of the account of the others. You are not answerable for them.

وَلَـٰكِن ذِكْرَىٰ - but only reminder. The only responsibility you have is to give powerful, good reminder. And Allah ﷻ is saying that a gathering where people are mocking is not a place where you can give good reminder. Their minds are in critical mode at that point. That's not the right atmosphere. Find a better time. Find a personal time.

Here's my advice: many of you have Muslim friends or family members who aren't very religious - cousins, coworkers, friends from way back. It's not that you shouldn't be friends with them. You can get together with them, go to their houses for dinner, socialize with them. But when they start talking about religion in that mocking way, ignore the conversation. Just ignore it.

If you want to give religious advice, do it one-on-one, not in that group setting. In the group, you can only do damage and you'll get owned. I'm telling you, you will get owned. But one-on-one, in a calm setting, that's where real conversations can happen. That's where ذكرى, real reminder, can take place.

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ - so that perhaps they may become conscious of Allah. The goal of reminder is to awaken taqwa, but it has to be done in the right way, at the right time, in the right setting.

translation: And leave those who take their religion as amusement and diversion and whom the worldly life has deluded. But remind with the Qur'an, lest a soul be given up to destruction for what it earned; it will have other than Allah no protector and no intercessor. And if it should offer every compensation, it would not be taken from it. Those are the ones who are given to destruction for what they have earned. For them will be a drink of scalding water and a painful punishment because they used to disbelieve
commentary:
وَذَرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لَعِبًا وَلَهْوًا وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَذَكِّرْ بِهِۦٓ أَن تُبْسَلَ نَفْسٌۢ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ لَيْسَ لَهَا مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِىٌّ وَلَا شَفِيعٌ وَإِن تَعْدِلْ كُلَّ عَدْلٍ لَّا يُؤْخَذْ مِنْهَآ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُبْسِلُوا۟ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ ۖ لَهُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ وَعَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ describes a particular category of people: وَذَرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لَعِبًا وَلَهْوًا - and leave those who have taken their religion as play and entertainment. There are people who only talk about religion as a game, as entertainment. They have certain talking points, certain anecdotes they've memorized. Every conversation you have with them, they bring up the same things: "Some things just don't make sense, and here's what they are. I read somewhere, I heard somewhere..."

They use the same material every time. It's their game, their script. وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا - worldly life has deceived them. Worldly life has such a hold on them that religion only has room as entertainment, as small talk. It doesn't have a central role in their lives. To them, religion is irrelevant, something on the side. If anything, it should be talked about as a joke. Leave those people alone.

وَذَكِّرْ بِهِۦٓ - however, at the same time, remind with it. Remind with what? With the Quran. When you study stories and ayat like this one, you realize how relevant and how alive the lessons of the Quran are. What a tragedy it is that Muslims generally don't know how powerful the guidance of the Quran is for our daily issues - things we go through every day. Allah ﷻ is giving us direct counsel: this is what you need to do, this is how you handle this situation, this is how you handle that situation.

These situations didn't just occur with the Prophet ﷺ and the Quraysh. They're still happening now, and they'll keep happening.

أَن تُبْسَلَ نَفْسٌۢ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ - lest a soul become completely filled with hopelessness because of what it has earned. The word تُبْسَلَ means to be handed over to destruction, to be left in complete confusion and despair. Remind people with the Quran so they don't end up in this state.

لَيْسَ لَهَا مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِىٌّ وَلَا شَفِيعٌ - it will not have anyone besides Allah as a protective friend, no one to make a case for it. On the Day of Judgment, there will be no lawyers, no advocates except by Allah's permission.

وَإِن تَعْدِلْ كُلَّ عَدْلٍ لَّا يُؤْخَذْ مِنْهَآ - and if it tries to give every possible compensation, every ransom available, it will not be accepted from that person. Even if someone shows up on Judgment Day wanting to offer everything - every good deed they could imagine, every form of payment - it won't be taken from them.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُبْسِلُوا۟ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ - these are the people who have been turned upside down, left in complete confusion because of what they earned. Their own choices led them to this state.

لَهُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ وَعَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ - they will have drink from boiling water, and painful punishment because of the disbelief they used to engage in. Notice: the boiling water isn't even the main punishment. That's just the beginning. Then comes the painful punishment - something beyond that.

translation: Say, "Shall we invoke instead of Allah that which neither benefits us nor harms us and be turned back on our heels after Allah has guided us? [We would then be] like one whom the devils enticed [to wander] upon the earth confused, [while] he has companions inviting him to guidance, [calling], 'Come to us.' " Say, "Indeed, the guidance of Allah is the [only] guidance; and we have been commanded to submit to the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
قُلْ أَنَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُنَا وَلَا يَضُرُّنَا وَنُرَدُّ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَابِنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ كَٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَهْوَتْهُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ حَيْرَانَ لَهُۥٓ أَصْحَـٰبٌ يَدْعُونَهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَى ٱئْتِنَا ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ هُوَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۖ وَأُمِرْنَا لِنُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Allah ﷻ now commands the Prophet ﷺ to pose a question: قُلْ أَنَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُنَا وَلَا يَضُرُّنَا - Say: Are we supposed to call upon anything other than Allah, something that has no power to benefit us and no power to harm us?

وَنُرَدُّ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَابِنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ - Are we supposed to be turned back on our heels, to retreat and deteriorate to what we used to be, even after Allah has guided us?

Then Allah ﷻ gives a powerful analogy: كَٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَهْوَتْهُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ حَيْرَانَ - like the one whom the devils have lured, whom they've gotten the best of on the earth, and he is now in a state of confusion. The word ٱسْتَهْوَتْهُ means the devils have captivated him, pulled him into their influence. He's wandering the earth حَيْرَانَ - completely overwhelmed, in shock.

Sometimes good people get overwhelmed by the wrong company. Bad human company invites demonic company. The shayateen among humans work with the shayateen among jinn, and this person becomes completely overtaken.

لَهُۥٓ أَصْحَـٰبٌ يَدْعُونَهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَى ٱئْتِنَا - but he has companions, old friends, who are calling him back to guidance: "Come with us! Come back!" Picture this: he's caught up in the wrong crowd. He used to be a good guy. You used to see him at the masjid once in a while, at the MSA sometimes. Now he's disappeared. You see him hanging out with the wrong people. His eyes are different - you can tell something's wrong. Maybe he's consuming something, getting into things he shouldn't.

But he has old friends who haven't given up on him. They call out: ٱئْتِنَا - "Come with us! Come to the program. Come to the masjid. Just stay with us."

This ayah describes friends who don't abandon friends. You don't abandon those friends who've gone astray. You keep calling them back.

Let me share a story. I had a student years ago in another city. Very serious, very smart guy. Extremely intelligent, only about 17 years old. He learned Arabic like it was a joke - everything came easy to him. He studied Quran, studied tafsir, studied hadith. Other young people looked up to him. He would give khutbahs sometimes, short talks.

Then he traveled abroad to study Arabic and Islamic studies. Somehow he ended up rooming with an agnostic. Six months later, he left the religion. This guy who had studied Quran and was teaching others - he came back and said, "I'm not a Muslim anymore."

When he returned, scholars sat him down. Famous speakers tried talking to him. I talked to his dad, and we set up meetings. I sat with him several times. He had these philosophical questions, various doubts. Everyone at the masjid was so angry. How could he talk like that? How could he talk about the Prophet ﷺ like that? How could he talk about Allah ﷻ like that? Everybody hated him. Nobody wanted to talk to him.

When I face difficulties, my formula is simple: open the Mushaf and start reciting, just randomly. You'll find guidance, you'll find therapy. What I found in those days was this ayah, and the message was clear: don't leave him. He's surrounded by voices of Shaytan. At least let him hear the voice of a friend too.

So I would reach out: "Hey, you want to go play some pool? Want to hang out? Play basketball?" I didn't bring up religion with him for a couple of years. Nothing. But I didn't leave him. The point was: don't abandon him. Just stay in touch somehow.

A couple of years later, I saw him at the masjid during Ramadan. I still didn't push religion on him. But there was improvement. Things were evening out. Things were getting better.

We can't abandon people like that, but we also can't engage them in debate mode. What do you do? Don't bring up religion argumentatively because that won't go well. Just let them be. Give them company. Slip in a reminder here and there without making it a debate.

قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ هُوَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ - Tell them: Indeed, the guidance of Allah - it is the ultimate guidance. Guidance is the ownership of Allah ﷻ. You can try to invite that friend and help them all you want, but when Allah's decree comes, if they deserve guidance, they'll get it. If they don't, they won't. You can't own guidance. You're not in charge of guiding anybody. You're only in charge of reminder - reminder, reminder, reminder.

But you still have to call them. You don't abandon them.

وَأُمِرْنَا لِنُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - And we have been commanded to submit ourselves to the Master of all nations. This is our path. This is what we hold onto.

translation: And to establish prayer and fear Him." And it is He to whom you will be gathered
commentary:
وَأَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ

Allah ﷻ concludes this passage with the foundations: وَأَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ - and establish the prayer. Notice the unique structure here. Usually the command would be أَمَرَنَا أَن نُّقِيمَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ - "We were commanded that we establish prayer." But instead, Allah ﷻ says أُمِرْنَا... وَأَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ - "We were commanded... and [you] establish prayer."

It's as if Allah ﷻ is speaking directly to us: "You - establish the prayer!" Not just maintain it, but truly establish it in your life.

وَٱتَّقُوهُ - and be conscious of Him, be cautious of Him. How do we submit to the Master of all nations? We maintain our connection with Allah ﷻ through salah. We maintain our conversation with Him. We keep reminding ourselves of what comes next.

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ - and He is the One to Whom you will be gathered. Only toward Him will we return. Only toward Him will we be brought back.

This is what we've been commanded to do: maintain these things. Maintain salah. Maintain taqwa. Keep remembering that we're returning to Him. These are the anchors that keep us steady when the world around us tries to pull us in every direction.

translation: And it is He who created the heavens and earth in truth. And the day He says, "Be," and it is, His word is the truth. And His is the dominion [on] the Day the Horn is blown. [He is] Knower of the unseen and the witnessed; and He is the Wise, the Acquainted
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُن فَيَكُونُ ۚ قَوْلُهُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ وَلَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ ۚ عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ

And He is the One who created the heavens and the earth with truth (bil-haqq). The expression بِٱلْحَقِّ encompasses multiple dimensions: truthfully, purposefully, and with perfect wisdom. When Allah ﷻ creates بِٱلْحَقِ, it means every aspect of creation has an intended purpose. This stands in direct opposition to بَاطِل, which signifies purposelessness. We find this contrast elsewhere in the Quran where Allah ﷻ declares, "Our Lord, You did not create this in vain" – affirming that creation without purpose would be batil. Therefore, when He creates with haqq, it means creation with divine purpose; when opposed to batil, it means creation with profound meaning rather than meaningless existence.

The ayah continues: "And the day on which He says 'Be,' then it will come into existence." قَوْلُهُ ٱلْحَقُّ – His word itself is the ultimate truth, the ultimate reality. His very command carries His purpose within it. The word haqq here emphasizes that His command is not arbitrary but filled with divine truth and wisdom.

"And He alone will have dominion on the day when it will be blown into the trumpet." While Allah ﷻ already possesses absolute dominion today – He is already Al-Malik – this ayah specifically mentions His kingship on the Day of Judgment for a powerful reason. In this world, false claimants to authority exist. People can make claims to power, to lordship, to kingship. But on that Day, no pretender will remain. Every false claim will be obliterated, leaving only His uncontested sovereignty manifest to all creation.

Notice the grammatical construction: يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ. Many translations render this as "the trumpet will be blown," but this misses a subtle rhetorical point. The structure makes what will be blown into the trumpet deliberately unnamed and mysterious – it's majhul, unspecified. The focus isn't on who blows or what instrument is used, but on the tremendous, indescribable event that will occur when something beyond our comprehension is breathed into that trumpet. The mystery itself amplifies the gravity of that moment.

عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ – the Knower of the unseen and the seen. His knowledge encompasses absolutely everything, both what is hidden from our perception and what is apparent to us. وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ – and He is the Wise, the All-Aware. He possesses ultimate wisdom in all His decisions and complete, detailed awareness of all matters, down to the finest particulars.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when Abraham said to his father Azar, "Do you take idols as deities? Indeed, I see you and your people to be in manifest error
commentary:
۞ وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ ءَازَرَ أَتَتَّخِذُ أَصْنَامًا ءَالِهَةً ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكَ وَقَوْمَكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

Now we enter the narrative of Ibrahim (AS), a particularly significant case study in this surah's exploration of tawhid versus shirk. Remember the earlier passage where we discussed someone who demonstrates the flawed nature of shirk to their people? Keep Ibrahim (AS) in mind as we see how he fulfills this role.

"And when Ibrahim said to his father, Azar..." The name "Azar" mentioned here has sparked scholarly discussion. Some scholars suggest this wasn't Ibrahim's biological father but rather his uncle, since the word أَب can be used in Arabic to refer to any respected elder. However, other scholars, including Imam Qurtubi, make a compelling argument that this was indeed his father's name, and that the Quran deliberately corrects historical inaccuracies found in the scriptures of the People of the Book. This would align with the broader theme we find later in this surah about how the People of the Book concealed and altered parts of their scripture. The Quran, in mentioning Azar specifically, may be setting the record straight.

"Are you taking idols as gods?" The word أَصْنَامًا doesn't just mean idols – it carries the connotation of beloved idols, objects of deep emotional attachment and devotion. Ibrahim (AS) confronts this attachment directly.

إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكَ وَقَوْمَكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ – "Indeed, I see you and your people in clear, manifest misguidance." The word مُّبِينٍ means there's no ambiguity about it, no room for doubt. The error is obvious, the misguidance patent and undeniable. This directness from Ibrahim (AS) sets the stage for what follows.

translation: And thus did We show Abraham the realm of the heavens and the earth that he would be among the certain [in faith]
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُوقِنِينَ

"And thus We show Ibrahim the dominion of the heavens and the earth." Notice the verb tense: نُرِىٓ is present tense, indicating continuous showing. This isn't a one-time revelation but an ongoing unveiling. Where the past tense أَرَيْنَا would indicate a single completed event, نُرِىٓ suggests Allah ﷻ continuously revealed the malakut – the kingdoms, the inner workings, the divine dominion – of the heavens and the earth to Ibrahim (AS).

The word مَلَكُوتَ is the plural form, implying multiple kingdoms – the earth is a kingdom, the skies are a kingdom, and within them are countless domains of Allah's sovereignty. These are not just physical realities but the deeper realities of creation, the divine order underlying everything.

وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُوقِنِينَ – "So that he would be among those with absolute conviction." This divine showing wasn't merely for knowledge but for يَقِين – that unshakeable certainty that transcends intellectual understanding and becomes lived reality. Ibrahim (AS) was being prepared for his role through this direct experiential knowledge of Allah's dominion.

## Ayahs 76-78

فَلَمَّا جَنَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلَّيْلُ رَءَا كَوْكَبًا ۖ قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَبِّى ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَ قَالَ لَآ أُحِبُّ ٱلْـَٔافِلِينَ

فَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلْقَمَرَ بَازِغًا قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَبِّى ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَ قَالَ لَئِن لَّمْ يَهْدِنِى رَبِّى لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ

فَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلشَّمْسَ بَازِغَةً قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَبِّى هَـٰذَآ أَكْبَرُ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَتْ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ

These ayahs present Ibrahim's famous engagement with his people's worship of celestial objects. Understanding this passage correctly requires us to recognize what's really happening here. The common interpretation suggests Ibrahim (AS) progressed through different stages of worship – first the star, then the moon, then the sun – before finally realizing none of them were God. However, this interpretation raises serious problems.

Ibrahim (AS) is depicted throughout the Quran as one of the most intelligent, far-sighted people who ever lived. Would someone of his intellect really need to wait for a star to set before realizing it would set? Would he need the moon to disappear before understanding it was temporary? Did he require the sun to vanish before grasping that it too would fade? This seems inconsistent with his demonstrated wisdom.

Consider also: later in his life, when facing the idol worshippers, how does Ibrahim (AS) demonstrate their foolishness? He breaks all their idols except the largest one, leaving the axe with it, then suggests they ask their "strongest god" what happened. It's a demonstration of the absurdity of their beliefs. Similarly, here he's not expressing his own confusion but rather dramatizing the illogic of his people's worship for their benefit.

Notice the critical word: قَالَ – "he said." Not فَكَّرَ – "he thought." He's speaking these words out loud, performing a demonstration. He's walking his people through their own reasoning process and showing them where it breaks down.

"When the night covered over him, he saw a star. He said, 'This is my lord.'" Then when it set (أَفَلَ – a term specifically used for heavenly bodies disappearing), "he said, 'I do not love those that set.'" The argument unfolds: if this is your god, why does it disappear? Why is it temporary?

"When he saw the moon rising (بَازِغًا – meaning becoming bright and luminous), he said, 'This is my lord.'" But when it too set, he said something more personal: لَئِن لَّمْ يَهْدِنِى رَبِّى لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ – "If my true Lord does not guide me, I will surely be among the lost people." This is key: he distinguishes between these false objects of worship and "my true Lord" (رَبِّى). He's already aware of the real Lord; he's pointing out the inadequacy of their false gods.

Then comes the sun. فَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلشَّمْسَ بَازِغَةً – "When he saw the sun rising in its full glory." Here we encounter a beautiful linguistic subtlety. In Arabic, الشَّمْس (the sun) is grammatically feminine. But Ibrahim (AS) doesn't say "هَـٰذِهِ رَبِّى" ("this [feminine] is my lord"). Instead he says هَـٰذَا رَبِّى using the masculine demonstrative pronoun.

Why? Because he's not actually pointing to the sun itself. He's keeping his reference ambiguous, perhaps pointing to something beyond or behind it, never truly dignifying the sun by acknowledging it as the referent of his words. The Allah ﷻ, in translating and relating this story, acknowledges the sun's feminine nature by describing it as بَازِغَةً (bazighatan – with the feminine ending) but Ibrahim's words maintain deliberate ambiguity.

This technique is called تَوْرِيَة in Arabic rhetoric – saying something that has one meaning to you while your listener understands something else. There's a famous example from Islamic history: when British colonial police were searching for a certain scholar who spoke against them, they came to him directly, not recognizing him, and asked, "Have you seen the Sheikh?" He took a step backward and said, "He was right here" – and they ran off looking for him. He didn't lie; the Sheikh was indeed right there moments before he stepped back. This is tawriya – the speaker has one intent, and the listener understands another.

Similarly, when Ibrahim (AS) says "هَـٰذَا رَبِّى", he may well mean something beyond what his people think he means. He never says "الشَّمْسُ رَبِّى" ("the sun is my lord"). He keeps it ambiguous: "This is my lord. This is greater" (هَـٰذَآ أَكْبَرُ). Greater than what? Than your idols? Than your understanding? The ambiguity is deliberate.

Then, فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَتْ – when it set, he declares: يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ – "O my people! Indeed, I am free of what you associate with Allah!" 

Notice: he was speaking to himself in the earlier statements, but suddenly shifts to addressing his people directly. This confirms that the entire demonstration was for their benefit, not his own spiritual journey. The conversation has been public all along; they've been listening and now he makes his declaration explicit.

translation: So when the night covered him [with darkness], he saw a star. He said, "This is my lord." But when it set, he said, "I like not those that disappear
And when he saw the moon rising, he said, "This is my lord." But when it set, he said, "Unless my Lord guides me, I will surely be among the people gone astray
And when he saw the sun rising, he said, "This is my lord; this is greater." But when it set, he said, "O my people, indeed I am free from what you associate with Allah
Indeed, I have turned my face toward He who created the heavens and the earth, inclining toward truth, and I am not of those who associate others with Allah
commentary:
إِنِّى وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِىَ لِلَّذِى فَطَرَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

"Indeed, I have turned my face completely toward the One who فَطَرَ the heavens and the earth." The verb فَطَرَ means to originate, to fashion with perfect design, to create with inherent purpose and beauty. This is deeper than simple creation; it implies craftsmanship, artistry, intentionality. Earlier in this very surah, we encountered فِطْرَة – the natural disposition Allah ﷻ has placed within humans to recognize Him. Ibrahim (AS) is turning toward the Fatir, the One who fashioned all of creation with that inherent design.

حَنِيفًا – with exclusive devotion, inclining away from all falsehood toward pure truth, with no deviation or distraction. This word captures the essence of Ibrahim's stance: singular focus, unwavering direction, complete dedication.

وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ – "And I am not at all among those who commit shirk." This is emphatic negation. After the demonstration that might have confused some observers, he makes it crystal clear: "I'm not from the mushrikin. I never was. Don't misunderstand what you just witnessed."

This statement is further reinforced by other ayahs in the Quran where Allah ﷻ says about Ibrahim (AS): وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ – "And he was never, at any point, from among those who committed shirk." The past tense construction وَمَا كَانَ emphasizes that this was never his state, not even momentarily. This definitively absolves Ibrahim (AS) from any accusation that he ever worshipped these celestial objects himself.

What we're witnessing is the revival of a legacy. Remember the earlier passage where the Prophet ﷺ was told through the Quran to demonstrate the flawed nature of shirk to the Quraysh? Ibrahim (AS) is doing exactly this with his people. Later, we'll encounter the command: فَبِهُدَىٰهُمُ ٱقْتَدِهْ – "Follow their guidance," referring to the guidance of previous prophets. The Prophet ﷺ is continuing Ibrahim's legacy, using the same methodology to expose the irrationality of associating partners with Allah ﷻ.

translation: And his people argued with him. He said, "Do you argue with me concerning Allah while He has guided me? And I fear not what you associate with Him [and will not be harmed] unless my Lord should will something. My Lord encompasses all things in knowledge; then will you not remember
commentary:
وَحَآجَّهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ ۚ قَالَ أَتُحَـٰٓجُّوٓنِّى فِى ٱللَّهِ وَقَدْ هَدَىٰنِ ۚ وَلَآ أَخَافُ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ رَبِّى شَيْـًٔا ۗ وَسِعَ رَبِّى كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا ۗ أَفَلَا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ

"And his people argued with him." The verb حَاجَّ means to present arguments against, to debate, to try to make a case. His demonstration has provoked a response; now they're arguing back.

He responds: أَتُحَـٰٓجُّوٓنِّى فِى ٱللَّهِ – "Are you going to argue with me about Allah?" Notice he doesn't say رَبِّى ("my Lord") here. Up until now, he's been saying رَبِّى to maintain some ambiguity in his demonstration. But now the ambiguity is lifted completely. He says ٱللَّهِ – the proper name. Not just any رَبّ which could theoretically be misunderstood, but Allah ﷻ Himself. He's revealing what was meant all along: he was always speaking about Allah ﷻ by His name, not about their false deities.

وَقَدْ هَدَىٰنِ – "And He has already guided me." This is crucial. He didn't arrive at this understanding through his own philosophical reasoning, observing the stars, moon and sun. No – وَقَدْ هَدَىٰنِ – "He has already guided me." This was divine revelation, divine guidance. Allah ﷻ had already shown him the truth. The demonstration with the celestial objects was pedagogical, not exploratory. He already possessed the guidance; he was trying to lead them to it.

وَلَآ أَخَافُ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِۦٓ – "And I do not fear what you associate with Him." When Ibrahim (AS) declared that the moon was nothing, that the sun was nothing, that these ancestral deities were nothing – when he dismissed all their false gods – his elders reacted with shock and threats. "Don't say that! The sun will get angry! The moon might harm you! You don't know what these powers will do to you!" They had deep superstitions about angering these cosmic forces.

Ibrahim (AS) addresses this fear directly: I'm not afraid of any of these things you've associated with Allah. إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ رَبِّى شَيْـًٔا – "Unless my Lord wills something." In other words, if anything were to happen to me, it would only be by Allah's will, not because of your false gods. Notice he says رَبِّى ("my Lord") here, affirming his personal relationship with the true Lord while distinguishing Him from their powerless idols.

وَسِعَ رَبِّى كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا – "My Lord encompasses all things in knowledge." His knowledge is complete, total, all-encompassing. Nothing escapes Him, nothing surprises Him, nothing is beyond His awareness.

أَفَلَا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ – "Will you not then remember?" The verb تَتَذَكَّرُونَ is profound here. It's not تَعْلَمُونَ ("learn") or تَفْهَمُونَ ("understand"). It's "remember." To remember, you must have known something before. Ibrahim (AS) is telling them: this truth is already within you. You already have the فِطْرَة – that natural disposition we discussed earlier in this surah. Allah ﷻ fashioned you with this knowledge embedded in your being. You just need to put in the effort to remember it, to uncover it, to return to it.

The connection to فِطْرَة from earlier in the surah is unmistakable: those who possess the remembrance of Allah ﷻ, who have that فِطْرَة awakened within them – they can remember. He's calling them to look inward and reconnect with what they've always known at the deepest level.

translation: And how should I fear what you associate while you do not fear that you have associated with Allah that for which He has not sent down to you any authority? So which of the two parties has more right to security, if you should know
commentary:
وَكَيْفَ أَخَافُ مَآ أَشْرَكْتُمْ وَلَا تَخَافُونَ أَنَّكُمْ أَشْرَكْتُم بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا ۚ فَأَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ أَحَقُّ بِٱلْأَمْنِ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

Ibrahim (AS) now turns their logic back on them with devastating clarity: وَكَيْفَ أَخَافُ مَآ أَشْرَكْتُمْ – "How can I fear what you've associated with Allah?" You expect me to be afraid of these powerless objects?

وَلَا تَخَافُونَ أَنَّكُمْ أَشْرَكْتُم بِٱللَّهِ – "While you don't fear that you've committed shirk with Allah?" You're not afraid of the consequences of associating partners with the Creator of everything?

مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا – "That for which He sent down no authority to you." The word سُلْطَان means authoritative proof, conclusive evidence that comes with power and authority behind it. You have no divine sanction for these beliefs, no revelation, no proof that carries the weight of truth. This connects beautifully to the previous passage where we discussed how Allah ﷻ sends down revelation, sends down guidance, sends down authority for what is true. They possess none of that for their shirk.

فَأَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ أَحَقُّ بِٱلْأَمْنِ – "So which of the two groups is more deserving of security?" The word أَمْن encompasses both physical safety and emotional peace – freedom from external threats and internal anxiety. There's safety from the seen (physical danger) and safety from the unseen (spiritual danger, anxiety about the future, fear of divine punishment). The strongest term combining both dimensions is when we pair إِيمَان (faith) with أَمْن (security). They work together.

Consider the Prophet's ﷺ beautiful dua when the new moon would appear: "O Allah, bring this moon upon us with أَمْن (security), إِيمَان (faith), سَلَامَة (safety) and إِسْلَام (submission)." Notice how أَمْن and إِيمَان are paired – they share the same linguistic root and spiritual connection. True security comes from true faith.

What we learn here is profound: those who commit shirk never have real peace in their lives. They may possess physical security – wealth, homes, assets – but internally, they're never at peace. The أَمْن, that deep internal peace, comes from إِيمَان in Allah ﷻ. When that faith is violated through shirk, internal peace is shattered. You cannot be truly at peace while your soul is in conflict with its فِطْرَة, its natural disposition toward recognizing Allah ﷻ alone.

إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ – "If you truly know." If you're being honest with yourselves, if you're using your intellect, which group actually deserves to feel secure?

translation: They who believe and do not mix their belief with injustice - those will have security, and they are [rightly] guided
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنَهُم بِظُلْمٍ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلْأَمْنُ وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ

The answer comes immediately: ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ – "Those who truly believed" – not superficial belief, but genuine, deep faith. وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنَهُم بِظُلْمٍ – "And did not mix their faith with wrongdoing."

The verb لَبَسَ means to clothe, to cover, but also to mix in, to corrupt, to contaminate. They didn't contaminate their pure faith with ظُلْم (wrongdoing). When the Companions (RA) heard this ayah, they were deeply concerned. Who among us doesn't commit some wrongdoing? Who among us doesn't make mistakes? How can anyone achieve this standard?

The Prophet ﷺ immediately clarified their concern. He explained that ظُلْم in this context specifically refers to شِرْك (shirk). He reminded them of the words of Luqman (AS) to his son: إِنَّ ٱلشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ – "Indeed, shirk is a tremendous wrong" (31:13). The word ظُلْم doesn't always mean shirk, but here, given the context of Ibrahim's dialogue with the mushrikin, it specifically refers to the various forms of shirk they were practicing.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلْأَمْنُ – "Those are the ones for whom there is security." They exclusively possess true أَمْن – real peace, genuine security both in this world and the next. They're the ones who truly own peace, who experience it as a lived reality.

وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ – "And they are the ones who are rightly guided." More than that, they're committed to guidance, continuously guided, holding fast to the path of truth.

This final ayah provides the answer to the question posed in ayah 81. Who truly deserves peace and security? Those who believe purely, without contaminating their faith with shirk. This is where real peace resides.

The relevance of these ayahs extends directly to our times. If you speak to any counselor, any Islamic educator working with the community today, they'll tell you: people are simply not at peace. Personal conflicts, internal turmoil, moral confusion, family breakdowns – these are epidemic. The questions people bring, the struggles they face, all point to a fundamental absence of peace.

The solution isn't found in techniques or programs alone, but in a genuine return to إِيمَان. We need education that focuses on reviving, strengthening and protecting faith. How do you build real iman if you haven't felt it before? How do you strengthen it if it's weak? How do you protect it once you have it? How do you prevent it from becoming corrupted? This is an entire educational agenda in itself.

When you have that peace inside yourself – that أَمْن that comes from true إِيمَان – what naturally flows from you? Peace for others. When people are at peace with themselves, they become sources of peace for those around them: their families, their communities, their societies. The late Dr. Israr Ahmad wrote a profound booklet called "Quran and World Peace," exploring precisely this concept – that peace in the world begins with individuals coming to peace with this deen. Once that internal peace is established through strong iman, it becomes contagious, spreading naturally to others.

This is the path of Ibrahim (AS): standing firm in pure faith, uncontaminated by shirk, and thereby gaining the security and guidance that everyone seeks. His legacy continues through every believer who chooses the same path, finding in it the peace that the world cannot provide and cannot take away.

translation: And that was Our [conclusive] argument which We gave Abraham against his people. We raise by degrees whom We will. Indeed, your Lord is Wise and Knowing
commentary:
**وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ ۚ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ مَّن نَّشَآءُ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ**

This ayah opens with a declaration: "These are Our arguments We had given them to Ibrahim (AS)." The use of "Our arguments" fortifies an essential truth—everything Prophet Ibrahim (AS) was doing in his confrontation with his people was by the dictates of revelation, not merely his own reasoning. The word حُجَّتُنَا means "our case" or "our way of making a case." Allah ﷻ is affirming that these were divine arguments granted to Ibrahim (AS) to use against his nation.

The ayah continues: "We grant multiple ranks. We raise multiple ranks for whoever We want." This demonstrates a principle of divine selection and elevation. Allah ﷻ chooses to raise certain people in status according to His wisdom. The ayah concludes with two of Allah's names: الْحَكِيمُ (the All-Wise) and الْعَلِيمُ (the All-Knowing). These names are particularly meaningful here because they explain why Allah ﷻ elevates whom He wills—His choices are based on perfect wisdom and complete knowledge.

translation: And We gave to Abraham, Isaac and Jacob - all [of them] We guided. And Noah, We guided before; and among his descendants, David and Solomon and Job and Joseph and Moses and Aaron. Thus do We reward the doers of good
commentary:
**وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ كُلًّا هَدَيْنَا ۚ وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِۦ دَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُوسُفَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ**

Allah ﷻ now lists the prophets descended from Ibrahim (AS), beginning with: "We granted him Ishaq and Ya'qub." The word وَهَبْنَا is significant—it means "We granted as a gift." The root word is wahb, which refers to an unusual, extraordinary gift. This choice of word emphasizes that Ishaq (AS) was a miraculous gift to Ibrahim (AS), who was extremely old when his son was born.

This also clarifies the birth order: Ishaq (AS) is mentioned as the younger brother, with Isma'il (AS) being the older son. This has implications for understanding which son was commanded to be sacrificed. The biblical narrative refers to "your only son," but this would refer to Isma'il (AS), the firstborn, not Ishaq (AS).

The ayah then states: "All of them were guided." This encompasses Ishaq, Ya'qub, and their descendants. Then Allah ﷻ mentions Nuh (AS): "And Nuh We had guided from much before." Going back to Ibrahim's offspring, Allah ﷻ lists: Dawud, Sulaiman, Ayyub, Yusuf, Musa and Harun (AS). The ayah concludes: "That is how We reward those who excel" (al-muhsineen—those who do ihsan).

The mention of these prophetic names serves a powerful purpose. The people of the book knew these prophets, so when they heard this recitation, it would send shock waves through them. How does an unlettered man from Makkah know these names? How does he know their lineages, who came from whom? Where did he get this information from? This indirect teaching tells them: We believe in all of these prophets too. This is not some other revelation—this is the same message.

translation: And Zechariah and John and Jesus and Elias - and all were of the righteous
commentary:
**وَزَكَرِيَّا وَيَحْيَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَإِلْيَاسَ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ**

The list continues with Zakariya, Yahya, Isa and Ilyas (AS). The ayah concludes: كُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ—"All of them were from the righteous." This is a beautiful designation. While the previous ayah described them as muhsineen (those who excel), here they are described as saliheen (the righteous). These terms paint a picture of moral and spiritual excellence.

translation: And Ishmael and Elisha and Jonah and Lot - and all [of them] We preferred over the worlds
commentary:
**وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَٱلْيَسَعَ وَيُونُسَ وَلُوطًا ۚ وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ**

The prophetic lineage continues with Isma'il, Al-Yasa', Yunus and Lut (AS). Then comes a remarkable statement: "Each and every one of them, We have given preference and honor over all other nations of the world." The word فَضَّلْنَا means "We preferred" or "We gave preference to." These prophets were honored and elevated above all the nations of their times.

translation: And [some] among their fathers and their descendants and their brothers - and We chose them and We guided them to a straight path
commentary:
**وَمِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ وَإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ ۖ وَٱجْتَبَيْنَـٰهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ**

Allah ﷻ expands the circle: "And from their ancestors, their later offspring, and their own brethren." This acknowledges that prophethood extended through families and communities. Then comes a critical word: وَٱجْتَبَيْنَـٰهُمْ—"We selected them based on their qualifications." The word اجتباء means to select based on merit, to choose those who are qualified. This wasn't random selection; it was based on the goodness within them.

"And We guided them to a straight path"—the sirat mustaqeem. Guidance to the straight path is the ultimate blessing, the culmination of Allah's selection.

translation: That is the guidance of Allah by which He guides whomever He wills of His servants. But if they had associated others with Allah, then worthless for them would be whatever they were doing
commentary:
**ذَٰلِكَ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ يَهْدِى بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ**

"That is the guidance of Allah. He guides by means of it whoever He wants from among His slaves." This reinforces that guidance is divine—Allah ﷻ guides whom He wills from among His servants.

Then comes a stark warning: "And even they"—referring to all these wonderful prophets just mentioned—"had they done shirk, everything they had done, all the good deeds they had accomplished, would have been seized from them, taken away from them." The phrase لَحَبِطَ means "would have been nullified, rendered worthless."

This demonstrates the devastating power of shirk. If prophets—who did more good deeds than anyone—had committed shirk (which of course they never would), all their good deeds would have been multiplied by zero. Worthless. Meaningless. Seized and taken away.

This answers a common question people have today: "I have a friend who's a good person, but he doesn't believe. He does so many good things—don't those good deeds count?" While we shouldn't tell anyone they're going to hell (judgment belongs to Allah ﷻ alone), and we should give dawah in respectful ways, we must understand something fundamental: your friend's good deeds don't compare to the good deeds of prophets. And this ayah teaches that even if prophets had committed shirk, their good deeds would have been worthless.

Our belief doesn't change. The religion acceptable to Allah ﷻ is Islam. Shirk will not be forgiven. This ayah puts everything in perspective—it shows us just how destructive shirk truly is.

translation: Those are the ones to whom We gave the Scripture and authority and prophethood. But if the disbelievers deny it, then We have entrusted it to a people who are not therein disbelievers
commentary:
**أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ ۚ فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيْسُوا۟ بِهَا بِكَـٰفِرِينَ**

"Those are the people We have granted the book to." Allah ﷻ gave them al-kitab (the scripture). "And al-hukm"—this means two things: the ability to make firm decisions and take strong stances, as well as wisdom. "And prophethood (an-nubuwwah)."

Then comes the response to rejection: "But if they disbelieve in it, these people"—meaning the people of Makkah, those hearing this message—"if they're going to disbelieve in all of these ayat, all of these previous prophets, all of what they were given, then We have already prepared a nation that's not going to be disbelievers in them."

The phrase وَكَّلْنَا means "We have put in place, We will install." Allah ﷻ is saying: I've already got another nation ready. I've already prepared another people who won't disbelieve in these prophets and these teachings. The Sahaba in Madinah are waiting. Another group is coming that will carry this message forward.

The answer is clear: You'll be replaced. No big deal. If you reject this, others are ready to accept it.

translation: Those are the ones whom Allah has guided, so from their guidance take an example. Say, "I ask of you for this message no payment. It is not but a reminder for the worlds
commentary:
**أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَبِهُدَىٰهُمُ ٱقْتَدِهْ ۗ قُل لَّآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا ۖ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ**

"Those are the people that Allah guided"—all those prophets mentioned. Then comes the command to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ: فَبِهُدَىٰهُمُ ٱقْتَدِهْ—"Then to their guidance, you better commit."

The word ٱقْتَدِهْ is powerful. The original root is ق د و, which gives us the word muqtadi—one who follows someone in a religious sense. When you follow an imam in salah, you are a muqtadi. The ha at the end (iqtadih instead of iqtadi) is there for emphasis, for empowerment of the verb. It's a rhetorical device telling the Prophet ﷺ: You must commit very strongly to their guidance.

This is telling him: You are on the same legacy as theirs. They were lied against too. They were patient. You hold on to that tradition too. You're on the same chain of prophets, the same mission, the same struggle.

Then the Prophet ﷺ is instructed to say: "Tell them, I don't ask you any compensation in response to my guidance, over what I'm giving to you." This echoes an earlier command in the surah where the Prophet ﷺ was told to say: "I have come to warn you with the Quran and whoever it may reach."

The ayah concludes: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ—"This is nothing but a powerful reminder for all nations, all peoples of the world." The word ذِكْرَىٰ means a reminder, but it carries the weight of something that makes you remember what you already knew deep down, a reminder that awakens consciousness.

The messenger is not appreciated. He's lied against. Yet he's told to stick to the legacy of previous prophets. Earlier in the surah, Allah ﷻ told him: "They're not lying against you. They're denying Allah's ayat." And again: "They didn't appreciate Allah as He deserves to be appreciated." Now at the end of this passage, the same theme returns—the rejection isn't personal. It's part of the prophetic legacy. Hold fast to their example, and continue delivering this universal reminder to all of humanity.

translation: And they did not appraise Allah with true appraisal when they said, "Allah did not reveal to a human being anything." Say, "Who revealed the Scripture that Moses brought as light and guidance to the people? You [Jews] make it into pages, disclosing [some of] it and concealing much. And you were taught that which you knew not - neither you nor your fathers." Say, "Allah [revealed it]." Then leave them in their [empty] discourse, amusing themselves
commentary:
PRIMARY_AYAH: 91

**وَمَا قَدَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِۦٓ إِذْ قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ بَشَرٍ مِّن شَىْءٍ ۗ قُلْ مَنْ أَنزَلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ٱلَّذِى جَآءَ بِهِۦ مُوسَىٰ نُورًا وَهُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ ۖ تَجْعَلُونَهُۥ قَرَاطِيسَ تُبْدُونَهَا وَتُخْفُونَ كَثِيرًا ۖ وَعُلِّمْتُم مَّا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنتُمْ وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِى خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ**

Before examining this powerful ayah, we need to understand its connection to what came before. The previous passage established that the Prophet ﷺ should follow the guidance given to earlier prophets, particularly the way of Ibrahim (AS). Allah ﷻ instructed him to declare that he seeks no compensation for delivering the message—it is merely a reminder for all nations and peoples.

The phrase وَمَا قَدَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِۦٓ (they did not appreciate Allah as He deserves to be appreciated) appears at critical moments throughout the Quran, always in contrast to something already mentioned. Here, the contrast is striking. After being told the messenger seeks no worldly gain and is continuing the legacy of previous prophets, Allah ﷻ reveals the deeper issue: they didn't appreciate Allah as He deserves. When people disregard the messenger, they are actually disregarding Allah ﷻ Himself. This was already explained earlier in the surah when Allah ﷻ told the Prophet ﷺ: "They are not calling you a liar—rather, the wrongdoers are denying the signs of Allah."

The specific manifestation of their failure to appreciate Allah ﷻ comes in their claim: مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ بَشَرٍ مِّن شَىْءٍ (Allah has not sent down anything upon any human being). They dismiss the entire concept of revelation as ridiculous.

Allah ﷻ then commands the Prophet ﷺ with a brilliant response: قُلْ مَنْ أَنزَلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ٱلَّذِى جَآءَ بِهِۦ مُوسَىٰ (Say: Who sent down the Book that came with Musa?). The question immediately raises another: Why bring up Musa (AS)? The Arabs of Makkah weren't particularly invested in Jewish scripture. The answer reveals a remarkable element of this late Makkan revelation.

By this point—some scholars say after nearly twelve years of the prophetic mission—word of the Quranic message had reached Madinah. The Jewish tribes there began crafting sophisticated objections and sending them to the Quraysh, who then posed these questions to the Prophet ﷺ as if they were their own. Allah ﷻ exposes this covert operation by calling out the original source directly. These critics are the very people who claim to follow the Torah, so let them answer: Who sent down the Book that came as نُورًا وَهُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ (light and guidance for the people)?

The ayah then shifts to second person plural—تَجْعَلُونَهُۥ قَرَاطِيسَ (you have made it into scrolls). Allah ﷻ directly addresses the Jewish scholars who hoarded their scripture. The word قَرَاطِيسَ refers to expensive, imported scrolls displayed during religious ceremonies but kept away from common people. They would show these sacred texts like relics, then vault them away where ordinary Jews could not access them. تُبْدُونَهَا وَتُخْفُونَ كَثِيرًا—you display some of it while hiding much.

Then comes a powerful reminder: وَعُلِّمْتُم مَّا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنتُمْ وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ (you were taught what neither you nor your fathers knew). You claim to be the people of literacy and learning, looking down on this "unlettered" messenger. But have you forgotten? You were once ignorant too. You didn't possess this knowledge inherently—it was taught to you through revelation.

قُلِ ٱللَّهُ—Say: Allah. Just answer the question. Who sent it all down? Allah ﷻ.

ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِى خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ (Then leave them in their discourse, playing around). The verb ذَرْ is not ordinary "leaving." In Arabic, تَرَكَ means to leave something, but وَذَرَ carries a sense of contempt—leaving something because it's not worth your time, something disgusting that you don't even want to touch. Leave them to their خَوْض, their thoughtless talk. The word خَوْض originally means to jump into water without thinking, like when someone dares you to leap into a lake and you just do it without considering the depth. To engage in خَوْض الكلام is to plunge into conversation without any real thought, just getting deeper and deeper into meaningless discourse. Let them play around in their apparently deep but actually empty discussions.

translation: And this is a Book which We have sent down, blessed and confirming what was before it, that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it. Those who believe in the Hereafter believe in it, and they are maintaining their prayers
commentary:
**وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مُبَارَكٌ مُّصَدِّقُ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَلِتُنذِرَ أُمَّ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۖ وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ**

In contrast to those empty conversations, Allah ﷻ declares: وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مُبَارَكٌ (this is a Book We have sent down, blessed). The word مُبَارَكٌ comes from بَرَكَة—blessing that increases and multiplies. While those discussions produce nothing, the more this Book is engaged with, the more goodness it generates. The recitation of the Quran itself creates barakah. When it was recited in Makkah, goodness emerged despite fierce resistance. When it was recited in Madinah, it transformed that community. Even today, when we recite Quran in our homes, blessing fills our spaces. The more this Book is discussed, studied and implemented, the more benefit automatically multiplies.

مُّصَدِّقُ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ—it confirms what is before it. This phrase has multiple dimensions. First, it confirms the previous revelations, particularly the Torah that the Jews possess. Second, مَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ (what is between its hands, what is right in front of it) can also refer to the Kaaba, the Qibla that stands before the believers. The Quran confirms the origins and sanctity of this House that the polytheists had taken over.

وَلِتُنذِرَ أُمَّ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا (so that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it). Makkah is أُمَّ ٱلْقُرَىٰ—the mother of all towns, the capital city from which influence radiates. The phrase وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا (those around it) is not geographically restrictive. It includes the vicinity of Makkah, then the bordering territories, then continents beyond, like ripples spreading across a pond. This message began at the center and continues expanding outward. We sit here in North America—a continent largely unknown to the rest of the world at that time—discussing the Book of Allah ﷻ. This is the continuing expansion of مَنْ حَوْلَهَا.

Earlier in this passage, Allah ﷻ said this message is إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَالَمِينَ (nothing but a reminder for all nations). Now He specifies that it starts from the center and spreads to encompass all worlds.

وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ (those who believe in the Hereafter will believe in it). This statement contains a profound truth about what draws people to accept this Book as revelation. Someone genuinely concerned with the afterlife will recognize this as the true faith.

Why? Because certain core beliefs never changed across all revelations. The details of religious practice differed—our fasting is different from what previous prophets were given, our prayer has its unique form—but the fundamentals remained constant. No prophet was given a different version of Tawheed. No prophet taught a different concept of the afterlife. The essential curriculum never changed, though one prophet might have been given more details than another.

What makes our concept of the Hereafter fundamentally different from other religions? Personal responsibility. Multiple faiths have some notion of an afterlife, but many exclude personal accountability. If you belong to the elite group or the "saved" religion, you have no accountability on that Day. Our religion says the opposite: believer or not, you have no guarantees. You must walk the line between hope and fear. Just because you said لَا إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّٰهُ doesn't mean you won't be tested. أَحَسِبَ ٱلنَّاسُ أَن يُتْرَكُوٓا۟ أَن يَقُولُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَهُمْ لَا يُفْتَنُونَ (Do people think they will be left alone because they say "We believe" and they will not be tested?) (29:2)

This is what distinguishes us from corrupted forms of earlier faiths. We believe they were originally taught personal responsibility too, but they removed this essential element from their religion. The fundamental truth that our deeds will be weighed and we are answerable to Allah ﷻ—this was taught to all prophets. When people maintain that essential understanding, this revelation rings true to their conscience.

There are two proofs that bring someone to accept this as revelation. One is previous scripture—the Quran confirms what people already have. The other is the human conscience itself. Allah ﷻ placed in our internal operating system some awareness of the afterlife. This is a psychological proof that we'll explore more deeply when we reach Surah Al-Qiyamah, insha'Allah.

وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ (and they guard their prayers). This reference to salah is significant. According to some scholars, this is a late Makkan revelation, possibly after Isra and Mi'raj when the five daily prayers were established. The Companions would recite the Quran weekly—one-seventh each day. When they reached later surahs like Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, An-Nisa, Al-Ma'idah and Al-An'am, they were reading about refined Islamic law, the Qibla change, the final touches on the religion. Then they would come to Al-An'am and experience a flashback to those early days. This wasn't ancient history for them—it was their own story. "I remember those days," they would think. "We were guarding our prayers, not even knowing the Qibla would change, not knowing all that would come." The message was being refreshed in them with each recitation, bringing those lived experiences back to life.

translation: And who is more unjust than one who invents a lie about Allah or says, "It has been inspired to me," while nothing has been inspired to him, and one who says, "I will reveal [something] like what Allah revealed." And if you could but see when the wrongdoers are in the overwhelming pangs of death while the angels extend their hands, [saying], "Discharge your souls! Today you will be awarded the punishment of [extreme] humiliation for what you used to say against Allah other than the truth and [that] you were, toward His verses, being arrogant
commentary:
**وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ قَالَ أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ وَلَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ شَىْءٌ وَمَن قَالَ سَأُنزِلُ مِثْلَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ فِى غَمَرَٰتِ ٱلْمَوْتِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ بَاسِطُوٓا۟ أَيْدِيهِمْ أَخْرِجُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمُ ۖ ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ ٱلْهُونِ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَكُنتُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ**

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا (Who could be a worse wrongdoer than someone who fabricates lies against Allah?). The word ٱفْتَرَىٰ comes from the root meaning "to make something up," but it specifically refers to lies constructed with malicious intent. Sometimes people tell innocent lies—"The food tastes delicious" when it doesn't. Sometimes lies are told to save yourself—"I was just in the bathroom." But افتراء is different. It's a calculated, detailed lie designed to cause harm and create corruption. It's planned out: How will we construct this lie? How will we spread it?

Allah ﷻ is saying: Could there be anyone more wrong than someone who plots and schemes to fabricate lies against Allah, who says أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ (revelation has been given to me) وَلَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ شَىْءٌ (when nothing has been revealed to him)? Are you really suggesting that this Messenger planned and executed some elaborate scheme to declare himself a prophet? That he fabricated all of this?

But there's another type of criminal equally condemned: وَمَن قَالَ سَأُنزِلُ مِثْلَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ (someone who says "I will bring down the like of what Allah has brought down"). This is the arrogant person who, in response to the messenger, claims: "This revelation you speak of? I could produce something like this in a weekend."

Both are equally criminal—the one who claims to be a messenger and the one who claims they can compete with revelation.

وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ (If you could only see). The word لَوْ typically expresses regret about the past, but when Allah ﷻ uses it, He speaks of future events as if they've already occurred. Why? Because for Allah ﷻ, who owns time, these guaranteed future events are as certain as past events are for us. He already sees what will happen.

إِذِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ فِى غَمَرَٰتِ ٱلْمَوْتِ (when the wrongdoers are in the overwhelming pangs of death). The word غَمَرَات comes from غُمْر, which means unconsciousness or being overcome. In classical Arabic, it's used when someone falls unconscious from intense pain, from the terror of war, or from the overwhelming approach of death. It's also used for someone who passes out from severe intoxication, or for someone so addicted to entertainment or gambling that they're not even themselves anymore—غَمَرَات اللَّهْو, unconscious from entertainment.

These wrongdoers are في غَمَرَاتِ ٱلْمَوْتِ—passing out and waking up repeatedly because of death. Imagine: they pass out and see the Angel of Death, then wake back in worldly life, then pass out again. Each time they lose consciousness, they glimpse what's coming.

وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ بَاسِطُوٓا۟ أَيْدِيهِمْ (and the angels are extending their hands toward them), saying أَخْرِجُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمُ (bring yourselves out, extract yourselves). The imagery is powerful. Your body is like a house you're living in—some philosophers called it a temple. You temporarily inhabit this physical form. Now the Landlord has sent His collection agency: "Come out. You no longer own this. This was temporarily given to you. Your lease is up. Get out."

ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ ٱلْهُونِ (Today you will be recompensed with the punishment of humiliation). The opposite of humiliation is pride, and for someone filled with pride, the worst imaginable fate is humiliation. The punishment is specifically described this way because of what comes at the ayah's end.

بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ (because of what you used to say about Allah without any right). Your continuous false statements against Allah ﷻ.

وَكُنتُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (and you used to show arrogance toward His signs). You were full of pride, displaying yourselves as great. How great are you now? You don't even own your own body anymore. You're being evicted from your own physical form. What happened to all that pride?

translation: [It will be said to them], "And you have certainly come to Us alone as We created you the first time, and you have left whatever We bestowed upon you behind you. And We do not see with you your 'intercessors' which you claimed that they were among you associates [of Allah]. It has [all] been severed between you, and lost from you is what you used to claim
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا فُرَٰدَىٰ كَمَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَتَرَكْتُم مَّا خَوَّلْنَـٰكُمْ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِكُمْ ۖ وَمَا نَرَىٰ مَعَكُمْ شُفَعَآءَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّهُمْ فِيكُمْ شُرَكَـٰٓؤُا۟ ۚ لَقَد تَّقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنكُم مَّا كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ

Allah ﷻ declares with absolute certainty: "You have already come to Us." The past tense here expresses a guaranteed future reality. There is no doubt about it—you will be coming to Us. This is one of those places where the Quran uses past tense to emphasize the certainty of what will happen on Judgment Day.

The ayah continues: فُرَٰدَىٰ - individually, one by one. No family member next to you, no friend beside you, no security detail accompanying you. Every single person will come to Allah ﷻ individually. No one will carry anyone else's burden that Day.

Then comes the powerful reminder: "Just as We created you the first time around." You're returning to Allah ﷻ in the same state of complete dependence you had when you first came into existence.

The expression وَتَرَكْتُم مَّا خَوَّلْنَـٰكُمْ (you have left what We wrapped you up in) is particularly striking. The word خَوَّلَ in Arabic means to give a blessing, but it also means to surround somebody, to wrap someone with a blessing. It's like when you buy someone a blanket and wrap them in it as a gift.

Think about all the layers you lived inside. You were wrapped inside your body. Outside that body, you were wrapped inside your room, inside your home, inside your neighborhood, then your city, then your country or continent, then this earth itself. All of these wrappings, all these gift-wrappings, these layers you used to live within—all of them have been taken off. All those gifts have been removed. Now here you are, exposed for who you really are.

وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِكُمْ (behind your backs) - you have left what you were wrapped up in, what blessings you enjoyed, way behind your backs. So far back.

The questioning intensifies: "And We don't see any of your شُفَعَآء (intercessors)—the ones who were supposedly going to make a case for you, the ones who were going to be with you." Some scholars explain that this is when the angels have taken people out and are bringing them up for questioning, ascending through the heavens. As they're going, the angels ask: "So where are your intercessors? Where are they?"

Others say this questioning is happening on Judgment Day itself, when Allah ﷻ is asking: "We don't see your intercessors, your buddies, your connections. Where did they go? The ones you had such confident assumptions about—that they would be شُرَكَـٰٓؤُا۟ (associates) in your matter, that they would be with you, that they would stand right next to you in the trial. Where are your participants now?"

What has actually happened? لَقَد تَّقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ (a complete chopping has taken place among you). The verb تَقَطَّعَ means to be completely cut off, severed. You've been completely cut off from them.

وَضَلَّ عَنكُم مَّا كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ (everything you used to have such confident assumptions about has all been lost). You can't find any of it now.

translation: Indeed, Allah is the cleaver of grain and date seeds. He brings the living out of the dead and brings the dead out of the living. That is Allah; so how are you deluded
commentary:
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ فَالِقُ ٱلْحَبِّ وَٱلنَّوَىٰ ۖ يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَمُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتِ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ

There is no doubt about it—certainly Allah ﷻ is فَالِقُ (the One who tears open), the terror opener. He tears open الْحَبِّ وَٱلنَّوَىٰ (the seed and the grain). This is about splitting open seeds so that life can emerge from them.

يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ (He extracts the living from the dead). But notice the next phrase: وَمُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتِ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ (and He brings out the dead from the living). There's a grammatical shift here that reveals something beautiful about the precision of Quranic language.

When Allah ﷻ talks about extracting the living, He uses يُخْرِجُ—a verb form associated with motion and action. This makes perfect sense because living things are in motion. But when He talks about the dead, He uses مُخْرِجُ—a noun form that suggests something more constant and still, just like death itself is still and constant.

This linguistic subtlety points to the nature of life and death themselves. Life involves movement, change, growth. Death involves stillness, permanence.

ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ (that is Allah). In other words, you have your own ideas about who Allah ﷻ is, but revelation tells you who Allah ﷻ actually is. This is who He is.

فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ (then how is it that you are turned away?). The word تُؤْفَكُونَ means to be given wrong directions, to be headed the right way but then turned away from it. The mushrikun made this same allegation against the prophets, claiming the messengers came to turn them away from their gods. But Allah ﷻ turns the accusation around: How are you being turned away after getting to know who He really is?

translation: [He is] the cleaver of daybreak and has made the night for rest and the sun and moon for calculation. That is the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing
commentary:
فَالِقُ ٱلْإِصْبَاحِ وَجَعَلَ ٱلَّيْلَ سَكَنًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ حُسْبَانًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ

فَالِقُ ٱلْإِصْبَاحِ—He tears open the morning. The same verb-noun dynamic from the previous ayah continues here. In the last ayah, Allah ﷻ talked about tearing open a seed—this tiny little thing under the ground where a blade of grass emerges that you can barely see. Now He talks about tearing open the entire horizon, the morning itself, where light spreads across the sky. To Him, there is no difference. Whether it's a seed or the sunrise, the scale doesn't matter to Allah ﷻ.

Notice something profound about the grammatical choices here. فَالِقُ (the one who tears) is a noun form—it suggests permanence and constancy. Why? Because imagine for a moment that everyone on earth is dead. Would the morning still rise? Would the sun still rise and set? Yes, absolutely. The sunrise is independent of human existence. It's constant and permanent, which is why Allah ﷻ uses the noun form.

But then the ayah shifts: وَجَعَلَ ٱلَّيْلَ سَكَنًا (and He made the night a means of rest). This uses جَعَلَ—a verb form. Why the change? Because think about it: if everyone on earth is dead, is the night still a means of getting rest? No. The night is only a means of rest for those who are alive.

وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ حُسْبَانًا (and He made the sun and the moon a means of calculation). Again, a verb form. If everyone is dead, is there any calculation happening? Is anyone tracking what day it is, what month, what phase of the lunar calendar? Is anyone observing when the sun sets or when the moon is full? No.

Making the night a time of peace and tranquility, making the sun and moon a means of calculation—these are things that require living beings to experience and utilize them. The things that are independent and permanent don't need us, so they get the noun form. The things that require our action and participation get the verbal form.

These linguistic subtleties are mind-boggling when you begin to see them.

ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (that is the calculation, the precise design, of the Almighty, the All-Knowing).

translation: And it is He who placed for you the stars that you may be guided by them through the darknesses of the land and sea. We have detailed the signs for a people who know
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلنُّجُومَ لِتَهْتَدُوا۟ بِهَا فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ ۗ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ

He is the One who made the stars especially for you, so you can find guidance through them—لِتَهْتَدُوا۟ بِهَا (so that you may be guided by means of them). This ayah becomes particularly significant when we study Surah Al-Waqi'ah later, where Allah ﷻ makes a powerful oath by the stars.

The guidance is specified: فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ (in the darkness of land and ocean). People traveling in the desert preferred to travel at night because the day was scorching hot. But without landmarks, how would they know where they were headed? Their navigation system was the stars. When traveling across the ocean with no reference points around them, what guided sailors? The stars.

The stars are constant, reliable sources of directional information. In Surah Al-Waqi'ah, Allah ﷻ will swear by the stars: "By the positions of the stars—and indeed, if you only knew, it is a mighty oath—this is indeed a noble Quran."

Allah ﷻ calls us to think about the stars and how we navigate using them because this is how He wants us to think about the Quran. A person is in the depth of darkness, traveling through the night, and the only light around them is the stars. Just like that, we're in the depths of darkness in this world, traveling through life, and the only light we have is the light of guidance—the light of revelation.

Just as those stars guided travelers through the darkness of land and sea, the ayat of the Quran guide us through the darkness of this world. Allah ﷻ compares the function of the Quran to the function of the stars in the middle of the night for a traveler, because we're all travelers in the night.

قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (We have already explained the ayat thoroughly for a nation that knows). Notice the shift here: لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (for a people who know) as opposed to لِقَوْمٍ عَالِمِينَ (for a knowing people). The difference is subtle but significant—يَعْلَمُونَ suggests people who want to know, who are actively seeking knowledge.

The word فَصَّلْنَا (We have detailed, explained thoroughly) also indicates that Allah ﷻ Himself explains the ayat. This is the foundation of tafsir—Quran explains Quran. You have to look at other places in the Quran where Allah ﷻ talks about something mentioned here, connecting the ayat together.

translation: And it is He who produced you from one soul and [gave you] a place of dwelling and of storage. We have detailed the signs for a people who understand
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌ ۗ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَفْقَهُونَ

He is the One who created you and spread you about from نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ (one single soul)—referring to Adam (AS).

فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌ—then there is a long-term stay of residence and a place where you're left as a trust. Let me explain these terms with a practical example. Nowadays we have security deposit boxes in banks, but back in those days, they didn't have banks. Instead, people had guarantors. If you were traveling and didn't want to carry your jewelry or valuables with you, you would go to a trustworthy person and say, "I'm traveling, but I don't want to leave my jewelry at home. You have a safe—why don't you keep it for me?" That person would hold it for you as an أَمِين (trustee).

The Prophet ﷺ himself played this role—people trusted him so much that they used to leave their important possessions with him. You might do something similar when you travel and leave your house keys or car keys with a neighbor or relative.

مُسْتَقَرّ is where you stay for a longer period. مُسْتَوْدَع is where something is left temporarily as a trust.

There are three main interpretations of what these two terms refer to:

First, some scholars say the مُسْتَقَرّ (long-term stay) is this worldly life, and the مُسْتَوْدَع (temporary holding place) is the belly of your mother. You're there for just a little while—nine months—and then you're here for a longer term in this world.

Others say the مُسْتَقَرّ is the afterlife and the مُسْتَوْدَع is your grave. Your grave is where you're held for a little while, just like someone holds something temporarily for safekeeping. Later you'll be extracted from it and brought to the afterlife.

A third interpretation suggests that the مُسْتَقَرّ is the afterlife altogether, and the مُسْتَوْدَع is this entire worldly life.

All three interpretations may be valid, but my personal inclination—and Allah ﷻ knows best—is toward either the first one (womb and worldly life) or the second one (grave and Judgment Day). This is because مُسْتَقَرّ is used elsewhere in the Quran to indicate something longer but still ultimately temporary, while مُقَام refers to something truly permanent. In the Quran, when describing Paradise, Allah ﷻ says إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ - حُسْنُ مُسْتَقَرٍّ وَمُقَامٍ. The afterlife is the مُقَام, the truly permanent abode.

قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَفْقَهُونَ (We have already clarified thoroughly the ayat for a nation that seeks to understand). Notice how the ending shifts from يَعْلَمُونَ (those who know) in the previous ayah to يَفْقَهُونَ (those who seek to understand with depth) here.

translation: And it is He who sends down rain from the sky, and We produce thereby the growth of all things. We produce from it greenery from which We produce grains arranged in layers. And from the palm trees - of its emerging fruit are clusters hanging low. And [We produce] gardens of grapevines and olives and pomegranates, similar yet varied. Look at [each of] its fruit when it yields and [at] its ripening. Indeed in that are signs for a people who believe
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ نَبَاتَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهُ خَضِرًا نُّخْرِجُ مِنْهُ حَبًّا مُّتَرَاكِبًا وَمِنَ ٱلنَّخْلِ مِن طَلْعِهَا قِنْوَانٌ دَانِيَةٌ وَجَنَّـٰتٍ مِّنْ أَعْنَابٍ وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلرُّمَّانَ مُشْتَبِهًا وَغَيْرَ مُتَشَـٰبِهٍ ۗ ٱنظُرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ ثَمَرِهِۦٓ إِذَآ أَثْمَرَ وَيَنْعِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكُمْ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ

He is the One who sent water from the sky. Notice something that happens frequently in the Quran: Allah ﷻ will begin with "He" and then in the middle of the ayah switch to "We." Even in English translation, you'll notice this shift happening in many places, particularly when Allah ﷻ mentions water.

The word "We" is an expression of Allah's royalty and majesty. This royal "We" is associated in the Quran especially with water. When you think of royalty—a king—what image comes to mind? A palace. The palace of Allah ﷻ is described as having its foundation over water: His throne is above the water. So when water comes down from the sky, it's an expression of royalty, a reminder of the palace itself, because His throne is built above water.

Water is a grant from the King. It's coming straight from the realm of His throne. It's the same element above which the throne itself exists. It's the source of all purity on this earth and the source of all life on this earth. The water coming from the sky is an expression of Allah's majesty, which is why so often in the Quran when Allah ﷻ talks about water, He switches from the formal "He" to the majestic "We."

So the ayah begins: هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ (He is the One who sent) water from the sky—that's formal. But then: فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ نَبَاتَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ (then We extracted by means of that water the produce of all kinds of things). This is where Allah ﷻ brings Himself closer, emphasizing His direct involvement.

فَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهُ خَضِرًا (then We brought out from it greenery)—خَضِر refers to green shrubs, farmland, plants, the kind of lush greenery that emerges.
