نُّخْرِجُ مِنْهُ حَبًّا مُّتَرَاكِبًا (We extract from it grains that are built up, riding one on top of the other). Think of corn kernels or an ear of peas—each grain sits on top of another, متراكب, stacked or layered.

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّخْلِ مِن طَلْعِهَا قِنْوَانٌ دَانِيَةٌ (and from date palms, from their newly sprouting branches, clusters hanging low). The word طَلْع comes from the rising of the sun—it refers to when a branch barely starts emerging. Then from its طَلْع, from its newly sprouting branch, come قِنْوَان—clusters or bunches.

These are specialized agricultural and botanical terms. قِنْوَان refers to the clusters of dates, while طَلْع is the early emergence. دَانِيَة means hanging low, accessible—these clusters start dangling down even before they've fully matured.

There are other Quranic terms worth noting here: قِنْوَان is used specifically for date palms and grapes, not for other plants. طَلْع can refer to all plants, but قِنْوَان is reserved for dates and grapes in particular.

وَجَنَّـٰتٍ مِّنْ أَعْنَابٍ (and gardens full of grapes) وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلرُّمَّانَ (and olive and pomegranate).

Then comes a fascinating phrase: مُشْتَبِهًا وَغَيْرَ مُتَشَـٰبِهٍ (very similar and not similar at all). They look alike in some ways—they're all fruits, they all have similar structures—yet they're completely different from each other in taste, texture, benefit, appearance.

ٱنظُرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ ثَمَرِهِۦٓ إِذَآ أَثْمَرَ (look towards its fruit as it comes out, as it starts bearing). أَثْمَرَ refers to the very moment fruit starts emerging—when it's just a tiny dot that gradually gets thicker and thicker.

وَيَنْعِهِۦٓ (and its ripening). The word يَنْع begins with a ya, but don't be confused—it's a noun, not a verb. It refers to a mature fruit that becomes solid and perfect. So the ayah is saying: Look at it when it's first born, tiny and emerging, and then look at it when it reaches full maturity.

Now, why is Allah ﷻ talking about fruits here? Connect this back to what came just before. A few ayahs ago, Allah ﷻ was talking about how you'll stay somewhere for a little while and then move on. He talked about how you'll come out of yourselves, how you'll be unraveled, how you'll leave everything you were wrapped in behind.

Now He says: Take a look at some fruits. What is He comparing? The fruit to you. The fruit in its perfect maturity—what's the only thing left for a perfectly mature fruit? To be plucked, to be harvested, and then to be peeled, unraveled. That's all that's left. There's only decline from that point of perfect ripeness.

This is a reflection on our own lives. We grow, we develop, we mature, and we reach our peak. But once we reach that full maturity, the only thing left is to be taken, to return to Allah ﷻ, to be "unraveled" from all these worldly wrappings.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكُمْ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (Indeed, in all of that are multiple miraculous signs for a nation that seeks to believe). The ending shifts again—now it's لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ, for people who believe or who want to believe. Each of these subtle shifts in the endings of these ayat—from "those who know" to "those who understand" to "those who believe"—points to different levels of engaging with Allah's signs.

translation: But they have attributed to Allah partners - the jinn, while He has created them - and have fabricated for Him sons and daughters. Exalted is He and high above what they describe
commentary:
وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ ٱلْجِنَّ وَخَلَقَهُمْ ۖ وَخَرَقُوا۟ لَهُۥ بَنِينَ وَبَنَـٰتٍۭ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ

**They made the jinn partners with Allah ﷻ, while He created them. And they falsely attributed to Him sons and daughters without knowledge. Glory be to Him, and exalted is He above what they describe.**

This ayah addresses one of the most severe forms of shirk practiced by the polytheists: they assigned partners to Allah ﷻ from among the jinn, even though He created them. The absurdity is striking. How can you worship something as a partner to Allah ﷻ when that very thing was created by Him?

The word خَرَقُوا carries profound meaning. The root خرق refers to tearing something apart, ripping it to shreds, or destroying it. It's the kind of tearing that happens when children destroy things, or when you rip something apart in an unnatural, disturbing way. In Arabic, when you do something completely against nature, utterly wrong and disturbing, you use this expression.

They didn't just make a simple claim. They "tore open" or violently distorted reality by attributing sons and daughters to Allah ﷻ. Some attributed daughters to Him, while others attributed sons. All of this was done بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ - without any knowledge whatsoever.

The ayah concludes with a powerful declaration: سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ - Glory be to Him and exalted is He above what they describe. The word يَصِفُونَ is particularly significant. It comes from the root وصف, which means to describe something in elaborate detail. This reveals something about polytheistic cultures: they don't just assign a son or daughter to Allah ﷻ and leave it at that. They create entire mythologies. They paint elaborate pictures, assign special powers, establish dates of birth, create hierarchies of abilities, develop detailed prayers for each deity, and build comprehensive systems around these false gods.

Allah ﷻ is far above all of this elaborate falsehood. He recognizes that significant effort goes into constructing these mythologies, yet He remains completely transcendent above all their descriptions.

translation: [He is] Originator of the heavens and the earth. How could He have a son when He does not have a companion and He created all things? And He is, of all things, Knowing
commentary:
بَدِيعُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَلَمْ تَكُن لَّهُۥ صَـٰحِبَةٌ ۖ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

**Originator of the heavens and the earth. How could He have a son when He does not have a companion? He created all things, and He is Knowing of all things.**

Allah ﷻ now introduces Himself through His creative power. The word بَدِيعُ is significant. You may know the term بدعة in a religious context as something made up with no basis. However, بديع in its original Arabic meaning refers to creative innovation - creating something with absolutely no precedent before you.

When we create something, we always draw from what already exists. A website designer uses templates, color schemes, and design patterns from other websites. Car designers, architects, fashion designers - they all take inspiration from precedents that already exist. We are not capable of بديع in the genuine sense - creation with no precedent whatsoever.

Allah ﷻ had no template to reference when creating mountains, rivers, or human beings. There was no precedent. But بديع means even more than this - it means creating with no ingredients. Nothing was there, and He created. Some philosophers believe that matter was always there and God merely molded it. We believe that matter itself is not eternal. There was nothing, and Allah ﷻ created out of nothing. The only thing eternal is Allah ﷻ Himself.

This understanding is crucial because there can be a divorce between what we learn in various fields of study and what we learn from revelation. If we allow contradictions to exist in our minds without reconciling them, they eventually create contradictions in our hearts. Over time, we may begin to think that the knowledge leading to career success represents "real truth," while revelation becomes merely culturally relevant but not literally true. This is a dangerous path.

The ayah poses a devastating logical question: How could He possibly have an offspring when He doesn't even have a spouse? The very concept is absurd. وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ - He created everything. وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ - and He is the Knower of all things.

translation: That is Allah, your Lord; there is no deity except Him, the Creator of all things, so worship Him. And He is Disposer of all things
commentary:
ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ

**That is Allah ﷻ, your Lord. There is no deity except Him, Creator of all things, so worship Him. And He is Disposer of all things.**

After introducing Himself through His creative power, Allah ﷻ now introduces Himself properly - without shirk. This is the real Allah ﷻ, not what exists in the distorted minds of the polytheists. ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ - That is Allah ﷻ, your Master.

لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - no one is to be worshiped or obeyed in any way, shape or form except He. Then comes خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ - the Creator of everything.

The sequence of phrases here is remarkable. Compare this to Surah Ghafir (40:62): ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - "That is Allah ﷻ, your Lord, Creator of all things. There is no deity except Him." The sequence has been altered. This isn't just a rearrangement of words; it's a rearrangement of entire phrases.

Why? Because context determines emphasis. In Surah Ghafir, when there's a discussion about creation, the phrase "Creator of everything" comes first. Therefore, no one should be worshiped except Him. Here in Surah Al-An'am, the immediate preceding conversation is about denying shirk. Who did they associate with Allah ﷻ? Jinn. What did they attribute to Him? Sons and daughters. The real battle is understanding that you cannot commit shirk with Him. So when He properly introduces Himself, what comes first? لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - the declaration against shirk. Secondarily, He is the Creator of everything.

فَٱعْبُدُوهُ - therefore, enslave yourselves to Him alone. وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ - and He is the One taking care of all things.

The placement of وكيل at the end is deliberate. Why did people associate partners with Allah ﷻ in the first place? Because they believed these partners would handle their day-to-day affairs. They worshiped trees for good agriculture, the sun so crops wouldn't burn, the moon for the tides that brought fish, stars for good fortune and children. They thought specific benefits would come from specific gods. Some traditions took it further, directly worshiping deities of wealth, literally worshiping to get rich.

Allah ﷻ declares that He is Wakil - He will take care of all your affairs. You don't need different departments like in government. You don't need one god for protection, another for good luck, another for children. He handles everything.

translation: Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives [all] vision; and He is the Subtle, the Acquainted
commentary:
لَّا تُدْرِكُهُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ

**Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives all vision. And He is the Subtle, the Aware.**

The word يُدْرِكُ comes from إدراك, meaning to reach or attain. When a child reaches maturity, we say أدرك. It's different from بصر, which means simply to see something. إدراك means your eyes reached something and you fully grasped what it is - complete perception.

Allah ﷻ is telling us that vision doesn't have the ability to fully perceive who He is. This is a profound statement about our limitations. We will only know about Allah ﷻ what we are able to handle. We cannot know everything about Him. He is beyond our perception.

الأبصار also means insight, not just physical vision. He is beyond our philosophical and theological reach. We can only know about Allah ﷻ what He tells us about Himself. Questions about Allah ﷻ that He Himself did not answer remain unanswered because we cannot handle those answers. They are beyond our capacity.

Some people ask all kinds of questions about Allah ﷻ. Most of the time, if someone has a genuine question, they will find the answer in revelation. But people also ask questions that are actually forms of shirk disguised as inquiry - questions that project human attributes onto Allah ﷻ, that assume His qualities work like the qualities we observe in creation. This is fundamentally flawed because لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَىْءٌ - there is nothing like Him.

وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ - but He perceives all vision completely. He fully grasps all perspectives, all points of view.

وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ - and He is the Subtle, the Aware. اللطيف is a special attribute that's difficult to fully capture in translation. Imagine a man with enormous strength and giant muscles. Ask him to press an egg without breaking it. Can he do it? Probably not. When you possess tremendous power, controlling it with precision becomes difficult. A father might pick up his child and throw them playfully in the air, accidentally hitting the ceiling because he didn't realize his own strength.

Allah ﷻ is اللطيف - there is no one more powerful than Him, yet at the same time He possesses complete subtlety and precision. He can execute His power in the most delicate ways. When a tiny seed splits open and a massive tree emerges, that is لطف - subtle, precise power. الخبير means the One who has complete awareness, full knowledge of all things.

translation: There has come to you enlightenment from your Lord. So whoever will see does so for [the benefit of] his soul, and whoever is blind [does harm] against it. And [say], "I am not a guardian over you
commentary:
قَدْ جَآءَكُم بَصَآئِرُ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَنْ أَبْصَرَ فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِىَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۚ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِحَفِيظٍ

**There have certainly come to you enlightenments from your Lord. So whoever will see does so for the benefit of his soul, and whoever is blind does so against it. And I am not a guardian over you.**

بَصَآئِرُ is the plural of بصيرة, which means something you can see and fully understand - observable evidence that leads to comprehension. The Prophet ﷺ was commanded to say, قُلْ هَـٰذِهِۦ سَبِيلِىٓ أَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ - "Say, this is my way; I invite to Allah ﷻ with insight" (12:108). The Quran presents things that can be clearly observed, phenomena you can witness in your own life that serve as proof.

This connects to the central thesis of this surah. The disbelievers kept demanding physical miracles: "We will not believe until we see something extraordinary." Allah ﷻ responds throughout this surah that enough has already been given. The Quran itself is sufficient. The character of the Messenger ﷺ is sufficient. Look at the fruit growing from dead soil. Pay attention to the morning emerging from darkness. Look at yourselves.

قَدْ جَآءَكُم بَصَآئِرُ - enough things for you to see clearly have already come to you. فَمَنْ أَبْصَرَ فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ - whoever chooses to see, it benefits only his own soul. وَمَنْ عَمِىَ فَعَلَيْهَا - whoever remains blind (عمي, meaning literally blind), that blindness only harms himself.

وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِحَفِيظٍ - and I am not a guardian over you. I'm not watching over you to force you to believe. The choice is yours.

translation: And thus do We diversify the verses so the disbelievers will say, "You have studied," and so We may make the Qur'an clear for a people who know
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَلِيَقُولُوا۟ دَرَسْتَ وَلِنُبَيِّنَهُۥ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ

**And thus do We diversify the verses so they will say, "You have studied," and so We may make it clear for a people who know.**

وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ - and that is how We alternate, vary, and diversify the ayat. We teach the same essential lessons over and over again through varying styles and different points of view.

وَلِيَقُولُوا۟ دَرَسْتَ - so that they may say, "You have studied." The word دَرَسْتَ comes from الدرس, which in modern Arabic means "the lesson." But in classical Arabic, it had a more specific meaning related to the method of teaching and learning.

In the past, students didn't have projectors or computer screens. They had a small writing tablet - a board they could write on, then erase, then write again, then erase. Paper was scarce and expensive. There was no ability to save multiple versions. The process of writing, erasing, writing again, erasing again was called الدرس.

The concept suggests a teaching methodology: the teacher writes a lesson, the student learns it, then the teacher erases it and asks, "Do you remember what I taught?" The information is no longer visible, so the student must recall from memory. Then the teacher writes it again, and the process continues. This thorough review and repetition is دَرَسْتَ - you've gone over it thoroughly, you've reviewed it enough times.

The disbelievers were essentially saying, "You've just been studying and reviewing stories from previous scriptures." But Allah ﷻ says this repetition and variation serves a greater purpose: وَلِنُبَيِّنَهُۥ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ - so that We may make it thoroughly clear for a people who want to know, who seek knowledge.

translation: Follow, [O Muhammad], what has been revealed to you from your Lord - there is no deity except Him - and turn away from those who associate others with Allah
commentary:
ٱتَّبِعْ مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

**Follow what has been revealed to you from your Lord. There is no deity except Him. And turn away from those who associate others with Allah ﷻ.**

ٱتَّبِعْ مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ - follow whatever has been revealed to you from your Lord. This raises an important question: when this command was given, much of the Sharia had not yet been revealed. Specific rulings about fasting, detailed halal and haram foods, many aspects of worship - these would come later. Some would come in this very surah, but most had not yet been revealed.

So what exactly is being commanded to follow? This question will be explored as we continue through the surah, but it points to something profound: following revelation isn't merely about following a list of legal rulings. It encompasses something deeper - following the guidance, the worldview, the understanding of tawhid that has been revealed.

لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - there is no deity except Him. This declaration of tawhid is reinforced once again.

وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ - and turn away from the polytheists. When they argue, when they make up falsehoods, when they run their mouths with nonsense - don't let it bother you. Ignore them. Don't let it get under your skin. This is a command for emotional resilience in the face of opposition, a reminder that the Messenger ﷺ isn't responsible for forcing belief, only for delivering the message with clarity.

translation: But if Allah had willed, they would not have associated. And We have not appointed you over them as a guardian, nor are you a manager over them
commentary:
وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَآ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۗ وَمَا جَعَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِوَكِيلٍ

This ayah addresses a critical spiritual reality that the Prophet ﷺ needed to understand deeply. The previous passage instructed him to turn away from those engaged in shirk. Now Allah ﷻ explains the wisdom behind this command: "Had Allah willed, they would not have committed shirk."

This statement addresses the Prophet's ﷺ natural concern for his people. As they became more bent upon disbelief, he grew increasingly worried. Perhaps he wasn't doing enough. Perhaps if they were shown the right proof or miracle, they would finally accept. But Allah ﷻ clarifies that their persistence in shirk isn't due to any shortcoming in the message or the messenger. These people are determined in their rejection, and no amount of additional evidence will change hearts that have chosen to remain closed.

The ayah continues: "And We have not made you a guardian over them" (حَفِيظًا). The word حَفِيظ refers to a watchful entity, someone responsible for monitoring and controlling others. Allah ﷻ is telling the Prophet ﷺ that this isn't his role. His responsibility is to deliver the message clearly, not to force acceptance or bear the burden of their choices.

Similarly, "nor are you a disposer of their affairs" (وَكِيل) emphasizes that the Prophet ﷺ is not responsible for managing their outcomes or making decisions on their behalf. A وَكِيل is someone who takes care of another's matters. The Prophet's ﷺ job description doesn't include controlling the hearts and choices of others—that authority belongs exclusively to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And do not insult those they invoke other than Allah, lest they insult Allah in enmity without knowledge. Thus We have made pleasing to every community their deeds. Then to their Lord is their return, and He will inform them about what they used to do
commentary:
وَلَا تَسُبُّوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَسُبُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَدْوًۢا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ زَيَّنَّا لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ عَمَلَهُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِم مَّرْجِعُهُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

While the previous ayah addressed the Prophet ﷺ directly, this one turns to the Muslim community. Allah ﷻ commands: "Do not curse those whom they call upon besides Allah."

This instruction is particularly significant given the audience. Many of the Muslims being addressed had themselves practiced shirk before accepting Islam. Having experienced that spiritual darkness firsthand, they might feel they have earned the right to speak harshly about the false beliefs they once held. Yet even they are told to restrain themselves.

The phrase الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ can be understood in two ways: don't curse those who call upon other than Allah (the people themselves), and don't curse what they call upon (their false gods). Both meanings apply. This prohibition extends to insulting their idols, their practices, and the people engaged in these practices.

Understanding the word سَبّ helps clarify this command. The root س-ب-ب refers to vile, filthy, offensive language—shameless cursing that provokes consequences. It's the kind of speech that causes things to happen, that becomes a سَبَب (cause) for further harm and division.

But why would Allah ﷻ prohibit criticizing falsehood, especially when He Himself condemns it throughout the Quran? The answer lies in understanding the weapons of truth versus the weapons of falsehood. Truth relies on evidence, proof, reason, and clear argument. Falsehood, on the other hand, relies on emotional manipulation, intimidation, insults, and distraction.

When believers resort to cursing and insults, they abandon the superior weapons that truth provides and pick up the inferior tactics of falsehood. This shift signals weakness—it suggests that rational arguments aren't strong enough, so emotion must compensate. For those paying attention, resorting to insults and aggression is itself proof that the argument has been lost.

We see this dynamic clearly in the story of Musa (AS) and Firaun. Throughout their exchanges, Musa (AS) maintains rational discourse and presents evidence. When Firaun exhausts his ability to respond intellectually, he switches to insults, intimidation, and threats—revealing to perceptive observers that he has already lost the debate.

The ayah warns of the consequence of violating this principle: "lest they insult Allah in enmity without knowledge." When Muslims attack the false beliefs of others aggressively, the natural human response isn't reflection—it's defensive reaction. People don't suddenly examine their beliefs objectively when those beliefs are insulted; instead, they double down on them and lash out.

This defensive response often manifests as hatred toward Allah ﷻ—not based on knowledge or understanding, but purely as an emotional reflex. The word عَدْوًا (enmity or animosity) captures this reactive hostility. And it happens بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ—without any real knowledge or understanding, just blind emotional retaliation.

This principle extends beyond religious discourse. In times of conflict, nationalism intensifies. During wars or sports tournaments, people suddenly express fierce love for their nation. But when there's no external threat, those same people criticize their country's flaws and problems. The "love" of nation is often just a mask for hatred of others. Don't provoke that dynamic, Allah ﷻ warns, because what seems like love for their beliefs is really hatred for yours, and they'll respond by directing that hatred toward Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with a sobering reminder: "Thus have We made the deeds of every nation seem pleasing to them." Every community, every culture, every nation loves their own way of doing things. Allah ﷻ has made this beautification part of human nature. People are attached to their traditions and practices, and attacking those attachments directly only strengthens them.

"Then to their Lord is their return, and He will inform them of what they used to do." Ultimate accountability rests with Allah ﷻ. The believers' job isn't to punish or humiliate those in error—it's to present truth clearly and respectfully. Justice and consequences belong to Allah ﷻ alone.

translation: And they swear by Allah their strongest oaths that if a sign came to them, they would surely believe in it. Say, "The signs are only with Allah." And what will make you perceive that even if a sign came, they would not believe
commentary:
وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ لَئِن جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَةٌ لَّيُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهَا ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَا يُشْعِرُكُمْ أَنَّهَآ إِذَا جَآءَتْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

The narrative returns to address the mushrikeen's persistent demands for miracles. "They swear by Allah their strongest oaths"—the Arabic captures the intensity of their claims. أَقْسَمُوا indicates they took formal oaths, while جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ describes oaths taken with maximum force and public display.

Understanding يَمِين (oath) requires recognizing its connection to the right hand. In Arab culture, extending your right hand in an oath meant staking your honor and dignity. You were essentially saying: "I place my reputation as collateral. If I don't fulfill this pledge, I have no dignity left." These were the politicians and leaders of Quraysh coming forward publicly to declare: "We stake our careers on this. If a miracle comes, we will absolutely believe."

Their demand was clear: "If only a sign would come to them, they would surely believe in it." Show us an angel descending, treasures emerging from the earth, or a physical book coming down from the sky—give us something tangible and we'll believe. They swore to it repeatedly, emphatically, putting their reputations on the line.

Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ: "Say: The signs are only with Allah." The construction إِنَّمَا indicates exclusivity—miraculous signs belong to Allah ﷻ alone. This isn't something you can order on demand. The timing, nature, and purpose of signs are entirely in His control, not subject to human requirements or negotiations.

Then comes a profound question directed at the believers: "And what would make you perceive that when it comes, they will not believe?" The phrase وَمَا يُشْعِرُكُمْ carries the sense of "What will make you understand? How can I get this through to you?"

The believers, witnessing these dramatic oaths, might think: "These people seem sincere. They're swearing so intensely—surely if Allah ﷻ shows them a miracle, they'll believe." But Allah ﷻ is telling them: No matter how convincing their oaths seem, no matter how much emotional manipulation they employ, understand this truth—when the miracle comes, they still won't believe. Don't let their theatrical performances deceive you.

translation: And We will turn away their hearts and their eyes just as they refused to believe in it the first time. And We will leave them in their transgression, wandering blindly
commentary:
وَنُقَلِّبُ أَفْـِٔدَتَهُمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ كَمَا لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَنَذَرُهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ

This ayah reveals a spiritual and psychological reality that explains why additional miracles won't help those who have already rejected the truth. "And We will turn away their hearts and their eyes"—the Arabic نُقَلِّبُ indicates alteration, a turning or changing of their essential faculties.

To understand this, consider how physical muscles work. When you exercise regularly, your muscles grow stronger and healthier. But when a muscle isn't used for an extended period—when someone is bedridden for months or blindfolded for a long time—that muscle weakens progressively until it can eventually die. A bedridden person can lose the ability to walk. Someone blindfolded long enough risks permanent blindness.

The spiritual faculties Allah ﷻ placed within us function similarly. The قَلْب (heart) and the ability to perceive truth are spiritual muscles. When revelation first came to the mushrikeen, they had the capacity—the قُدْرَة (ability) and potential—to recognize the truth. Allah ﷻ had placed within them the فِطْرَة, the natural disposition to acknowledge and accept truth when they encounter it.

But what happens when you consistently refuse to exercise that capacity? When truth is presented day after day, and you deliberately turn away each time, that spiritual muscle weakens. Keep denying, keep rejecting, and eventually that ability dies completely. The capacity to recognize, appreciate, and accept truth can be permanently lost through persistent rejection.

The word أَفْئِدَة (plural of فُؤَاد) is significant here. Unlike قَلْب, which refers to the heart generally, فُؤَاد describes the passionate heart, the heart that becomes overwhelmed and deeply moved when encountering something powerful and true. Allah ﷻ is saying He will turn even this—the deepest capacity for spiritual perception.

The cause is stated clearly: كَمَا لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ—"as they did not believe in it the first time." The كَمَا here indicates causation: as a consequence of them not believing initially, as a result of their rejecting the truth when it first came. They didn't exercise that spiritual muscle when they had the chance, and now it has atrophied beyond recovery.

"And We will leave them in their transgression, wandering blindly." First came the refusal to see what was clearly around them—they closed their eyes to manifest truth. This killed the first spiritual muscle. Then, when that capacity died, eventually the heart's ability to perceive truth died as well. طُغْيَان refers to transgression and rebellion, while يَعْمَهُونَ describes blindness—specifically عَمَى, the blindness of the heart.

Earlier in Surah Al-An'am, we read that whoever becomes blind spiritually, that blindness is counted against them. Now we're told: they will wander blindly—first their eyes became incapable of seeing truth, and then their hearts became blind. Which is worse? Blindness of the eyes or blindness of the heart? The latter is far more devastating, and this is the terminal point they've reached.

translation: And even if We had sent down to them the angels [with the message] and the dead spoke to them [of it] and We gathered together every [created] thing in front of them, they would not believe unless Allah should will. But most of them, [of that], are ignorant
commentary:
۞ وَلَوْ أَنَّنَا نَزَّلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ وَكَلَّمَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَحَشَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ قُبُلًا مَّا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَجْهَلُونَ

This ayah takes the argument to its ultimate conclusion. Even if Allah ﷻ were to grant every single demand they've made—and more—it wouldn't result in belief. "And even if We had sent down the angels to them, and the dead spoke to them, and We gathered before them everything face to face..."

The word قُبُلًا can be understood in two ways. It can mean "face to face"—bringing everything directly before them so they can witness it personally, removing any excuse about not seeing for themselves. It can also mean bringing forth everything from the past—all the historical events they claim to doubt, all the prophets and testimonies they say they would believe if they could just witness them—Allah ﷻ could make all of it present and tangible.

The construction مَّا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ is emphatic: "they would NOT have believed." This isn't uncertainty—it's a definitive statement. No matter what miraculous evidence is provided, belief won't result from it.

"Except that Allah should will"—إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ reminds us that ultimately, all belief comes from Allah's will. If He wanted to force belief, He could. But that's not how He has designed this test. The whole purpose of this earthly life is to see who will choose to believe when given free will and sufficient evidence, not to force belief through overwhelming supernatural displays.

"But most of them are ignorant." The word يَجْهَلُونَ (from جَهَالَة) is often translated as "ignorant," but it carries a deeper meaning. It refers to acting emotionally, impulsively, without engaging the intellect. Earlier, even the Prophet ﷺ was cautioned وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْجَاهِلِينَ—don't become too emotional in your judgment.

These people have abandoned intellectual engagement entirely. They haven't exercised their capacity for reason and reflection, so that faculty has died. All they have left is جَهَالَة—emotional impulse and reactive behavior. They don't want truth; they want to justify their preexisting positions. No amount of evidence will penetrate that barrier because they're not engaging the part of themselves that could process and accept evidence. They're operating purely on emotion and ego.

translation: And thus We have made for every prophet an enemy - devils from mankind and jinn, inspiring to one another decorative speech in delusion. But if your Lord had willed, they would not have done it, so leave them and that which they invent
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا لِكُلِّ نَبِىٍّ عَدُوًّا شَيَـٰطِينَ ٱلْإِنسِ وَٱلْجِنِّ يُوحِى بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ زُخْرُفَ ٱلْقَوْلِ غُرُورًا ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّكَ مَا فَعَلُوهُ ۖ فَذَرْهُمْ وَمَا يَفْتَرُونَ

This ayah shifts to provide comfort and context to the Prophet ﷺ. "And thus We have made for every prophet an enemy." Allah ﷻ Himself established this as part of the prophetic mission. When the Prophet ﷺ faces fierce opposition, he should remember: I placed these enemies before you intentionally.

There's a wisdom to this arrangement. The Arabic proverb says: "Whoever fears the climbing of the high mountain will never experience the beautiful heights." Messengers weren't given easy tasks precisely because they were capable of difficult ones. They faced the worst of people, the hardest challenges, so that if guidance could reach even the most obstinate hearts, their followers would know that any challenge could be overcome.

But notice how Allah ﷻ describes these enemies: شَيَـٰطِينَ ٱلْإِنسِ وَٱلْجِنِّ—"devils from among mankind and jinn." He doesn't simply say "enemies who are human and jinn." He specifically identifies them as devils. There's a critical distinction here between being a disbeliever and becoming an active enemy of the prophetic mission.

Many people in Makkah were non-Muslims throughout the Prophet's ﷺ time there. Some were indifferent, some disagreed but remained civil, some even eventually came to Islam after the conquest. When Makkah was finally opened to the Muslims, people approached the Prophet ﷺ saying, "I never really paid attention before. Can someone recite the Quran to me now? I want to consider it seriously." The Prophet ﷺ didn't turn them away or say, "Where were you for 23 years?" On the contrary, the Quran itself instructs in Surah At-Tawbah that if a mushrik seeks safe passage to hear the Quran, grant it to them. Take them to a secure place where they won't feel intimidated or coerced, and let them hear the word of Allah ﷻ and make their own decision.

Being a disbeliever (كَافِر) or even a polytheist (مُشْرِك) doesn't automatically make someone an enemy of the Prophet ﷺ. But to become a شَيْطَان—a devil—means making opposition to Islam your life's mission. These are people whose purpose becomes: destroy this message, undermine this messenger, prevent people from accessing this guidance. That transformation from mere disbelief to active enmity is what's being described here.

The collaboration between these devils is emphasized: يُوحِى بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ—"they inspire one another." The word يُوحِى (from وَحْي) normally refers to divine revelation, but it can mean any form of subtle communication or inspiration. These enemies coordinate, share strategies, and reinforce each other's efforts.

What do they share? زُخْرُفَ ٱلْقَوْلِ—"adorned speech" or "beautified speech." The word زُخْرُف relates to زُخْرُفَة, which means decoration and beautification. When a building has an attractive facade, that's زُخْرُفَة. When speech is made to sound appealing and convincing through rhetorical techniques, that's زُخْرُفَ ٱلْقَوْلِ.

The modern term for this is propaganda. They spread beautiful-sounding but deceptive speech—غُرُورًا (delusion, deception). Political rhetoric, hateful discourse dressed up in reasonable-sounding language, manipulation through eloquence—all of this falls under زُخْرُفَ ٱلْقَوْلِ غُرُورًا.

"And if your Lord had willed, they would not have done it." Once again, Allah ﷻ reminds us of His ultimate control. These enemies exist by His permission, as part of the test. "So leave them and what they invent"—فَذَرْهُمْ وَمَا يَفْتَرُونَ. The command is clear: don't exhaust yourself trying to stop their propaganda or respond to every false accusation. Leave them to their fabrications. Truth will prevail through its own strength, not through endless battles against every lie.

translation: And [it is] so the hearts of those who disbelieve in the Hereafter will incline toward it and that they will be satisfied with it and that they will commit that which they are committing
commentary:
وَلِتَصْغَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَفْـِٔدَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَلِيَرْضَوْهُ وَلِيَقْتَرِفُوا۟ مَا هُم مُّقْتَرِفُونَ

This ayah explains another dimension of why this propaganda and beautified false speech exists. "And so that the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter will incline toward it."

The word تَصْغَىٰ comes from a root meaning to incline or lean toward something with interest and desire. It describes more than casual interest—it's the inclination of someone who becomes obsessed, who seeks out and craves something. In classical Arabic, a صَغَا referred to a devoted group or clique that surrounded someone, always wanting to be near them, seeking their attention and approval.

Allah ﷻ is saying that those who don't believe in the afterlife—who have no sense of ultimate accountability—their hearts will be drawn to this false, beautified speech. They'll become fans of it, obsessed with it, forming communities around it.

"And so they may be pleased with it"—وَلِيَرْضَوْهُ. They won't just tolerate it; they'll be completely satisfied with this message of propaganda and deception. It will fulfill something they're looking for, some need or desire that truth doesn't satisfy for them.

"And so they may commit whatever they are committing"—وَلِيَقْتَرِفُوا۟ مَا هُم مُّقْتَرِفُونَ. The word اِقْتِرَاف is significant. While كَسْب means to earn something generally, اِقْتِرَاف specifically refers to earning through criminal or illegitimate means. If كَسْب is honest work, اِقْتِرَاف is robbery, fraud, or corruption.

There's even an expression in Arabic legal terminology: التَّوْبَةُ قَبْلَ الْقُدْرَةِ تُسْقِطُ الْحَدَّ—"Repentance before being caught nullifies the punishment." If someone committed a crime but turns themselves in before anyone was even looking for them, their sincere repentance before being discovered can nullify the legal consequences. We saw this principle reflected in Surah Al-Ma'idah regarding those who repent before being apprehended.

Here, اِقْتِرَاف describes the continuous accumulation of sins and crimes against their own souls. The phrase مَا هُم مُّقْتَرِفُونَ—"whatever they are committing"—indicates ongoing, habitual earning of sins. They're not just making isolated mistakes; they're building entire lives and systems around earning wrongdoing, piling crimes upon themselves continuously.

This is why the propaganda appeals to them: it gives them permission and justification for the sins they want to commit. It provides intellectual cover for their desires and validates their choices. The beautified false speech tells them what they want to hear, so they embrace it eagerly and use it to justify whatever sins they were determined to commit anyway.

translation: [Say], "Then is it other than Allah I should seek as judge while it is He who has revealed to you the Book explained in detail?" And those to whom We [previously] gave the Scripture know that it is sent down from your Lord in truth, so never be among the doubters
commentary:
أَفَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْتَغِى حَكَمًا وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مُفَصَّلًا ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُۥ مُنَزَّلٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ

After describing the propaganda machine arrayed against the truth, Allah ﷻ now instructs the Prophet ﷺ on how to respond. "Should I seek other than Allah as a judge?" The question itself is rhetorical—the answer is obviously no. A حَكَم is someone who makes decisions, who arbitrates and determines what is right and wrong, true and false.

The mushrikeen wanted to make their own standards the judge: "Show us what we demand, meet our conditions, and then we'll believe." But Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ﷺ to reject this framework entirely. The only judge of what constitutes sufficient evidence, the only arbiter of truth, is Allah ﷻ Himself.

"While it is He who has sent down to you the Book detailed"—مُفَصَّلًا. The word مُفَصَّل indicates something thoroughly explained and detailed. On one level, it describes the Book itself: every ruling, every principle, every story is presented with the precise level of detail needed. Nothing essential is missing, nothing crucial is left vague.

On another level, مُفَصَّلًا describes how the revelation was sent down—gradually, in response to specific situations, providing exactly what was needed when it was needed. This methodical, contextualized revelation is itself a form of تَفْصِيل—detailed, careful exposition.

The ayah continues: "And those to whom We gave the Scripture know that it is sent down from your Lord with truth." This refers to the People of the Book—Jews and Christians—who have their own scriptures. Particularly, this refers to their scholars who understand their own texts deeply.

These knowledgeable people among the People of the Book recognize the Quran as genuine revelation. They've studied their own scriptures extensively; they know what revelation sounds like, how it functions, what characteristics it bears. When they encounter the Quran honestly, they recognize it as the same type of speech—revelation from the Lord with حَقّ (truth, purpose, and reality).

This isn't just a theoretical claim. Throughout the Prophet's ﷺ life, numerous Jewish and Christian scholars who encountered the Quran recognized it as revelation. Some, like Salman Al-Farsi (RA) and Waraqah ibn Nawfal (RA), explicitly affirmed this. The ayah states that those with real knowledge of scripture—not those blinded by political concerns or attachment to their positions—they know this is revealed truth.

The ayah concludes with a command to the Prophet ﷺ: "So do not be among the doubters." فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ means don't let their propaganda, their demands, their theatrical oaths and protests cause you to doubt. The word مُمْتَرِين comes from مِرْيَة, which means doubt or uncertainty.

This command serves multiple purposes. On one level, it's reassurance to the Prophet ﷺ: You have every reason to be certain. The revelation is detailed, those with knowledge recognize it as truth, and Allah ﷻ is the only judge whose standard matters. Don't let their noise create uncertainty in you.

On another level, this command will be heard by the Muslims and the mushrikeen alike. For Muslims, it reinforces that certainty is possible and appropriate—you're not being unreasonable or close-minded by having conviction in this message. For the mushrikeen, it demonstrates the Prophet's ﷺ unshakeable confidence. He's not plagued by the doubts they try to instill. His certainty is rooted in something far deeper than their propaganda can reach.

translation: And the word of your Lord has been fulfilled in truth and in justice. None can alter His words, and He is the Hearing, the Knowing
commentary:
وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقًا وَعَدْلًا ۚ لَّا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

The word of your Master has already been completed - صِدْقًا وَعَدْلًا - in terms of being true and in terms of being a declaration of fairness. It is already complete.

This concept of completion requires careful understanding. In Surah Al-Ma'idah, the surah right before this one chronologically (though much later in revelation), we read: "Today I have perfected your religion for you, completed My favor upon you, and chosen Islam as your religion" (5:3). The religion itself and its instructions and injunctions were completed at that later stage. However, ideologically - when it comes to being a message of truth and a message of justice - that dimension of the religion was already complete from the beginning.

There are two senses of the religion's completeness. There is a practical sense where the religion is fully established, where the Kaaba is cleansed, where the halal is promoted and the haram is prohibited. That practical completeness came later. But in the ideological sense, in the knowledge sense, the religion has been complete from the very beginning. In terms of truth and justice, it has always been complete.

لَّا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ - There is absolutely no power capable of changing any of His words. No one can alter whatsoever any of His words. وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ - And He is the All-Hearing, All-Knowing.

translation: And if you obey most of those upon the earth, they will mislead you from the way of Allah. They follow not except assumption, and they are not but falsifying
commentary:
وَإِن تُطِعْ أَكْثَرَ مَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يُضِلُّوكَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ

This ayah addresses the modern problem of democracy and majority rule. The contemporary idea suggests that majority rule is inherently right, and there is a push to spread democracy across the world based on this premise. Interestingly, modern political rhetoric often ties democracy with strong religious language, which creates an irony - if we claim the majority is always right, wasn't it the minority who followed the prophets? The majority rejected them.

There is also a hypocritical element in how "majority" is defined. If the majority aligns with our beliefs, then it is considered good democracy. But if the majority agrees to something contrary to our views, suddenly it cannot be right.

Allah ﷻ addresses this not from a political standpoint, but from a guidance perspective. وَإِن تُطِعْ أَكْثَرَ مَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يُضِلُّوكَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - If you were to follow most of whoever is on the earth, they will misguide you away from the path of Allah ﷻ. This is an if-then statement: they will misguide you. The majority is not a precedent, not something reliable when it comes to guidance.

إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ - Most people are only following nothing but assumption (dhann). This is more provable to us now than ever before. Consider how markets operate: there is a debt crisis or economic uncertainty somewhere, fear about oil supplies, and immediately - based purely on assumption about what might happen - stocks rise and fall. Stock analysts and traders watch the news rather than actual business fundamentals. They watch assumptions about what might or might not happen, and entire economies shift based on these speculations.

When central banking authorities release policy statements - just a few words about interest rates or economic outlook - markets can surge or crash. No proof, no evidence, just words. إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ - they follow nothing but assumption.

وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ - And they are doing nothing but making things up. The verb يَخْرُصُونَ comes from خَرْص (khars), which refers to making precise calculations without any actual basis. The classical example describes someone looking at a palm tree and declaring it has exactly 387.3 dates on it - a very precise calculation, but complete fabrication. This describes people who produce statistics and figures, none based on anything provable. "80% of people say..." "95% of people who eat this cereal will never have heart attacks..." These are manufactured facts, assumptions presented as certainty.

translation: Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who strays from His way, and He is most knowing of the [rightly] guided
commentary:
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ مَن يَضِلُّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۖ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ

Your Master - in fact, He knows best who is going to go astray from His path, who does go off His path. وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ - And He knows best who is truly committed to the path of guidance, who are genuinely committed to following guidance.

translation: So eat of that [meat] upon which the name of Allah has been mentioned, if you are believers in His verses
commentary:
فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا ذُكِرَ ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ إِن كُنتُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦ مُؤْمِنِينَ

Then consume from what Allah's ﷻ name has been pronounced upon, if in fact you truly believe in His ayat.

This marks the beginning of what can be understood as a cultural war. Most rulings about halal and haram were not revealed until Madinah, but some major prohibitions came down even in Makkah. One was riba (usury), mentioned in Surah Ar-Rum, one of the few prohibitions that came in Makkan revelation. Another prohibition mentioned in the Quran is regarding animals slaughtered in the names of false gods.

This was a declaration that even the food consumed in the name of shirk is haram for us. The Muslims could not even share a plate with those who practiced this anymore. We must understand the context: at this point, Muslims were not yet a separate community. They lived in mushrik households, secretly meeting with the Prophet ﷺ before returning to their families. They were not yet a separate entity as they would become in Madinah with their own court system and structure. They were living among everyone else. And now Allah ﷻ was saying: you cannot even eat with them. The cultural separation had begun.

إِن كُنتُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦ مُؤْمِنِينَ - If in fact you believe in His signs, in His ayat, then this is required of you.

translation: And why should you not eat of that upon which the name of Allah has been mentioned while He has explained in detail to you what He has forbidden you, excepting that to which you are compelled. And indeed do many lead [others] astray through their [own] inclinations without knowledge. Indeed, your Lord - He is most knowing of the transgressors
commentary:
وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا ذُكِرَ ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ وَقَدْ فَصَّلَ لَكُم مَّا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِلَّا مَا ٱضْطُرِرْتُمْ إِلَيْهِ ۗ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا لَّيُضِلُّونَ بِأَهْوَآئِهِم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ

What would be wrong with you that you would not eat from what Allah's ﷻ name has been pronounced upon? This refers to certain traditions of the mushrikeen. Earlier in this surah, we discussed how they would make elaborate explanations and detailed mythologies - they had a complex set of rules about what they could and could not eat. From the Muslim perspective, there were animals they claimed could not be slaughtered in Allah's ﷻ name. They had developed these policies.

Allah ﷻ asks: What is wrong with you? Why would you not eat from what Allah's ﷻ name has been pronounced upon? وَقَدْ فَصَّلَ لَكُم مَّا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ - And it has already been explained to you in detail what has been made forbidden upon you. إِلَّا مَا ٱضْطُرِرْتُمْ إِلَيْهِ - Except in cases where you are compelled, meaning when you are close to death from starvation.

وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا لَّيُضِلُّونَ بِأَهْوَآئِهِم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ - But the fact is, a huge multitude mislead others by means of their false and empty desires, without any actual factual knowledge. إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ - No doubt about it, your Master - He is the One who knows best regarding those who transgress.

This passage addresses a fundamental connection: what we consume is directly connected to our spiritual health. The discussion of iman and the ability to recognize truth is interwoven with this cultural declaration about not eating their food, and the beginnings of halal and haram food laws. While some people become obsessively compulsive about these issues, there is genuine reason to take it seriously. There is reason to take halal and haram seriously, because it is tied directly into the ayat of iman, into iman itself, and into avoiding shirk.

translation: And leave what is apparent of sin and what is concealed thereof. Indeed, those who earn [blame for] sin will be recompensed for that which they used to commit
commentary:
وَذَرُوا۟ ظَـٰهِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَبَاطِنَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْسِبُونَ ٱلْإِثْمَ سَيُجْزَوْنَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَقْتَرِفُونَ

Abandon the outward dimension of sin and the inward dimension of it. Allah ﷻ is addressing shirk in this context - the apparent shirk and the hidden shirk. Outside, a person might claim to worship Allah ﷻ, but inside they have no true recognition of His authority. Outwardly, they remember Allah ﷻ, but their heart is thinking about what movie to watch tonight. This encompasses both the external and internal dimensions of wrongdoing.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْسِبُونَ ٱلْإِثْمَ - Those who earn sin. Notice the verb used is يَكْسِبُونَ - "they earn" - as opposed to يَفْعَلُونَ - "they do." When you earn something, you work for it thinking you are gaining something good. The word كَسْب implies they believe they are earning something valuable, not realizing it is actually sin. They think they are learning something beneficial, accumulating something worthwhile, but they do not realize it is sin.

سَيُجْزَوْنَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَقْتَرِفُونَ - They will be compensated in accordance with what sins, what crimes they earned for themselves. The verb يَقْتَرِفُونَ emphasizes the active earning and accumulation of these wrongs.

translation: And do not eat of that upon which the name of Allah has not been mentioned, for indeed, it is grave disobedience. And indeed do the devils inspire their allies [among men] to dispute with you. And if you were to obey them, indeed, you would be associators [of others with Him]
commentary:
وَلَا تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا لَمْ يُذْكَرِ ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَفِسْقٌ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ لَيُوحُونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآئِهِمْ لِيُجَـٰدِلُوكُمْ ۖ وَإِنْ أَطَعْتُمُوهُمْ إِنَّكُمْ لَمُشْرِكُونَ

And do not consume from what Allah's ﷻ name has not been pronounced upon. وَإِنَّهُۥ لَفِسْقٌ - It is nothing but corruption in itself. It is inherently corrupt.

وَإِنَّ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ لَيُوحُونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآئِهِمْ - No doubt about it, the devils inspire their close friends, their associates. They give hints and suggestions to those allied with them. لِيُجَـٰدِلُوكُمْ - So that they may argue with you, so they can bring religious arguments against you.

The mushrikeen developed a clever philosophical counter-argument. The Muslims said they would not eat animals that died naturally, without Allah's ﷻ name being pronounced over them - Muslims had to slaughter animals properly themselves. So the mushrikeen responded with this seemingly logical point: "An animal that God Himself kills is haram to you, but when you kill it, it becomes halal? What God does is haram, but what you do is halal? Look how religious you are!"

Allah ﷻ explains that the devils give their allies these clever arguments to make cases against the believers. وَإِنْ أَطَعْتُمُوهُمْ إِنَّكُمْ لَمُشْرِكُونَ - And if you follow them, if you think "that makes sense" and accept their argument, then you will be committing shirk. You will be the ones engaged in shirk.

translation: And is one who was dead and We gave him life and made for him light by which to walk among the people like one who is in darkness, never to emerge therefrom? Thus it has been made pleasing to the disbelievers that which they were doing
commentary:
أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَـٰهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُۥ نُورًا يَمْشِى بِهِۦ فِى ٱلنَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُۥ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

There was an argument implied about the dead animal, but now Allah ﷻ shifts to explain that people who consume such things become spiritually dead. أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَـٰهُ - Isn't it the case that the one who had been dead, then We gave him life? This is spiritual death and spiritual life.

وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُۥ نُورًا يَمْشِى بِهِۦ فِى ٱلنَّاسِ - And We placed for him a light that he walks around with among the people. This is the light of the Quran. A person was spiritually dead, then Allah ﷻ exposed them to the revelation, and now they walk among people carrying that light.

كَمَن مَّثَلُهُۥ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا - Can this person be compared to someone whose example is being in layers of darkness, never coming out of those shades of darkness? There is no comparison.

كَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - That is how it has been beautified for them - whatever they are engaged in. Allah ﷻ mentions darkness, not just the darkness of shirk metaphorically, but they actually love literal darkness. They love going out at night, partying and celebrating in the dark. Most evil happens in the nighttime. This has been beautified for the wrongdoers - whatever they are up to.

translation: And thus We have placed within every city the greatest of its criminals to conspire therein. But they conspire not except against themselves, and they perceive [it] not
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا فِى كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ أَكَـٰبِرَ مُجْرِمِيهَا لِيَمْكُرُوا۟ فِيهَا ۖ وَمَا يَمْكُرُونَ إِلَّا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ

There is an interesting side discussion happening in this surah about the Prophet ﷺ and his challenges, the believers and their challenges, the Prophet's ﷺ attitude towards the believers, and the believers' attitude toward their opposition. The believers were told not to curse their enemies, because the Prophet ﷺ would never do that. The believers were instructed to exercise restraint.

Now we learn about who the enemies are. وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا فِى كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ أَكَـٰبِرَ مُجْرِمِيهَا - That is how We made, in every single town, We installed the greatest of its criminals in positions of power. The word جَعَلْنَا means We gave them power, We installed them, We placed the biggest of their criminals in positions of authority.

Why? When they see the justice of Islam - recall that earlier Allah ﷻ said His word is complete صِدْقًا وَعَدْلًا - truth and justice. Truth is one thing the corrupt dislike; justice is something the corrupt truly cannot stand. When they see this religion progressing and calling for these two things, they inevitably become its enemies. Those who take advantage of injustice will be the first to suffer when justice is established. So the biggest criminals are placed in power.

لِيَمْكُرُوا۟ فِيهَا - So they can make many deceptive plans within it. The word مَكْر is used when you plot against an enemy using any means - deception, trickery - to manipulate them into doing what you want. Interestingly, this word is also attributed to Allah ﷻ: وَمَكَرُوا۟ وَمَكَرَ ٱللَّهُ - "They planned, and Allah planned" (3:54). But when used for Allah ﷻ, it is always in a retaliatory context. They started the deceptive plotting, and Allah ﷻ responds: "You think you can deceive? Let Me show you what that is like. I will make you think you are making progress, and I will lead you right to your own destruction." Similarly with كَيْد - scheming - إِنَّهُمْ يَكِيدُونَ كَيْدًا وَأَكِيدُ كَيْدًا - "They are planning a scheme, and I am planning a scheme" (86:15-16). It is retaliatory when attributed to Allah ﷻ.

وَمَا يَمْكُرُونَ إِلَّا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ - They are not plotting any kind of destruction except for their own selves. Their schemes will backfire on them. وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ - And they have absolutely no realization of this whatsoever.

translation: And when a sign comes to them, they say, "Never will we believe until we are given like that which was given to the messengers of Allah." Allah is most knowing of where He places His message. There will afflict those who committed crimes debasement before Allah and severe punishment for what they used to conspire
commentary:
وَإِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَةٌ قَالُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ حَتَّىٰ نُؤْتَىٰ مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ رُسُلُ ٱللَّهِ ۘ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسَالَتَهُۥ ۗ سَيُصِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ صَغَارٌ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَمْكُرُونَ

وَإِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَةٌ - Whenever a miraculous sign comes to them, whenever a piece of revelation comes to them. قَالُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ حَتَّىٰ نُؤْتَىٰ مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ رُسُلُ ٱللَّهِ - They say, "We will not accept, we will not believe until we are given the likes of what was given to previous messengers of God" - meaning physical miracles, tangible supernatural signs.

ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسَالَتَهُۥ - Allah ﷻ knows best where and how to place His message. He knows where His message should go and who should carry it.

سَيُصِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ صَغَارٌ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ - Those who engaged in crime are going to be afflicted, something is going to target them. What will target them? صَغَار. This word comes from صَغِير, which means small, but صَغَار specifically refers to being made small after being big, being brought low from a position of height. It describes humiliation, particularly when a king is deposed or a leader is overthrown. They used to be powerful, and now they have been brought down completely - now they are like a janitor or a cook, stripped of all authority and status. They used to be great, now they are nothing.

Allah ﷻ is telling them this in the Quran. Remember what already happened in the previous surah - they have already been brought down. The Prophet ﷺ recites to them: سَيُصِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ صَغَارٌ - they are going to experience this humiliation. Even the People of the Book who fought against the Prophet ﷺ will have to face صَغَار. These are open declarations, direct threats.

Consider the context: this is being recited when they hate the Muslims most, when they are at their most powerful. Allah ﷻ declares war against the criminals themselves. This is happening in Makkah, the head of all towns, the most politically powerful and stable of all towns. And they go directly in their faces and say: "You criminals, you are going to be made so small. You are going to feel humiliated." How much more offensive could this be? When people get aggressive with us, we typically become defensive, we try to explain ourselves. The Quran did not take that approach. It called them out directly: "You criminals will be made politically insignificant. You will be made so small."

وَعَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَمْكُرُونَ - And on top of that, an intense punishment because of the plots they continue to make.

translation: So whoever Allah wants to guide - He expands his breast to [contain] Islam; and whoever He wants to misguide - He makes his breast tight and constricted as though he were climbing into the sky. Thus does Allah place defilement upon those who do not believe
commentary:
فَمَن يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُۥ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُۥ لِلْإِسْلَـٰمِ ۖ وَمَن يُرِدْ أَن يُضِلَّهُۥ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُۥ ضَيِّقًا حَرَجًا كَأَنَّمَا يَصَّعَّدُ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّجْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

This is a beautiful and deep reality. فَمَن يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُۥ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُۥ لِلْإِسْلَـٰمِ - Whoever Allah ﷻ intends to guide, He opens his chest, He expands his chest for submission (Islam).

This expression of expanding the chest was used for the Prophet ﷺ himself: أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ - "Did We not expand your chest for you?" (94:1). Now Allah ﷻ says that whoever He wants to guide, anyone He decides deserves guidance - يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُۥ لِلْإِسْلَـٰمِ - their chest will be expanded for Islam.

We make dua: "O Allah, fill our hearts with the light of iman and expand our chests for Islam." Islam literally means to be submissive, to surrender. It also comes from سَلامَة, which means to be at peace and to be safe. Allah ﷻ says يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُۥ لِلْإِسْلَـٰمِ - when someone submits to Allah ﷻ, they will find themselves safe, at peace with that submission. They will understand that this is truly the best thing for them.

Islam represents the practical dimension of faith. What is the spiritual dimension? Iman. This ayah says that in the instructions themselves, in the actual submission to Allah ﷻ, a person will find peace.

How do you know Allah ﷻ intends guidance for someone? When they only find relaxation in acts of worship. Consider the beginning stages for a young person who may not take religion seriously. Then something turns in their heart, and they become devoted to salah. Now they are driving and it is getting late for prayer, and their heart starts becoming disturbed. They do not want any other conversation, they do not want to listen to the news, they do not want to know what else is happening. They just want to get somewhere so they can pray. They feel their chest getting tight, their breath becoming short, because they cannot wait to pray. It becomes their lifeblood. This is a gift from Allah ﷻ - that someone would find such relaxation in worship. Once they pray, it is as though they can finally breathe again. الْحَمْدُ لِلَّٰهِ.

وَمَن يُرِدْ أَن يُضِلَّهُۥ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُۥ ضَيِّقًا حَرَجًا - On the opposite end, whoever Allah ﷻ intends to misguide, We make his chest ضَيِّقًا and حَرَجًا. The word ضَيِّق means to feel constricted because of lack of space, like being in a tight corridor, or playing hide and seek and squeezing yourself into a closet where you can barely move. Mountain climbers sometimes get stuck between rocks - that is the state of ضَيِّق.

حَرَج means you feel uncomfortable because of restrictions placed on you. You cannot move freely. Consider how children feel when told: "Don't go there. Don't touch that. Sit right here. Don't move." That child feels حَرَج - restricted, unable to budge, uncomfortable. It also describes discomfort when you are stuck somewhere, paralyzed and unable to move.

Incidentally, حَرَج is also used in hadith terminology in a way not commonly used elsewhere. حَرَج refers to a battle or massacre among Muslims, when Muslims kill each other - fighting within the community.

Allah ﷻ says whoever He wants to misguide, their chest becomes tight with Islam. They feel like they are running out of space, claustrophobic. They become so uncomfortable, feeling: "These restrictions are making me suffocate. I want to be free. Why am I so tied down?"

كَأَنَّمَا يَصَّعَّدُ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ - It is as though he is climbing up to the sky. When you climb higher and higher up a mountain, it is exhausting. The word يَصَّعَّدُ uses intensified syllables to communicate the sentiment - the difficulty and intensity of the climb. As you go higher up the mountain, what happens? You become exhausted, catching your breath. And what happens to the air? It becomes thinner, making it even harder to breathe. It becomes more and more and more uncomfortable.

On one hand, there is a person who only finds comfort in submission to Allah ﷻ. On the other hand, there is a person who is profoundly uncomfortable with even the thought of religion. You will find people like this, sadly even among Muslims, even within your own family. They hear a little bit about the religion and become so uncomfortable: "Can you talk about something else? I feel nauseous. I cannot be here. There are too many people wearing hijab. I have got to get out. There are too many guys with beards." Sometimes you have to entice them into Islamic programs: "There is free pizza!" You do not tell them there is a lecture. "Free pizza!" They go, and then they see a speaker or other Muslims and think: "I don't want to hear this." Even when the recitation of Quran is playing in the car: "Can we put something else on? Can we turn it off? I don't want to listen to that."

كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّجْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ - That is how Allah ﷻ places الرِّجْس on those who refuse to believe. الرِّجْس refers to things declared filthy by the religion, specifically things Allah ﷻ taught us are impure and contaminated. This is how We pile رِجْس upon those who refuse to believe.

The one thing that would purify them is revelation. The one thing that would cleanse them spiritually, Allah ﷻ takes it away from them as a consequence of their refusal. سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَىٰ.

translation: And this is the path of your Lord, [leading] straight. We have detailed the verses for a people who remember
commentary:
**وَهَـٰذَا صِرَٰطُ رَبِّكَ مُسْتَقِيمًا ۗ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ**

"And this is the path of your Lord, perfectly straight. We have already detailed the signs for people who are mindful."

This ayah concludes a section by emphasizing the clarity and straightness of Allah's ﷻ path. The word مُسْتَقِيمًا appears in the grammatical state of hal, indicating that this path exists in a perpetual state of being straight—it remains straight, unwavering and uncompromising in its direction.

When Allah ﷻ says قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ, "We have already clarified and detailed the signs," He emphasizes that the work of explanation has been completed. The signs are crystal clear for those willing to see them.

The phrase لِقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ is particularly powerful in its grammatical construction. The root ذ-ك-ر means "to remember," but when it appears in the second form (تذكّر), it carries the meaning of making an effort to remember. The subsequent mudari' (present tense) form already contains within it the concept of continuous effort. When this becomes an adjective describing people—يَذَّكَّرُونَ —it minimizes the requirement even further. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: We have clarified the signs for people who would make even the slightest effort to remember, who would put in any work at all on their own behalf. Had they exerted even minimal effort, they would clearly see that this path is straight, with no crookedness in it whatsoever.

translation: For them will be the Home of Peace with their Lord. And He will be their protecting friend because of what they used to do
commentary:
**۞ لَهُمْ دَارُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۖ وَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ**

"For them is the Home of Peace with their Lord, and He is their protecting friend because of what they used to do."

Those who follow this straight path will have دَارُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ —the Home of Peace. This term, which has been adopted for Islamic centers, publishing companies and bookstores, originally refers to Paradise itself: the eternal abode where residents experience perpetual peace and safety.

The beauty of this promise lies in its connection to what came before. Allah ﷻ has already mentioned in earlier passages شَرَحَ اللَّهُ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ —"Allah opened his chest for Islam." Those whose hearts found peace with Islam in this world will receive the Home of Peace in the next. Their hearts were at peace first with the religion, and now they receive the eternal home of peace.

This home exists عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ —in the company of their Master, in His presence. This closeness to Allah ﷻ represents the ultimate honor and blessing.

The ayah concludes with وَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ—"And He is their protecting friend because of what they used to do." The word وَلِيّ encompasses the meanings of protector, guardian, and intimate friend. Allah ﷻ becomes their waliy on account of their deeds.

This phrase carries profound comfort for believers engaged in the struggle of calling to Islam. The Sahaba (RA) worked tirelessly, day and night, often without seeing tangible results. Unlike the Prophet ﷺ who received divine revelation and reassurance, the Companions (RA) were volunteers in this mission. When volunteers don't see results—when the first program they organize has no attendees, when their efforts seem to yield no fruit—the natural human response is to give up.

But the Sahaba (RA) never operated this way. Allah ﷻ reassures them: You may not see the fruits of your labor, you may not perceive any light at the end of the tunnel, but I am your protective friend. I will give you the Home of Peace on account of what you've been doing. Those efforts have produced results—perhaps not visible to you, but visible to Me. This is why He is their waliy.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], the Day when He will gather them together [and say], "O company of jinn, you have [misled] many of mankind." And their allies among mankind will say, "Our Lord, some of us made use of others, and we have [now] reached our term, which you appointed for us." He will say, "The Fire is your residence, wherein you will abide eternally, except for what Allah wills. Indeed, your Lord is Wise and Knowing
commentary:
**وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ قَدِ ٱسْتَكْثَرْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْإِنسِ ۖ وَقَالَ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْإِنسِ رَبَّنَا ٱسْتَمْتَعَ بَعْضُنَا بِبَعْضٍ وَبَلَغْنَآ أَجَلَنَا ٱلَّذِىٓ أَجَّلْتَ لَنَا ۚ قَالَ ٱلنَّارُ مَثْوَىٰكُمْ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ**

"And [mention] the Day when He will gather them all together, [and will say], 'O company of jinn, you have [misled] many of mankind.' And their allies among mankind will say, 'Our Lord, some of us made use of others, and we have [now] reached our term which You appointed for us.' He will say, 'The Fire is your residence, wherein you will abide eternally, except for what Allah wills. Indeed, your Lord is Wise and Knowing.'"

Before examining this ayah in detail, it's important to understand that Judgment Day is not a single event but rather an enormous conference with multiple parallel sessions and sequential phases. Throughout the Quran, we find various descriptions of the Day of Judgment that may seem contradictory on the surface. In some places, Allah ﷻ says people won't be asked about their sins; in others, He says they will be questioned about everything, even the smallest deed—مَا يُغَادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً—"it leaves nothing small nor great."

These aren't contradictions but rather descriptions of different phases and different groups. Like any major event with initial sessions, middle sessions and final sessions, Judgment Day unfolds in stages. In the initial phase, nobody needs to be asked about their sins—everyone will simply know where they stand. When judgment begins, the reality crashes down: "Oh my God, it's really happening. What have I been doing, leaving Allah ﷻ behind, putting Him aside?" You haven't even been handed your record of deeds yet, but the look on your face reveals everything. Just as a teacher can tell from students' expressions who passed and who failed before even announcing grades, on that Day, the spiritual state will be immediately visible.

Then come the more detailed phases where people actually see their deeds recorded and accounted for.

In this ayah, Allah ﷻ initiates a remarkable conversation, turning first to the jinn. This represents one of the rare places in the Quran where Allah ﷻ directly addresses them. He says: يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ قَدِ ٱسْتَكْثَرْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْإِنسِ—"O company of jinn, you have acquired a huge number of human beings." The verb اسْتَكْثَرْتُم means to make something abundant, to accumulate in large quantities. The jinn successfully deceived, whispered to, and recruited countless humans, making them into allies (awliya) and leading them to the Fire.

But then their human counterparts, their partners in corruption, speak up: رَبَّنَا ٱسْتَمْتَعَ بَعْضُنَا بِبَعْضٍ—"Our Lord, we made use of each other." They're actually taking pride in their crime, refusing to let the jinn take all the credit. "No, no, it wasn't just them—we enjoyed each other's company, we utilized each other, we worked together in this corruption."

وَبَلَغْنَآ أَجَلَنَا ٱلَّذِىٓ أَجَّلْتَ لَنَا—"And we have reached our appointed term which You set for us." The deadline that Allah ﷻ had established eventually came.

Allah's ﷻ response is devastating in its brevity: قَالَ ٱلنَّارُ مَثْوَىٰكُمْ—"He said, 'The Fire is your dwelling.'" The word مَثْوَى is significant. In Arabic, mathwa refers to a place where generations of a family have lived, a place with deep ancestral roots. You look back and say, "My father's father's father's father lived in this house." When the Prophet ﷺ knew intricate details about Madyan (Midian), it was said to him: "You weren't living there for generations; you didn't have a house there where your ancestry resided. How do you know this history?" The answer was revelation.

When this word is used for Hellfire, it conveys a chilling reality: you will be there so long that you'll look back and feel as though you have ancient history in that place. Except this ancient history isn't about your ancestors—it's about you yourself. مَثْوَىٰكُمْ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ—"Your dwelling, wherein you will remain permanently."

إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ—"Except for what Allah wills." This phrase reminds us that nothing happens outside Allah's ﷻ will and knowledge, even the duration of punishment.

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ—"Indeed, your Lord is Wise and Knowing." He is Hakeem (All-Wise) in His decisions and judgments, and 'Aleem (All-Knowing) about every action and intention.

translation: And thus will We make some of the wrongdoers allies of others for what they used to earn
commentary:
**وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُوَلِّى بَعْضَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَعْضًۢا بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ**

"And thus will We make some of the wrongdoers allies of others for what they used to earn."

This ayah establishes a divine principle: Allah ﷻ causes wrongdoers to befriend one another, to become guardians and protectors (awliya) of each other. The verb نُوَلِّى comes from the same root as وَلِيّ, meaning to make someone a guardian or ally of another.

بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ—"Because of what they used to earn." This alliance between wrongdoers is not arbitrary; it's based on what they continuously earned through their actions. Their choices, their persistence in wrong, their mutual exploitation—all of this naturally draws them together in this world and will keep them together in the Hereafter.
