translation: O company of jinn and mankind, did there not come to you messengers from among you, relating to you My verses and warning you of the meeting of this Day of yours?" They will say, "We bear witness against ourselves"; and the worldly life had deluded them, and they will bear witness against themselves that they were disbelievers
commentary:
**يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى وَيُنذِرُونَكُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا ۚ قَالُوا۟ شَهِدْنَا عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِنَا ۖ وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَشَهِدُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰفِرِينَ**

"O company of jinn and mankind, did there not come to you messengers from among you, relating to you My verses and warning you of the meeting of this Day of yours?" They will say, "We bear witness against ourselves"; and the worldly life deluded them, and they will bear witness against themselves that they were disbelievers."

Allah ﷻ addresses both groups together: يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ—"O gatherings of jinn and human beings." The rhetorical question follows: أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ—"Didn't messengers come to you from among you?"

This phrase مِّنكُمْ ("from you") has sparked discussion among scholars. Does it mean that jinn also had their own messengers, separate from human prophets? The majority position among the mufassirun (Quranic commentators) is that jinn did not have separate prophets. The more important question, however, is: why does it matter? This is not information that pertains to our guidance. Allah ﷻ will not question us on Judgment Day about whether we definitively determined if jinn had their own messengers.

Yet some people take these tangential issues and make them their life's mission: "What does مِّنكُمْ really mean? Is there alien life mentioned in the Quran?" They'll have conversation after conversation about these peripheral matters while neglecting the central message. These are distractions from guidance.

The phrase مِّنكُمْ can also simply mean "in your midst." The jinn came and heard the Messenger ﷺ speak; messengers were present among both communities, whether directly or through their message reaching both.

يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى—"Relating to you My signs, My miraculous verses." The verb يَقُصُّونَ means to narrate, to tell the story. The messengers would continuously narrate Allah's ﷻ signs to people.

وَيُنذِرُونَكُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا—"And they would continually warn you of the meeting of this Day of yours." Notice the demonstrative pronoun هَـٰذَا ("this") and the possessive يَوْمِكُمْ ("your day"). This is an Ishara Qariba, a near demonstrative, emphasizing immediacy and personal connection. This is YOUR day, the day YOU had to meet, now present before you.

قَالُوا۟ شَهِدْنَا عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِنَا—"They will say, 'We testify against ourselves.'" The response is immediate and devastating. شَهِدَ لَهُ means to testify in favor of someone; شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِ means to testify against someone. Here, they testify against their own selves.

وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا—"And worldly life deceived them." The verb غَرَّ means to deceive, to delude. ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا —"worldly life"—is one of the most repeated concepts in the Quran. Allah ﷻ addresses different ways this life deceives different groups: disbelievers and atheists who don't believe in an afterlife, hypocrites, people who mix true understanding of religion with fabricated ideas without basis. Each group experiences deception from worldly life in its own way.

For those who لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْآخِرَةِ —"don't believe in the Hereafter"—all they had was this short, inferior life, and it completely deceived them about reality.

وَشَهِدُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰفِرِينَ—"And they will testify against themselves that they were disbelievers." The verb كَانُوا emphasizes a continuous past state: they had been, they were persistently ungrateful and rejecting of truth.

translation: That is because your Lord would not destroy the cities for wrongdoing while their people were unaware
commentary:
**ذَٰلِكَ أَن لَّمْ يَكُن رَّبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ بِظُلْمٍ وَأَهْلُهَا غَـٰفِلُونَ**

"That is because your Lord would never destroy the cities unjustly while their people were unaware."

This ayah explains Allah's ﷻ perfect justice. ذَٰلِكَ (dhalika—"that") refers back to why messengers were sent. Allah ﷻ would never destroy any town or nation wrongfully—مَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ بِظُلْمٍ.
وَأَهْلُهَا غَـٰفِلُونَ—"While its citizens were unaware." The word غَـٰفِلُونَ means heedless, unaware, not paying attention. This doesn't mean Allah ﷻ gives them three days' warning and then destroys them. Rather, guidance came to them, prophets were sent to them, they were clearly made aware of what truth is. Only after they denied the messenger, after they had full knowledge and still rejected it, did destruction come upon them.

This principle is emphasized elsewhere in the Quran: وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا  —"And We would never punish until We send a messenger."

The foolishness of the Quraysh was that when the Messenger ﷺ came to them, they would mockingly say, "Let's see the punishment then! We don't see anything impressive yet!" They didn't understand that the punishment comes precisely when you reject the messenger. They were essentially asking for their own destruction to be expedited.

translation: And for all are degrees from what they have done. And your Lord is not unaware of what they do
commentary:
**وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَـٰتٌ مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا۟ ۚ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ**

"And for all are degrees from what they have done. And your Lord is not unaware of what they do."

وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَـٰتٌ مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا۟—"And for everyone there are ranks based on what they did." This is a profound statement about divine justice. On one hand, it applies to disbelievers—not all disbelievers are the same in their rejection. There are levels and degrees even in disbelief, even in Hellfire. Allah ﷻ doesn't simply say, "Disbeliever, disbeliever, disbeliever—Hell, Hell, Hell, that's it."

On the other hand, and perhaps more importantly for believers, not all believers stand at the same level. Allah ﷻ knows precisely what each person does, where each person stands. We don't all have the same rank with Him.

This concept was already introduced earlier in the surah: وَلَا تَتَمَنَّوْا مَا فَضَّلَ اللَّهُ بِهِ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ—"Don't wish for what Allah has given to some of you over others." Allah ﷻ didn't make us all the same. Some people have been given certain abilities, others have been given different abilities, different opportunities, different circumstances.

But Allah ﷻ doesn't rank us based on height, skin color, educational opportunity, or economic standing. He doesn't rank us by any of those external factors. The only criterion that determines rank in His sight is qualitative: How did you work with what you were given? This is a qualitative test, not a quantitative one.

وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ—"And your Lord is not at all unaware of what they do." The construction مَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ uses double negation for emphasis: Your Master is not—not at all—unaware regarding what they're up to. He sees everything, knows everything, and will judge everything with perfect justice.

translation: And your Lord is the Free of need, the possessor of mercy. If He wills, he can do away with you and give succession after you to whomever He wills, just as He produced you from the descendants of another people
commentary:
**وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ذُو ٱلرَّحْمَةِ ۚ إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفْ مِنۢ بَعْدِكُم مَّا يَشَآءُ كَمَآ أَنشَأَكُم مِّن ذُرِّيَّةِ قَوْمٍ ءَاخَرِينَ**

"And your Lord is the Free of Need, the possessor of mercy. If He wills, He can do away with you and give succession after you to whomever He wills, just as He produced you from the descendants of another people."

وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ—"Your Lord is Al-Ghani, absolutely free of need." He doesn't need anyone or anything. He doesn't need people to come to Islam; He's not desperate for them to accept guidance. And precisely because He doesn't need them, He would have every right to annihilate them immediately when they act obnoxiously toward Him and His message. He doesn't need to keep them around.

ذُو ٱلرَّحْمَةِ—"Yet He is also the Possessor of incredible mercy." These two attributes go hand in hand. In our worldly relationships, when someone shows you abundant mercy and lets things slide, it's usually because they love you deeply and can't imagine being without you. Parents show mercy to their children partly because they need their children emotionally; they want to be with them. Children sometimes manipulate this, taking advantage of elderly parents, knowing the parents will continue showing mercy because they need that relationship.

But Allah ﷻ is different. He doesn't need you, so He has no obligation to show you mercy. When you have something you don't need—a pet that starts biting and attacking you—you have the right to get rid of it immediately. Allah ﷻ doesn't need human beings, yet He still shows mercy. This combination of being completely free of need while choosing to show mercy is uniquely divine.

إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ—"If He wanted, He could do away with you." He could be done with you entirely, remove you from existence.

وَيَسْتَخْلِفْ مِنۢ بَعْدِكُم مَّا يَشَآءُ—"And He would bring in your place, follow you up with whatever He wills." Whatever nation He wants, whatever ethnicity He wants, He could create to replace you.

كَمَآ أَنشَأَكُم مِّن ذُرِّيَّةِ قَوْمٍ ءَاخَرِينَ—"Just as He brought you up from the descendants of another people." This is not an abstract threat. History shows this pattern. The nation of 'Ad was destroyed by Allah ﷻ. The few believers who survived became the foundation for the nation of Thamud. From some of the offspring of Thamud, the Arabs emerged. You came from the descendants of other nations, so Allah ﷻ can easily bring another nation from yours.

This is a stark warning for the Muslim Ummah. If we don't take ourselves seriously as Muslims—in our personal character, in what we actually do—Allah ﷻ can replace us with an entirely different Muslim community.

There's a famous statement that applies here: Every time there's a problem, we Muslims point to external factors. "The government is doing this, the politicians are doing that, the media is attacking us." But what are YOU doing? Where is YOUR salah? Where is YOUR zakat? What are you doing with your family? Have you even written a will with proper Islamic inheritance shares? Have you paid your wife's mahr? Is your income completely halal? Do you pay your zakat properly every year? What have you done to teach your children? Where do you stand personally?

We're ready to complain about big systemic things we can't change, but we won't take personal responsibility. That's a cop-out. And Allah ﷻ addresses this attitude multiple times in the Quran. If you don't take yourself seriously, He doesn't need you. فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا يَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَكُونُوا أَمْثَالَكُم —"If you turn away, He will replace you with a people other than you, then they will not be like you." That's devastating enough, but then He adds: they won't even be like you—meaning they won't even look like you, won't talk like you, won't be disappointments like you. That's painful to hear, but it's the reality we must face.

translation: Indeed, what you are promised is coming, and you will not cause failure [to Allah]
commentary:
**إِنَّ مَا تُوعَدُونَ لَـَٔاتٍ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ**

"Indeed, what you are promised is coming, and you will not cause failure."

إِنَّ مَا تُوعَدُونَ لَـَٔاتٍ—"No doubt, whatever you have been promised is definitely coming." The pronoun is indefinite—whatever has been promised, all of it, will come to pass. Whether it's the Day of Judgment, resurrection, accountability, Paradise, or Hell—all of it is coming without question.

وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ—"And you will not be able to overpower, to overcome, to cause failure." The verb أَعْجَزَ means to make someone helpless or to overpower them. The grammatical construction here is significant. The مَا and بِـ combination creates a strong negation. You will not—in any way, under any circumstances—be able to overpower this message, this mission, this Prophet ﷺ, or this call. Nothing can stop what Allah ﷻ has decreed.

translation: Say, "O my people, work according to your position; [for] indeed, I am working. And you are going to know who will have succession in the home. Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed
commentary:
**قُلْ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنِّى عَامِلٌ ۖ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَن تَكُونُ لَهُۥ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلدَّارِ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ**

"Say, 'O my people, work according to your position; indeed, I am working. And you are going to know who will have succession in the home. Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed.'"

قُلْ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ—"Say: O my nation, go ahead and work, put yourselves to work according to your position." The word مَكَانَة refers to position, standing, or stance. "Work from the position you've taken against me. Go ahead, exhaust yourselves in that position. Do whatever you want with me. Take your best shot."

This command comes at the height of Quraysh aggression against the Prophet ﷺ. After three years of living in a cave during the boycott, after watching his Companions (RA) tortured, after seeing them publicly humiliated, after attempts on his own life, Allah ﷻ tells him to go out and declare: "You've already done everything under the sun to stop me. You got anything else? Go ahead, work on it. Do everything you can."

إِنِّى عَامِلٌ—"I'm working too." But what is his work? Just da'wah. He has no strategy except what Allah ﷻ has commanded him: keep preaching the message. "You do whatever you want to do. Go ahead."

The Prophet ﷺ doesn't say this on his own initiative. He has been commanded to say it. Imagine the courage required: After spending the whole night in qiyam (night prayer), after Allah ﷻ gave him in Surah Al-Muzzammil the heavy word—إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا —he must leave his house, face the enemy, and declare: "Bring your worst. I'm working too."

فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَن تَكُونُ لَهُۥ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلدَّارِ—"Soon you will find out who will really own the eventual home, the final dwelling, the ultimate outcome." The word عَـٰقِبَة means the final result, the ultimate consequence. ٱلدَّار means the home, the abode. Who will have the final victory? Who will possess the ultimate home?

إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ—"No doubt about it—wrongdoers are not going to prosper. They will not succeed." The verb يُفْلِحُ means to succeed, to prosper, to achieve ultimate success. No matter how much they seem to succeed temporarily, wrongdoers will not achieve true, lasting success.

translation: And the polytheists assign to Allah from that which He created of crops and livestock a share and say, "This is for Allah," by their claim, "and this is for our partners [associated with Him]." But what is for their "partners" does not reach Allah, while what is for Allah - this reaches their "partners." Evil is that which they rule
commentary:
**وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ مِمَّا ذَرَأَ مِنَ ٱلْحَرْثِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ نَصِيبًا فَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَهَـٰذَا لِشُرَكَآئِنَا ۖ فَمَا كَانَ لِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ فَلَا يَصِلُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ فَهُوَ يَصِلُ إِلَىٰ شُرَكَآئِهِمْ ۗ سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ**

"And they assigned to Allah from that which He created of crops and livestock a share and said, 'This is for Allah,' by their claim, 'and this is for our 'partners' [associated with Him].' But what is for their 'partners' does not reach Allah, while what is for Allah—this reaches their 'partners.' Evil is that which they rule."

This ayah begins a significant shift in the surah. The methodology (manhaj) of Prophet Ibrahim (AS) was to expose the stupidity of shirk (polytheism) to the mushrikun (polytheists). But his method—physically destroying idols—was not the method given to our Prophet ﷺ. Our Messenger ﷺ was already told: وَلَا تَسُبُّوا الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ—"Don't curse those they worship besides Allah."

So if the approach isn't to physically destroy their idols or curse their gods, what is it? Let's have a conversation about what they believe to be sacred. Let's ask some pointed questions about their worship practices. A significant portion of the surah from this point will examine and expose the irrationality of their beliefs through questioning.

وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ مِمَّا ذَرَأَ مِنَ ٱلْحَرْثِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ نَصِيبًا—"They made for Allah, from what comes out from crops and from cattle, a portion." The verb ذَرَأَ means to create or bring forth. From the produce of their fields—crops, grains, vegetables—and from their livestock—milk, meat, wool, hides—they designated portions.

فَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ—"They said, with full confidence in their own claim, 'This portion belongs to Allah.'" The word زَعْم indicates a claim or assertion made with confidence but without real knowledge or authority.

وَهَـٰذَا لِشُرَكَآئِنَا—"And this portion is for our partners"—meaning their many gods, the ones they believed would intercede for them on Judgment Day, the ones they thought took care of their daily needs. This reveals a common feature in polytheistic religions: they maintain a concept of one supreme God alongside multiple lesser deities. In Hinduism, there's a supreme God with many subordinate gods. Even in certain branches of Christianity, God the Father is the main God, with Jesus and the Holy Spirit as divine but subordinate. The Quraysh operated similarly—Allah ﷻ was the supreme deity, but they needed intermediary gods for practical matters.

فَمَا كَانَ لِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ فَلَا يَصِلُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ—"Whatever was dedicated to their false partners would never reach Allah." Here's where their practice became truly absurd. They would make this division: "This is for Allah, this is for the idols." But then they would reason: "Allah is unseen and doesn't need anything, so let's just give this portion 'for Allah' back to the idols too." So they would place it in front of the idols, and obviously they themselves would eventually use it. The portion allegedly for Allah ﷻ never actually went to anything done in Allah's ﷻ name.

وَمَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ فَهُوَ يَصِلُ إِلَىٰ شُرَكَآئِهِمْ—"But what was supposedly for Allah—that would reach their partners." The "Allah portion" ended up with the idols, while the idol portion stayed with the idols. Everything went to the false gods; nothing was genuinely dedicated to the One True God.

سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ—"What a horrible judgment they make! How terrible are their decisions!" The verb سَآءَ expresses how evil or terrible something is. Their decision-making, their reasoning, their entire system of worship was fundamentally flawed and offensive to basic logic, let alone to the Creator who provided everything they had.

This ayah exposes not just the theological error of shirk but its practical absurdity and the deep disrespect it shows toward Allah ﷻ, who is Al-Ghani (Free of Need) yet deserving of all gratitude and worship.

translation: And likewise, to many of the polytheists their partners have made [to seem] pleasing the killing of their children in order to bring about their destruction and to cover them with confusion in their religion. And if Allah had willed, they would not have done so. So leave them and that which they invent
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ زَيَّنَ لِكَثِيرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ قَتْلَ أَوْلَـٰدِهِمْ شُرَكَآؤُهُمْ لِيُرْدُوهُمْ وَلِيَلْبِسُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ دِينَهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا فَعَلُوهُ ۖ فَذَرْهُمْ وَمَا يَفْتَرُونَ

This ayah exposes one of the most heinous practices that emerged from corrupted religious traditions: the killing of children in the name of false gods. Allah ﷻ says that just as He has shown us these distortions, He also allowed the beautification of this horrific act for huge multitudes from among the polytheists. The word زَيَّنَ (beautified) reveals a critical reality about how evil becomes normalized. When something as fundamentally wrong as child sacrifice becomes "beautified," it demonstrates the profound spiritual corruption that had taken hold.

The phrase شُرَكَآؤُهُمْ (their associates) refers to their false deities and the religious authorities who served them. These partners in shirk made this ugliest of crimes appear acceptable, even sacred. This heinous practice, sadly, was not unique to pre-Islamic Arabia but has been found in various polytheistic cultures throughout history.

Allah ﷻ then explains the dual purpose behind this corruption: لِيُرْدُوهُمْ (to destroy them) and وَلِيَلْبِسُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ دِينَهُمْ (to confuse over them their religion). The first purpose is straightforward destruction - the literal killing of their offspring. The second is more insidious: creating layers of confusion and mystery in their religious practices. By establishing elaborate, baseless rituals and prohibitions, these false religious authorities created a system where people could no longer distinguish truth from falsehood.

The ayah concludes with a powerful reminder of divine will: وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا فَعَلُوهُ (if Allah had willed, they would not have done it). This acknowledges Allah's ultimate sovereignty while not excusing their crimes. The command فَذَرْهُمْ وَمَا يَفْتَرُونَ (leave them and what they fabricate) instructs the Prophet ﷺ to disengage from endless debate with those who have closed their hearts to truth.

It's essential to understand the context of this direct confrontation. This is not dawah in the sense of gentle invitation - that stage had already passed with the Quraysh. The Makkan period contains both phases: early gentle invitation and later direct confrontation after enmity became established. The Quran is now calling out their false beliefs with unflinching clarity. This is the "gloves off" conversation that becomes necessary when good character and patient reasoning have been exhausted. We must be careful not to apply this confrontational approach indiscriminately in our own contexts, especially with people we're genuinely trying to invite to Islam.

translation: And they say, "These animals and crops are forbidden; no one may eat from them except whom we will," by their claim. And there are those [camels] whose backs are forbidden [by them] and those upon which the name of Allah is not mentioned - [all of this] an invention of untruth about Him. He will punish them for what they were inventing
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَنْعَـٰمٌ وَحَرْثٌ حِجْرٌ لَّا يَطْعَمُهَآ إِلَّا مَن نَّشَآءُ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَأَنْعَـٰمٌ حُرِّمَتْ ظُهُورُهَا وَأَنْعَـٰمٌ لَّا يَذْكُرُونَ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَا ٱفْتِرَآءً عَلَيْهِ ۚ سَيَجْزِيهِم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ details the absurd dietary and ritual restrictions the mushrikeen had invented. They would declare: هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَنْعَـٰمٌ وَحَرْثٌ حِجْرٌ (these cattle and crops are hijr - forbidden, untouchable). The word حِجْرٌ literally means something solid like rock, suggesting something absolutely forbidden to approach. They claimed nobody could eat these except those they designated as sacred, adding بِزَعْمِهِمْ (according to their claims) - a clear divine commentary on the baseless nature of these restrictions.

The ayah then lists three categories of fabricated prohibitions. First, وَأَنْعَـٰمٌ حُرِّمَتْ ظُهُورُهَا (cattle whose backs are forbidden) - they declared certain animals so sacred that riding them was prohibited. These animals were supposedly dedicated to particular deities. Second, وَأَنْعَـٰمٌ لَّا يَذْكُرُونَ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَا (cattle over which they do not mention Allah's name) - when slaughtering these animals, they were forbidden from pronouncing Allah's name because the animals were dedicated to other gods.

Allah ﷻ explicitly labels all of this: ٱفْتِرَآءً عَلَيْهِ (fabrication against Him). This was all schemed and plotted as lies against Allah ﷻ. The ayah concludes with the warning: سَيَجْزِيهِم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (He will compensate them for what they used to fabricate). Justice will come for those who attribute false laws to Allah ﷻ.

These prohibitions may seem bizarre to us, but they reveal a deeper truth about religious corruption. When religious authority becomes divorced from divine revelation, it inevitably becomes a tool for social, political, and economic control. The elite establish elaborate rules not for spiritual benefit but to maintain their power over the masses. People follow these traditions without questioning, generation after generation, because "that's what everyone does."

translation: And they say, "What is in the bellies of these animals is exclusively for our males and forbidden to our females. But if it is [born] dead, then all of them have shares therein." He will punish them for their description. Indeed, He is Wise and Knowing
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ مَا فِى بُطُونِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ خَالِصَةٌ لِّذُكُورِنَا وَمُحَرَّمٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِنَا ۖ وَإِن يَكُن مَّيْتَةً فَهُمْ فِيهِ شُرَكَآءُ ۚ سَيَجْزِيهِمْ وَصْفَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ

This ayah exposes another layer of their invented restrictions, this time concerning unborn animals. They declared: مَا فِى بُطُونِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ خَالِصَةٌ لِّذُكُورِنَا (whatever is in the bellies of these cattle is exclusively for our males). When they would slaughter a pregnant animal, they declared the unborn offspring permissible only for men to consume, forbidden for women.

But their inconsistency is immediately exposed: وَإِن يَكُن مَّيْتَةً فَهُمْ فِيهِ شُرَكَآءُ (but if it is stillborn, then they are partners in it). If the animal died naturally and had a fetus inside, suddenly both men and women could eat it. There was no principle behind these rules, no divine wisdom - just arbitrary declarations that happened to serve the interests of those in power.

The response from Allah ﷻ is direct: سَيَجْزِيهِمْ وَصْفَهُمْ (He will compensate them for their descriptions). He will hold them accountable for these attributions they made, these false characteristics they assigned to His creation and His law. The ayah concludes with إِنَّهُۥ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ (certainly He is All-Wise, All-Knowing) - a stark contrast to their ignorant fabrications. While they invent rules without wisdom or knowledge, Allah ﷻ possesses perfect wisdom and complete knowledge.

translation: Those will have lost who killed their children in foolishness without knowledge and prohibited what Allah had provided for them, inventing untruth about Allah. They have gone astray and were not [rightly] guided
commentary:
قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَتَلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَهُمْ سَفَهًۢا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَحَرَّمُوا۟ مَا رَزَقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱفْتِرَآءً عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ

This ayah delivers a devastating verdict on those who committed these crimes. قَدْ خَسِرَ (they have certainly lost) - the transaction of their lives has resulted in complete loss. The ayah identifies two major crimes that led to this loss.

First, ٱلَّذِينَ قَتَلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَهُمْ سَفَهًۢا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ (those who killed their children out of stupidity, without any knowledge). The word سَفَهًۢا means foolishness or stupidity in its most profound sense - not just a mistake in judgment but a fundamental corruption of natural human instinct. The phrase بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ (without knowledge) emphasizes that they had no legitimate basis, no divine instruction, nothing but blind following of invented traditions.

Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) used to weep when reciting the Quran, particularly overwhelmed by passages like this. During the days of jahiliyyah, he had buried his own daughter alive, a crime that haunted him even after embracing Islam. This ayah would have cut him deeply - the recognition of acting سَفَهًۢا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ, in stupidity without knowledge.

The second crime: وَحَرَّمُوا۟ مَا رَزَقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱفْتِرَآءً عَلَى ٱللَّهِ (they forbade what Allah had provided them, fabricating lies against Allah). They took the pure sustenance Allah ﷻ had made permissible and declared it forbidden, then attributed these prohibitions to Him.

The conclusion is unambiguous: قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ (they were lost and they were not committed to guidance). The past tense ضَلُّوا (they were lost) combined with the negative وَمَا كَانُوا۟ (they were not) shows this wasn't a temporary deviation - they were in a state of misguidance with no commitment to finding the truth.

translation: And He it is who causes gardens to grow, [both] trellised and untrellised, and palm trees and crops of different [kinds of] food and olives and pomegranates, similar and dissimilar. Eat of [each of] its fruit when it yields and give its due [zakah] on the day of its harvest. And be not excessive. Indeed, He does not like those who commit excess
commentary:
۞ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَ جَنَّـٰتٍ مَّعْرُوشَـٰتٍ وَغَيْرَ مَعْرُوشَـٰتٍ وَٱلنَّخْلَ وَٱلزَّرْعَ مُخْتَلِفًا أُكُلُهُۥ وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلرُّمَّانَ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا وَغَيْرَ مُتَشَـٰبِهٍ ۚ كُلُوا۟ مِن ثَمَرِهِۦٓ إِذَآ أَثْمَرَ وَءَاتُوا۟ حَقَّهُۥ يَوْمَ حَصَادِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تُسْرِفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ

After exposing their false prohibitions, Allah ﷻ now presents the truth about His provisions. وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَ جَنَّـٰتٍ مَّعْرُوشَـٰتٍ (He is the One who brought forth gardens that are ma'rooshat). The word مَّعْرُوشَـٰتٍ comes from arsh, which means both a roof and a support structure. In agricultural terms, it refers to plants that need trellises - delicate plants that must be tied to stakes or supports to remain standing. These are gardens requiring careful, attentive cultivation.

وَغَيْرَ مَعْرُوشَـٰتٍ (and those not ma'rooshat) - plants that rise and thrive on their own, needing no external support. This contrast encompasses the full range of Allah's agricultural provisions, from the most delicate to the most robust.

وَٱلنَّخْلَ وَٱلزَّرْعَ مُخْتَلِفًا أُكُلُهُۥ (and date palms and crops, different in the way they are consumed). The phrase مُخْتَلِفًا أُكُلُهُۥ is grammatically notable - the adjective مُخْتَلِفًا precedes the noun أُكُلُهُۥ it describes, and takes its grammatical case from الزَّرْعَ (crops) rather than from أُكُلُهُۥ (consumption). This is a rare grammatical construction in the Quran worth noting for students of Arabic.

The diversity described here is remarkable - not just different plants, but different ways of eating them. Some are peeled, some are chewed whole, some are made into soup, some are crushed, some are ground into flour for bread. The variety in Allah's creation reflects His generosity and wisdom.

وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلرُّمَّانَ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا وَغَيْرَ مُتَشَـٰبِهٍ (and olives and pomegranates, similar and dissimilar). Even within the same species, there's variation - different types of olives, different varieties of pomegranates, each with unique characteristics yet sharing common features.

The command that follows is significant: كُلُوا۟ مِن ثَمَرِهِۦٓ إِذَآ أَثْمَرَ (eat from its fruit when it bears fruit). This is permission - consume what Allah ﷻ has provided. In contrast to the mushrikeen's invented prohibitions, Allah's law is characterized by permission and facilitation.

But with this permission comes responsibility: وَءَاتُوا۟ حَقَّهُۥ يَوْمَ حَصَادِهِۦ (and give its right on the day of its harvest). Even in the Makkan period, Allah ﷻ was hinting at the concept of zakat. When you harvest, when you gather the fruits of your labor, remember that Allah ﷻ has a right in that provision. Give from what He has given you.

The timing is specific - يَوْمَ حَصَادِهِۦ (the day of harvest). This is particularly relevant for agricultural communities where income doesn't come in regular installments but arrives all at once during harvest season. When a farmer receives his yearly income in one lump sum, there's a natural human tendency toward extravagance. Hence the immediate follow-up: وَلَا تُسْرِفُوٓا۟ (and do not be extravagant).

The ayah concludes with a profound principle: إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (certainly He does not love those who are extravagant). The word مُسْرِفِينَ refers to those who exceed bounds in spending. Islam establishes a middle path between miserliness and wastefulness, between hoarding and squandering. This balance is part of Allah's wisdom in legislation.

translation: And of the grazing livestock are carriers [of burdens] and those [too] small. Eat of what Allah has provided for you and do not follow the footsteps of Satan. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ حَمُولَةً وَفَرْشًا ۚ كُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ

After discussing plant-based provisions, Allah ﷻ turns to livestock: وَمِنَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ حَمُولَةً وَفَرْشًا (and from the cattle, hamoolah and farsh). The word حَمُولَةً comes from haml, meaning to carry or bear weight. These are animals you can ride upon or load with goods - camels, horses, donkeys, cows, and bulls. They serve as transportation and beasts of burden.

وَفَرْشًا literally means something that hugs the ground or spreads out low. These are the smaller livestock - sheep, goats, rabbits - animals from which you derive benefit but cannot realistically use for transportation. The imagery is vivid - while some animals stand tall enough to ride, others stay close to the earth.

The command mirrors the previous ayah: كُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ (eat from what Allah has provided you). Again, the emphasis is on divine permission and provision. These animals are not sacred in themselves; they are gifts from Allah ﷻ to be used according to His guidance.

The warning that follows is critical: وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ (and do not follow the footsteps of Shaytan). This same phrase appeared in Surah Al-Baqarah, also in the context of permissible food. There's a deep connection between consumption and Shaytan's influence. His very first success in leading humanity astray involved getting someone to eat something forbidden. When we consume what Allah ﷻ has prohibited, or prohibit what He has permitted, we're continuing Shaytan's original strategy.

إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (certainly he is to you a clear enemy). The word مُّبِينٌ means openly clear, obvious. There should be no confusion about Shaytan's enmity toward humanity. His career began with deception around food, and he continues to use the same tactics - making the haram appear attractive and the halal appear restrictive.

translation: [They are] eight mates - of the sheep, two and of the goats, two. Say, "Is it the two males He has forbidden or the two females or that which the wombs of the two females contain? Inform me with knowledge, if you should be truthful
commentary:
ثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَزْوَٰجٍ ۖ مِّنَ ٱلضَّأْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمَعْزِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۗ قُلْ ءَآلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ أَمَّا ٱشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحَامُ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۖ نَبِّـُٔونِى بِعِلْمٍ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

Now Allah ﷻ directly challenges their invented dietary laws by examining the very animals they made claims about. ثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَزْوَٰجٍ (eight pairs) - the livestock can be categorized into eight types based on species and gender.

مِّنَ ٱلضَّأْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ (from the sheep, two) - male and female sheep. وَمِنَ ٱلْمَعْزِ ٱثْنَيْنِ (and from the goats, two) - male and female goats. These are the smaller livestock, what we referred to earlier as farsh.

Then comes the devastating logical challenge: قُلْ (Say) - this is Allah ﷻ instructing the Prophet ﷺ on how to confront their baseless claims. ءَآلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ (has He forbidden the two males or the two females?) This is a simple question demanding a simple answer. If you claim certain animals are haram, specify: is it the males that are forbidden or the females?

أَمَّا ٱشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحَامُ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ (or what the wombs of the two females contain?) - or perhaps it's the offspring that are forbidden? The question forces them to articulate the basis of their prohibition. They can't, because there is no basis.

نَبِّـُٔونِى بِعِلْمٍ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (inform me with knowledge if you are truthful). The word نَبِّـُٔونِى is a strong form meaning "inform me explicitly, clearly." The addition of بِعِلْمٍ (with knowledge) is the crushing blow - not just any answer, but one based on actual knowledge, on evidence, on divine revelation. If you're making these claims about what's halal and haram, where's your evidence? Where's your scholarship? Where's your divine text?

The phrase إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (if you are truthful) implies they are not truthful. They cannot provide knowledge because they're fabricating. This is Allah ﷻ calling out the baseless nature of their religious inventions with surgical precision.

translation: And of the camels, two and of the cattle, two. Say, "Is it the two males He has forbidden or the two females or that which the wombs of the two females contain? Or were you witnesses when Allah charged you with this? Then who is more unjust than one who invents a lie about Allah to mislead the people by [something] other than knowledge? Indeed, Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلْإِبِلِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَمِنَ ٱلْبَقَرِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۗ قُلْ ءَآلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ أَمَّا ٱشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحَامُ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۖ أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَآءَ إِذْ وَصَّىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا ۚ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا لِّيُضِلَّ ٱلنَّاسَ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

The challenge continues with the larger livestock: وَمِنَ ٱلْإِبِلِ ٱثْنَيْنِ (and from the camels, two - male and female). وَمِنَ ٱلْبَقَرِ ٱثْنَيْنِ (and from the cattle, two - bull and cow). These complete the eight pairs.

The same logical challenge is repeated: قُلْ ءَآلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ أَمَّا ٱشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحَامُ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ (Say: has He forbidden the two males, or the two females, or what the wombs of the two females contain?). The repetition emphasizes that this challenge applies to all their invented prohibitions, not just some.

But then an additional challenge is added: أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَآءَ إِذْ وَصَّىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا (or were you witnesses when Allah ordained this for you?). This is profound. Even if you can't articulate the logic, even if you can't provide the knowledge-based evidence, perhaps you'll claim direct divine instruction. Fine - were you present? Did Allah ﷻ Himself counsel you about these prohibitions? Were you witnesses to this revelation?

Of course they were not. They have neither logical basis, nor scholarly evidence, nor direct divine instruction. Their entire system is fabricated.

فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا (then who is more wrong than one who invents lies against Allah?) The word أَظْلَمُ is the superlative form - the most wrong, the greatest wrongdoer. There is no worse crime than attributing to Allah ﷻ what He never said, making laws in His name that He never authorized.

The purpose of this fabrication is exposed: لِّيُضِلَّ ٱلنَّاسَ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ (so that he may mislead people without knowledge). This reveals the true nature of religious corruption. These invented laws serve a purpose - they're tools of control and manipulation. When religious authorities make up elaborate rules and prohibitions, they gain power over people. This control translates into social, political, and economic benefits.

The phrase بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ (without knowledge) is critical and multifaceted. First, the one inventing these lies has no knowledge - no legitimate basis for their claims. Second, the people being misled have no knowledge - they're following blindly. When a society becomes disinterested in real knowledge, when people stop asking "why?" and just follow tradition because "that's what everyone does," they become vulnerable to this manipulation.

The distinction between knowledge that makes you a better human being and knowledge that merely makes you a more sophisticated consumer is crucial here. You can master technology, engineering, medicine - and these are important skills - but if you lack the knowledge that gives you purpose and meaning in life, you remain vulnerable to manipulation. Real education develops you as a complete human being, not just as skilled labor.

The ayah concludes with a sobering principle: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (certainly Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people). When a nation collectively engages in this level of injustice - fabricating laws in Allah's name and using religion to mislead people - Allah ﷻ withdraws His guidance from them. This is the natural consequence of persistent wrongdoing.

translation: Say, "I do not find within that which was revealed to me [anything] forbidden to one who would eat it unless it be a dead animal or blood spilled out or the flesh of swine - for indeed, it is impure - or it be [that slaughtered in] disobedience, dedicated to other than Allah. But whoever is forced [by necessity], neither desiring [it] nor transgressing [its limit], then indeed, your Lord is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
قُل لَّآ أَجِدُ فِى مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ مُحَرَّمًا عَلَىٰ طَاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُۥٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَكُونَ مَيْتَةً أَوْ دَمًا مَّسْفُوحًا أَوْ لَحْمَ خِنزِيرٍ فَإِنَّهُۥ رِجْسٌ أَوْ فِسْقًا أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

After exposing their fabrications, Allah ﷻ now presents the truth through His Messenger ﷺ. قُل (Say) - the Prophet ﷺ is instructed to announce clearly: لَّآ أَجِدُ فِى مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ مُحَرَّمًا عَلَىٰ طَاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُۥٓ (I do not find in what has been revealed to me anything forbidden for a consumer to consume). This is a statement of simplicity and clarity that contrasts starkly with their elaborate prohibitions.

Then come the exceptions - and they are few: إِلَّآ أَن يَكُونَ مَيْتَةً (except that it be carrion - dead meat not properly slaughtered). أَوْ دَمًا مَّسْفُوحًا (or blood that is poured forth). The word مَّسْفُوحًا means spilled or flowing blood, not the trace amounts that remain in properly slaughtered meat.

أَوْ لَحْمَ خِنزِيرٍ (or the flesh of swine). This is stated categorically. فَإِنَّهُۥ رِجْسٌ (for it is an abomination). The word رِجْسٌ means impure, filthy, abominable. This impurity is both physical and spiritual. By standards of religion and spiritual wellness, swine is considered corrupt.

أَوْ فِسْقًا أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ (or fisq - corruption - that which has been dedicated to other than Allah). The word فِسْقًا means corruption or deviation from what is right. What makes an animal corrupt for consumption? أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ (it has been slaughtered with a name other than Allah pronounced over it) - when it's dedicated to false gods or slaughtered without acknowledging Allah ﷻ as the source of all provision.

These are the only prohibitions in the revelation given to the Prophet ﷺ. Everything else is permitted. This stands in stark contrast to the mushrikeen's endless list of invented restrictions.

But even these prohibitions have an exception for genuine necessity: فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ (but whoever is compelled by necessity). The word ٱضْطُرَّ indicates genuine compulsion - you're dying of hunger in the desert and there's nothing else available.

However, this permission comes with conditions: غَيْرَ بَاغٍ (without being in a state of rebellion). The word بَاغٍ means someone transgressing or rebelling. This refers to someone who, after eating enough to survive, continues eating out of curiosity or desire. You eat one bite and survive - that's permitted. You continue eating because "actually, it tastes interesting" - that becomes baghiy, transgression.

وَلَا عَادٍ (nor being aggressive). Even in necessity, you should take only what you need with a sense of distaste for what you're forced to do. Some people might deliberately put themselves in situations where they can claim necessity, or they might eat forbidden food as a act of defiance. This is عَادٍ (transgression born of hostility).

فَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (then certainly your Lord is Forgiving, Merciful). Even in this extreme situation, even when consuming what is normally prohibited, Allah's mercy encompasses you. His forgiveness covers the genuine necessity. This same principle appeared in Surah Al-Baqarah, establishing a consistent principle: Allah's law considers human necessity while maintaining clear boundaries.

translation: And to those who are Jews We prohibited every animal of uncloven hoof; and of the cattle and the sheep We prohibited to them their fat, except what adheres to their backs or the entrails or what is joined with bone. [By] that We repaid them for their injustice. And indeed, We are truthful
commentary:
وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ حَرَّمْنَا كُلَّ ذِى ظُفُرٍ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْبَقَرِ وَٱلْغَنَمِ حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ شُحُومَهُمَآ إِلَّا مَا حَمَلَتْ ظُهُورُهُمَآ أَوِ ٱلْحَوَايَآ أَوْ مَا ٱخْتَلَطَ بِعَظْمٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ جَزَيْنَـٰهُم بِبَغْيِهِمْ ۖ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ

This ayah explains the contrast between the simple dietary laws given to Muslims and the more complex restrictions imposed on the Jews. وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ (and upon those who are Jews) - literally, "those who became Jewish" or "those who practice Judaism."

حَرَّمْنَا كُلَّ ذِى ظُفُرٍ (We made forbidden to them everything with an undivided hoof). The word ظُفُرٍ (thufur) refers to animals that have a single claw or hoof rather than a split hoof. This includes camels and other animals that Muslims can consume but were forbidden to the Jewish people.

The restrictions become more detailed: وَمِنَ ٱلْبَقَرِ وَٱلْغَنَمِ حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ شُحُومَهُمَآ (and from cattle and sheep, We made forbidden to them their fats). Not the entire animal, but specifically the fat. Then come the exceptions to this fat prohibition: إِلَّا مَا حَمَلَتْ ظُهُورُهُمَآ (except what their backs carry) - referring to the meat on the back. أَوِ ٱلْحَوَايَآ (or the intestines). أَوْ مَا ٱخْتَلَطَ بِعَظْمٍ (or what is mixed with bone) - the flesh attached to bones they could eat, but not the separated fat.

These extremely specific, technical details about what parts of which animals could be consumed created a complex system of dietary law. For Muslims, the instruction is simple: slaughter properly, drain the blood, done. For the Jewish people, it became: here's the meat on the back you can eat, here's what's around the bones you can eat, don't touch the fat from these specific areas.

The reason for this complexity is then stated explicitly: ذَٰلِكَ جَزَيْنَـٰهُم بِبَغْيِهِمْ (that is how We recompensed them for their rebellion). The word بِبَغْيِهِمْ (their transgression) refers to their continuous violations of Allah's commands. When they violated the simpler laws, Allah ﷻ made the laws more complex. This might seem counterintuitive - why make things harder for people already struggling with easier rules? But it's actually a form of consequence: if you want trouble, you'll get trouble. You couldn't handle five simple rules? Here are fifty detailed rules. Now you have fifty ways to violate instead of five. It's like a student who refuses to do homework being assigned five times more homework - not to help them succeed, but as a consequence of their defiance.

These details were known only to Jewish rabbis and scholars, held within their scholarly traditions. Yet here is Allah ﷻ revealing these specifics to an unlettered Prophet ﷺ who had no access to rabbinical texts or training. This itself serves as a proof of the Quran's divine origin.

The ayah concludes with emphasis: وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ (and certainly We are truthful). This phrase appears because these technical details of Jewish dietary law could be verified. The Jewish scholars in Madinah knew these laws. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: go ahead and verify this with the rabbis. Ask them if this is true. They will confirm it, proving that this revelation comes from the same divine source as their own scripture, and that the one receiving it truly is a Prophet of Allah ﷻ.

translation: So if they deny you, [O Muhammad], say, "Your Lord is the possessor of vast mercy; but His punishment cannot be repelled from the people who are criminals
commentary:
فَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُل رَّبُّكُمْ ذُو رَحْمَةٍ وَٰسِعَةٍ وَلَا يُرَدُّ بَأْسُهُۥ عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

The ayah addresses the Prophet ﷺ with a powerful instruction: if they continue to lie against you and reject the truth, remind them of a fundamental reality about their Master. Tell them رَبُّكُمْ ذُو رَحْمَةٍ وَٰسِعَةٍ – your Master is the possessor of expansive, incredible mercy. The very fact that they have not been destroyed despite their persistent denial stands as evidence of Allah's ﷻ vast mercy. He hasn't unleashed His punishment yet, even though they continue in their opposition.

But the ayah immediately balances this reminder of mercy with a sobering warning: وَلَا يُرَدُّ بَأْسُهُۥ عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ – His punishment against the criminal nation will not be turned away. The word بَأْس refers to the war that Allah ﷻ declares. Notice how the construction emphasizes that this ba's, this war, belongs to Allah ﷻ Himself. When He declares war, when those floodgates open and that final announcement is made, there will be no canceling it, no turning it back. While He is extremely merciful right now in this moment of respite, once that decree of punishment comes, it cannot be reversed.

translation: Those who associated with Allah will say, "If Allah had willed, we would not have associated [anything] and neither would our fathers, nor would we have prohibited anything." Likewise did those before deny until they tasted Our punishment. Say, "Do you have any knowledge that you can produce for us? You follow not except assumption, and you are not but falsifying
commentary:
سَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَآ أَشْرَكْنَا وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَلَا حَرَّمْنَا مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ ذَاقُوا۟ بَأْسَنَا ۗ قُلْ هَلْ عِندَكُم مِّنْ عِلْمٍ فَتُخْرِجُوهُ لَنَآ ۖ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَإِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا تَخْرُصُونَ

Allah ﷻ now exposes a common argument used by the disbelievers: سَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَآ أَشْرَكْنَا – those who commit shirk will soon say, "If Allah ﷻ had wanted, we wouldn't have committed shirk." They continue: وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُنَا – and our parents wouldn't have done shirk either. وَلَا حَرَّمْنَا مِن شَىْءٍ – and we wouldn't have made anything haram.

This is their attempt to use divine will as an excuse for their actions. They point to their historical traditions and cultural practices, asking how such deeply rooted beliefs could exist if Allah ﷻ didn't will them. But the Quran immediately exposes this as nothing new: كَذَٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ – this is exactly how those who came long before them also lied against the truth. حَتَّىٰ ذَاقُوا۟ بَأْسَنَا – until they tasted Our punishment.

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to challenge them directly: قُلْ هَلْ عِندَكُم مِّنْ عِلْمٍ فَتُخْرِجُوهُ لَنَآ – tell them, "Do you have any actual knowledge? Then bring it out for us. Let's see it." This is a powerful rhetorical challenge. They claim their practices have divine sanction, so where is the evidence? Where is the revealed knowledge?

The reality of their position is exposed in the final statement: إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ – you are following nothing except assumption and speculation. وَإِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا تَخْرُصُونَ – and you are doing nothing but making wild guesses. The word خَرْص refers to speculation, like someone looking at a palm tree and randomly estimating how many dates it will produce. They're just guessing, making things up without any basis in revealed knowledge.

translation: Say, "With Allah is the far-reaching argument. If He had willed, He would have guided you all
commentary:
قُلْ فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحُجَّةُ ٱلْبَـٰلِغَةُ ۖ فَلَوْ شَآءَ لَهَدَىٰكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

Here Allah ﷻ declares a fundamental truth: قُلْ فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحُجَّةُ ٱلْبَـٰلِغَةُ – tell them, to Allah ﷻ alone belongs the final, conclusive argument. The word حُجَّة refers to an argument that cannot be refuted, evidence that is undeniable. But notice the qualifier: ٱلْبَـٰلِغَةُ – the one that reaches, that penetrates deeply, the far-reaching argument that goes straight to the heart of the opponent.

This is the argument that even the opponent recognizes deep down inside as true. It's checkmate. Game over. There's no comeback from it. This concept inspired the title of Imam Ghazali's (RA) famous work, Hujjat al-Islam. What we learn from this ayah is something profound about Quranic discourse: if you truly want to make a compelling case about something that will reach people's hearts, what should your argument stem from? From the Quran itself. He alone owns the convincing argument that penetrates hearts.

The ayah concludes: فَلَوْ شَآءَ لَهَدَىٰكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ – and had He wanted, He would have guided all of you together. This dismantles their earlier excuse. They claimed that if Allah ﷻ wanted, they wouldn't have done shirk. Now Allah ﷻ turns it around: if He wanted to force guidance upon you, He could have. But that's not how this test works. You have been given choice, evidence and accountability.

This ayah also teaches us something vital about Islamic unity. While we have many sciences in Islam – Hadith, Aqeedah, Akhlaq, and their countless branches – the Quran as discourse, as reminder, as conversation naturally binds Muslims together. When the Quran as a living conversation disappears from our lives, we take these other sciences and use them as excuses to become different from one another, to drift apart. But the power of the Quran is incomparable. The institution that reminds us of this collective discourse is salat al-jama'ah, the congregational prayer. When salat happens in congregation, a person's school of thought, their understanding of Hadith or Aqeedah – all of these fade into the background. You travel somewhere, hear the adhan, enter a masjid and pray. You don't know the beliefs or backgrounds of those standing beside you, and none of it matters. What unifies you is the salah itself, and centrally, the Quran being recited. For the Companions (RA), every salah was a khutbah, every prayer was a reminder because they understood what was being recited – the hujjah balighah, the convincing case that penetrates deep inside the heart.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "Bring forward your witnesses who will testify that Allah has prohibited this." And if they testify, do not testify with them. And do not follow the desires of those who deny Our verses and those who do not believe in the Hereafter, while they equate [others] with their Lord
commentary:
قُلْ هَلُمَّ شُهَدَآءَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَرَّمَ هَـٰذَا ۖ فَإِن شَهِدُوا۟ فَلَا تَشْهَدْ مَعَهُمْ ۚ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَهُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ

The command continues: قُلْ هَلُمَّ شُهَدَآءَكُمُ – tell them, "Bring forth your witnesses!" The word هَلُمَّ means to call someone, "Come here," but in a casual, dismissive way. Bring your experts, your witnesses who are ready to testify ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَرَّمَ هَـٰذَا – those who bear witness that Allah ﷻ made this haram.

This is a direct challenge to their baseless claims about what Allah ﷻ has prohibited. They've been making up rules, attributing them to divine authority. So now: show your evidence, bring your witnesses.

The instruction that follows is striking: فَإِن شَهِدُوا۟ فَلَا تَشْهَدْ مَعَهُمْ – then if they do bring these false witnesses and testify, don't you bear witness alongside them. Don't validate their falsehood with your presence or silence. This is followed by a broader warning: وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا – and don't follow the empty desires of those who lied against Our miraculous signs.

The ayah describes these people further: وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ - those who don't believe in the afterlife. This returns us to a theme from the beginning of the surah. We're reaching the conclusion now, and a review happens. The ayah ends: وَهُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ – and they are the ones who equate others with their Master. The subject of shirk, of false equivalence with Allah ﷻ, bookends the surah's message.

What becomes clear here is that when somebody other than Allah ﷻ is willing to testify about what is halal and what is haram without divine authority, they are putting themselves in the place where Allah ﷻ alone belongs. They are making themselves equivalent to Him in legislative authority.

## Ayahs 151-152

۞ قُلْ تَعَالَوْا۟ أَتْلُ مَا حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۖ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَكُم مِّنْ إِمْلَـٰقٍ ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُكُمْ وَإِيَّاهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ ۖ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ

وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ مَالَ ٱلْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ ۖ وَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۖ وَإِذَا قُلْتُمْ فَٱعْدِلُوا۟ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ ۖ وَبِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْفُوا۟ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ

After the false witnesses were challenged with هَلُمَّ – a casual "come here" – now the command shifts dramatically. The Prophet ﷺ is told: قُلْ تَعَالَوْا۟  – tell them, "Come on up!" The word تَعَالَوْا۟ comes from علو, meaning height. When someone standing above a mountain calls to those below, they use this word: come up here. The Messenger ﷺ, when he calls, calls people to something higher, something elevated.

A beautiful comparison is being made. When they called their false witnesses, it was just هَلُمَّ – "Hey, come here, testify for me already." But when the Prophet ﷺ calls, it's تَعَالَوْا۟ – "Come up to something higher." The imagery is powerful: someone on top of a mountain can see what people at the bottom cannot see. The Prophet ﷺ has a view they don't have, access to knowledge they lack. He's in a position to tell them what their Master has revealed.

أَتْلُ مَا حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ – I will recite to you what your Master has made haram upon you. Now comes the divine list of prohibitions, beginning with the essentials.

**First:** أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا – that you don't associate any partners with Him whatsoever. This is the foundation, the first and most critical command.

**Second:** وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا – and that you show excellence, the best possible treatment, toward your parents. Notice the structure: don't do shirk with Allah ﷻ, your Creator, and then immediately, honor those whom Allah ﷻ used predominantly in your creative process – your parents.

**Third:** وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَكُم مِّنْ إِمْلَـٰقٍ – don't kill your children out of poverty. On one hand, the ayah addresses the rights of parents; on the other, the rights of children. You must be excellent parents yourself, and understand that Allah ﷻ honored you with children. You cannot kill them. The preposition مِن – "because of" – indicates that poverty already exists. When we say "because of poverty," it implies the poverty is already present, like saying "I'm eating because I'm hungry" means the hunger exists now. 

So Allah ﷻ says: نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُكُمْ وَإِيَّاهُمْ – We will provide for you and for them. Notice that "you" comes first because you're the one currently experiencing poverty. The assurance addresses your immediate concern: We'll take care of you, and We'll take care of your children too.

**Fourth:** وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ – don't even go near all forms of shamelessness, what is obvious of it and what is hidden. The verb used is لَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ – don't approach, don't go near. This is stronger than simply "don't do." It means maintain distance from it entirely. This comprehensive prohibition will be elaborated in Surah Al-A'raf, with detailed discussion particularly drawn from the story of Adam (AS).

The phrase مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ covers everything: the obvious forms and the hidden forms, the direct and the indirect, the clearly wrong and the questionable gray areas. Don't go near shamelessness that you can't argue about, and don't go near the kind where people make excuses – "Oh, it's just PG-13. There's only one bad scene. It's not that bad. Don't get so worked up about it. I'll look away." 

Some even cite religious texts to justify their habits: "The first glance doesn't hurt you, right? I'm ready to cite that hadith for my filthy habits." But Allah ﷻ says in another place: يَعْلَمُ خَائِنَةَ ٱلْأَعْيُنِ – He knows the stealing of the eyes. The word بَطَنَ – what's hidden or covered – can also manifest through language. Language can inspire imagery, imagery can inspire thought. Filthy language is also fahishah. Filthy gestures, filthy implications, filthy jokes – these are all included. People today have become so accustomed to inappropriate humor that they don't even recognize it as shamelessness anymore. That's just becoming desensitized to fahishah. Even most modern comedy revolves around shamelessness. Get rid of all of it, the obvious and the subtle.

**Fifth:** وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ – don't kill a soul that Allah ﷻ Himself has made sacred, except with just cause. There are cases where life can be taken – through the justice system, for example – but only with proper justification according to divine law.

The first set of commandments concludes: ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ– That is what He counsels you with, so that hopefully you may use your intellect, so that you may understand and apply reason.

**Sixth:** وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ مَالَ ٱلْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ – don't come near the wealth of the orphan except in the way that is best. Again, notice لَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ – don't even approach it, except with the intention that is genuinely best, most beneficial for the orphan. حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ  – until he reaches maturity and can manage his own affairs.

**Seventh:** وَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ – fulfill the measure and the scale with justice. الْكَيْل refers to measurement by volume or length – like measuring cloth. الْمِيزَان refers to weight. Together, these cover all forms of measurement and trade. Today, this extends to contracts, deliverables, technical specifications, punch clocks – any measure by which we fulfill obligations to others. The key is بِٱلْقِسْطِ – with justice, avoiding any unfairness.

Then comes a statement of divine mercy: لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا – We don't burden any soul at all except with its capacity. Allah ﷻ doesn't ask the impossible. He asks what you're capable of within your means.

**Eighth:** وَإِذَا قُلْتُمْ فَٱعْدِلُوا۟ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ – when you speak, when you testify or make a statement, be fair and just, even if it concerns close relatives. This is a review of what was presented in ayah 90, but here in summarized form. Don't let family ties compromise your testimony or sense of justice.

**Ninth:** وَبِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْفُوا۟ – and to the promise of Allah ﷻ, you must fulfill it. Especially the covenant with Allah ﷻ – guard it carefully and fulfill it completely.

This second set concludes: ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ – That is what He counsels you with, so that you may remember, so that you may take heed and be mindful.

translation: Say, "Come, I will recite what your Lord has prohibited to you. [He commands] that you not associate anything with Him, and to parents, good treatment, and do not kill your children out of poverty; We will provide for you and them. And do not approach immoralities - what is apparent of them and what is concealed. And do not kill the soul which Allah has forbidden [to be killed] except by [legal] right. This has He instructed you that you may use reason

And do not approach the orphan's property except in a way that is best until he reaches maturity. And give full measure and weight in justice. We do not charge any soul except [with that within] its capacity. And when you testify, be just, even if [it concerns] a near relative. And the covenant of Allah fulfill. This has He instructed you that you may remember
And, [moreover], this is My path, which is straight, so follow it; and do not follow [other] ways, for you will be separated from His way. This has He instructed you that you may become righteous
commentary:
وَأَنَّ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطِى مُسْتَقِيمًا فَٱتَّبِعُوهُ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلسُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ

After laying out the commandments, Allah ﷻ declares: وَأَنَّ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطِى مُسْتَقِيمًا – and that this is My path, standing perfectly straight. Notice the pronoun: صِرَٰطِى – MY path. This is the path that belongs to Allah ﷻ, not invented by people, not subject to cultural variation or personal interpretation.

The command follows: فَٱتَّبِعُوهُ – so follow it. Stay on this one path.

Then comes the warning: وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلسُّبُلَ – and don't follow the pathways, the multiple diverging paths. Notice the contrast: one صِرَاط, one straight path of Allah ﷻ, versus many سُبُل, many different paths that people invent. The reason for the prohibition is clear: فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ – so that they don't scatter you away from His path, so that you don't become dispersed and separated from the one true way.

Previous ayahs mentioned صِرَاط, and now here it's mentioned again with emphasis: صِرَٰطِى مُسْتَقِيمًا – My path that stands straight. Then the terminology shifts to سَبِيل when referring to His path being lost. This linguistic variation adds emphasis and draws attention to the different contexts.

The ayah concludes with the third and final counsel statement: ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ – That is what He counsels you with so that you may attain taqwa, so that you may become people of God-consciousness and protection from His displeasure.

Notice the progression in these three concluding phrases:
- First set: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ – so you may use intellect
- Second set: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ – so you may remember
- Third set: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ – so you may attain taqwa

These commandments engage the mind, the memory and ultimately the heart in consciousness of Allah ﷻ.

translation: Then We gave Moses the Scripture, making complete [Our favor] upon the one who did good and as a detailed explanation of all things and as guidance and mercy that perhaps in [the matter of] the meeting with their Lord they would believe
commentary:
ثُمَّ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ تَمَامًا عَلَى ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْسَنَ وَتَفْصِيلًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لَّعَلَّهُم بِلِقَآءِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ

A transition occurs with ثُمَّ – then. This doesn't mean "then" in a chronological sequence, but rather "then consider this additional case." Allah ﷻ says: ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ – We gave Musa (AS) the Book. This serves as historical precedent and evidence that this straight path is not new.

The description of what was given to Musa (AS) is comprehensive: تَمَامًا عَلَى ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْسَنَ – complete, perfect, sufficient for the one who wanted to excel. In other words, if somebody wanted to reach the level of ihsan, of excellence in their religion, the Book given to Musa (AS) was enough for them. They had everything they needed.

وَتَفْصِيلًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ – and it contained detailed explanation of all things they needed to know. وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً – and it was sent as guidance and as a mercy.

Then comes the purpose: لَّعَلَّهُم بِلِقَآءِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ – so that they would hopefully believe in the meeting with their Master. Of all the beliefs that could have been highlighted about why the Torah was revealed, Allah ﷻ emphasizes this one: belief in the meeting with Him, belief in the afterlife and accountability.

This is particularly significant because one of the first conversations Allah ﷻ had with Musa (AS) included mention of the Hour. When Musa (AS) went up the mountain and dialogue began, among those early exchanges was: إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ ءَاتِيَةٌ – the Hour is certainly coming (20:15). Even while Musa (AS) was in the sacred valley, holding his staff, being told about his mission – right there, he was reminded of the Day of Judgment.

And what aspect of belief did the vast majority of Jews later abandon? They did away with belief in the afterlife. So Allah ﷻ highlights this – the very thing they were given the Book to believe in, they eventually rejected.

translation: And this [Qur'an] is a Book We have revealed [which is] blessed, so follow it and fear Allah that you may receive mercy
commentary:
وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مُبَارَكٌ فَٱتَّبِعُوهُ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ

After mentioning the Book given to Musa (AS), Allah ﷻ now speaks of the Quran: وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مُبَارَكٌ – and this is a Book We have sent down, full of barakah, full of blessing. The word مُبَارَك indicates something that contains the power of increase, growth and goodness that multiplies.

The command is direct: فَٱتَّبِعُوهُ – so follow it. This connects back to what was said before: This is My path, so follow it. The Messenger ﷺ was highlighted as embodying that path, and now the Book is highlighted as the guide to that path.

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ – and maintain consciousness of Allah ﷻ, protect yourselves from His displeasure, so that all of you may be shown mercy.

What we see here is the institution of two things: the Messenger ﷺ and the Message. First Allah ﷻ said "This is My path" – highlighting the messenger's path. Now "This is a Book full of blessing" – highlighting the message itself. These two work together. The messenger embodies the path, and the Book illuminates it.

translation: [We revealed it] lest you say, "The Scripture was only sent down to two groups before us, but we were of their study unaware
commentary:
أَن تَقُولُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ عَلَىٰ طَآئِفَتَيْنِ مِن قَبْلِنَا وَإِن كُنَّا عَن دِرَاسَتِهِمْ لَغَـٰفِلِينَ

This ayah explains one of the purposes for sending the Quran: أَن تَقُولُوٓا۟ – lest you say, so that you wouldn't have the excuse to say إِنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ عَلَىٰ طَآئِفَتَيْنِ مِن قَبْلِنَا – "The Book was only given to two groups that came before us."

What two groups? The Jews and the Christians. The Arabs could have claimed that revelation was something exclusive to other nations, that they had no access to divine guidance in their own language. They might have said: وَإِن كُنَّا عَن دِرَاسَتِهِمْ لَغَـٰفِلِينَ – and we were completely unaware of their studies and teachings, completely heedless of what they had. "How could we be expected to follow something we never had access to, something not in our language, not part of our heritage?"

Allah ﷻ removes this excuse by sending the Quran in clear Arabic to the Arabs themselves, and through them to all of humanity.

translation: Or lest you say, "If only the Scripture had been revealed to us, we would have been better guided than they." So there has [now] come to you a clear evidence from your Lord and a guidance and mercy. Then who is more unjust than one who denies the verses of Allah and turns away from them? We will recompense those who turn away from Our verses with the worst of punishment for their having turned away
commentary:
أَوْ تَقُولُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَكُنَّآ أَهْدَىٰ مِنْهُمْ ۚ فَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ ۚ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَصَدَفَ عَنْهَا ۗ سَنَجْزِى ٱلَّذِينَ يَصْدِفُونَ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْدِفُونَ

Another potential excuse is addressed: أَوْ تَقُولُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَكُنَّآ أَهْدَىٰ مِنْهُمْ – or lest you say, "If only the Book had been sent down to us, we surely would have been better guided than them." This is the arrogance of hypothetical righteousness: "If we had been given what they were given, we would have done so much better. We wouldn't have corrupted it. We would have followed it properly."

Allah ﷻ cuts off this excuse: فَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ – so now a clear proof, a manifest revelation has come to you from your Master. وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ – and guidance and mercy. You have it now. You have no excuse left.

Then comes the rhetorical question: فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ – so who could be a worse wrongdoer, who could be more unjust than someone who lied against the miraculous signs of Allah ﷻ? وَصَدَفَ عَنْهَا – and turned sharply away from them?

The word صَدَفَ is used when something makes a sharp, immediate turn. It's used to describe the sharp angle of a camel's knee because of how abrupt the bend is. The image is of someone heading directly toward the truth, then suddenly veering away at a sharp angle, deliberately turning their back on it.

The warning concludes: سَنَجْزِى ٱلَّذِينَ يَصْدِفُونَ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ – soon We will compensate those who turn sharply away from Our miraculous signs with the worst kind of punishment. بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْدِفُونَ – on account of the turning away that they did, because of their deliberate rejection.

translation: Do they [then] wait for anything except that the angels should come to them or your Lord should come or that there come some of the signs of your Lord? The Day that some of the signs of your Lord will come no soul will benefit from its faith as long as it had not believed before or had earned through its faith some good. Say, "Wait. Indeed, we [also] are waiting
commentary:
هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ رَبُّكَ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ ۗ يَوْمَ يَأْتِى بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ لَا يَنفَعُ نَفْسًا إِيمَـٰنُهَا لَمْ تَكُنْ ءَامَنَتْ مِن قَبْلُ أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِىٓ إِيمَـٰنِهَا خَيْرًا ۗ قُلِ ٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ

The surah concludes with a powerful question: هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ – are they waiting for anything except that the angels should come to them? أَوْ يَأْتِىَ رَبُّكَ – or that your Master should come? أَوْ يَأْتِىَ بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ – or that some of the signs of your Master should arrive?

These are the unmistakable signs of the Day of Judgment. They're waiting for something so obvious, so undeniable that they'll have no choice but to believe. But that's not how this test works.

يَوْمَ يَأْتِى بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ – the Day when some of the signs of your Master arrive – referring particularly to the rising of the sun from the west, one of the major signs before the Day of Judgment. لَا يَنفَعُ نَفْسًا إِيمَـٰنُهَا – no soul will benefit from its faith لَمْ تَكُنْ ءَامَنَتْ مِن قَبْلُ – that didn't used to believe from before.

When that miraculous sign appears, people will rush to declare faith. "I believe now! I'm a believer!" But that faith will be worthless. The time for the test will have ended. أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِىٓ إِيمَـٰنِهَا خَيْرًا – or that didn't earn any good through its faith. Even those who had faith but did nothing with it, who earned no good deeds through that faith, will find it doesn't benefit them.

The surah ends with a stark command: قُلِ ٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ (qulintadhiru inna muntadhirun) – tell them, "Go ahead and wait. We are waiting too."

They said they're waiting to see miraculous proof before they believe. And now, as we reach the conclusion of this surah, Allah ﷻ turns it back on them: Go ahead then, wait. Wait for that day when faith will no longer benefit you. We're waiting too – waiting to see how things turn out for you, waiting for the inevitable outcome of your choice to reject the clear guidance that came to you.

This is an open declaration, a final warning issued to those who persist in denial. The messenger has conveyed the truth, the arguments have been made, the path has been clarified. Now there's nothing left but to wait and see who was truly on guidance and who will face the consequences of turning away.

translation: Indeed, those who have divided their religion and become sects - you, [O Muhammad], are not [associated] with them in anything. Their affair is only [left] to Allah; then He will inform them about what they used to do
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ شِيَعًا لَّسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِى شَىْءٍ ۚ إِنَّمَآ أَمْرُهُمْ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ

Allah ﷻ addresses a critical issue that has plagued religious communities throughout history: the division and fragmentation of religion itself. The ayah begins with إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ (those who cut apart their religion). The verb فَرَّقُوا carries the meaning of breaking something into pieces, dividing what was meant to remain whole.

The phrase فَرَّقُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ (they caused division in their religion) deserves careful reflection. What does it mean to cut apart the religion? Allah ﷻ created certain things to remain united, to stay together as complementary pairs. The mufsidūn (corrupting people) are those who break apart what Allah ﷻ intended to keep joined. Within our faith, there are fundamental elements that must coexist: belief in Allah ﷻ cannot be separated from belief in the Hereafter. Hope cannot exist without fear, and fear without hope leads to despair. We cannot claim belief in our own responsibility while denying the decree of Allah ﷻ—both truths stand together.

The Quran and the Sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ form an inseparable whole. The proof of the Messenger's authenticity lies both in the miracle of the Quran and in his noble character. This life and the next life are two halves of a complete picture. This world is not an evil place to escape from; rather, it is the arena where we prepare for eternity. These few years we spend on earth, though short, carry immense weight because everything that follows is determined by how we live them.

Other essential pairings include knowledge and action. Our minds should develop and mature in understanding the wisdom of this religion, while simultaneously our hearts should remain soft and receptive. Tragically, there are people who acquire extensive religious knowledge yet their hearts grow harder over time. They may be extremely knowledgeable but haven't experienced tears in prayer for years—a deeply concerning spiritual state. The two elements that should develop together have been torn apart.
